original_option: (1) Scale (asset size); (2) Long-term investment horizon; (3) Regulatory frameworks; (4) Governance framework; (5) Principal-agent issues.
new_option: (1) Scale (asset size); (2) Long-term investment horizon.

original_option: diseconomies of scale — may be unable to invest in niche investments like venture capital (VC) where small allocations may not generate sufficient returns to justify due diligence costs; some strategies are capacity constrained; large trading orders incur market impact costs.
new_option: diseconomies of scale — may be unable to invest in niche investments like venture capital (VC) where small allocations may not generate sufficient returns to justify due diligence costs.

original_option: are around 5% or less — with net payouts typically modest as percent of AUM; but exceptions exist: frozen defined benefit plans might be in a de-risking mode that increases liquidity needs.
new_option: are around 5% or less — with net payouts typically modest as percent of AUM.

original_option: tend to be much more asset/liability focused while operating within tight regulations designed to ensure adequacy of capital — vs pensions/endowments/SWFs which are more asset-focused with liability considerations less central.
new_option: tend to be much more asset/liability focused while operating within tight regulations designed to ensure adequacy of capital.

original_option: 1970s domestic fixed income → 1980s 60% equity/40% FI → 1990s international equity markets → 21st century increased alternative assets (PE, HF, RE, other illiquid); pensions have moved to LDI; SWFs innovated with active management; endowments adopted Endowment Model.

one person (the agent) makes decisions on behalf of another (the principal) and their interests are not aligned — internal agents include investment committee members and staff; external agents include third-party asset managers, broker/dealers, consultants, and board members.
new_option: one person (the agent) makes decisions on behalf of another (the principal) and their interests are not aligned — internal agents include investment committee members and staff.

original_option: high base fees due regardless of fund performance give little incentive for the fund manager to produce superior performance — such arrangements are common among hedge funds and have led to greater demand for fee transparency and alignment of interest between hedge fund managers and their clients.
new_option: high base fees due regardless of fund performance give little incentive for the fund manager to produce superior performance.

original_option: mission, investment objectives, and guidelines — plus asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting implementation, and reporting requirements; helps minimize principal-agent challenges.
new_option: mission, investment objectives, and guidelines — plus asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting implementation, and reporting requirements.

original_option: DB pension funds: surplus volatility (SD of asset returns in excess of liability returns); SWFs: probability of losses or of not maintaining purchasing power over a time period; endowments/foundations: volatility of total returns; banks/insurers: VaR or CVaR and scenario-based stress tests.
new_option: DB pension funds: surplus volatility (SD of asset returns in excess of liability returns).

original_option: almost exclusive reliance on public equities and fixed income (traditional 60/40), largely passively managed, tight tracking error limits, very little to no alternatives; advantages: low cost/fees, transparent, low manager selection risk; disadvantage: limited value-added.
new_option: almost exclusive reliance on public equities and fixed income (traditional 60/40), largely passively managed, tight tracking error limits, very little to no alternatives.

original_option: significant exposure to alternative investments (private markets, hedge funds), significant active management, and externally managed assets; pros: high value-added potential; cons: expensive and difficult to implement for most SWFs due to their large asset sizes.
new_option: significant exposure to alternative investments (private markets, hedge funds), significant active management, and externally managed assets.

original_option: relies more on internally managed assets (insourcing); features reference portfolio (passive mix of public equities/FI/cash defining risk-equivalent benchmark), total portfolio approach (ensuring planned risk exposures at total portfolio level), and active management including tilting asset allocation through to stock selection.
new_option: relies more on internally managed assets (insourcing).

original_option: focuses on hedging liabilities and interest rate risk via duration-matched fixed-income exposure; often separates into hedging portfolio (long duration FI, possibly IR swaps to extend duration) and return-generating portfolio (public equities and alternatives); typically has significant exposure to long duration fixed-income securities.
new_option: focuses on hedging liabilities and interest rate risk via duration-matched fixed-income exposure.

original_option: GIC (Singapore SWF) uses G3 inflation (US, Japan, Eurozone); Future Fund of Australia targets CPI + 4-5%; some US endowments use Higher Education Price Index (HEPI) published by Commonfund; investor-specific choices matter.
new_option: GIC (Singapore SWF) uses G3 inflation (US, Japan, Eurozone).

original_option: defined benefit (DB) — sponsor commits to paying specified retirement benefit and assumes investment risk — and defined contribution (DC) — contributions defined but ultimate retirement benefit not specified or guaranteed by plan sponsor.
new_option: defined benefit (DB) — sponsor commits to paying specified retirement benefit and assumes investment risk.

original_option: often experience negative cash flow (benefit payments exceed contributions) and must hold more cash and other liquid investments compared to open mature plans; often in de-risking mode.
new_option: often experience negative cash flow (benefit payments exceed contributions) and must hold more cash and other liquid investments compared to open mature plans.

original_option: employer contribution of 9.5% of employee's salary — DC-based system with strict licensing obligations for trustees offering the default option (MySuper), including single diversified default strategy, avoiding excessive fees, and delivering value for money.
new_option: employer contribution of 9.5% of employee's salary.

original_option: (1) budget stabilization funds; (2) development funds; (3) savings funds; (4) reserve funds; (5) pension reserve funds — each with different investment horizons, liabilities, and liquidity profiles.
new_option: (1) budget stabilization funds.

original_option: 3% of the fund's value annually — linked to the expected real return earned by GPFG; goal of gradually phasing oil revenue into the Norwegian economy.
new_option: 3% of the fund's value annually — linked to the expected real return earned by GPFG.

original_option: (1) Constant Growth Rule (fixed amount adjusted for inflation with 4-6% caps/floors); (2) Market Value Rule (pre-specified % of moving average asset values, typically 4-6%, 3-5 year smoothing); (3) Hybrid Rule/Yale spending rule (weighted average of the two).
new_option: (1) Constant Growth Rule (fixed amount adjusted for inflation with 4-6% caps/floors).

original_option: 5% to 5.5% real (net of inflation), since endowments typically pay out 4-6% of assets annually; net spending after gifts is often 2-4% of assets so real return target reflects the pass-through of purchasing power.
new_option: 5% to 5.5% real (net of inflation), since endowments typically pay out 4-6% of assets annually.

original_option: distribute a minimum of 5% of asset value annually in grants that support their mission; failing subjects the foundation to 30% tax on undistributed income; most also have 2% excise tax on net investment income.
new_option: distribute a minimum of 5% of asset value annually in grants that support their mission.

original_option: long duration — historically set portfolio return objectives with time horizons of 20 to 40 years; range of products (Individual Life, Group Life and Disability, Individual Annuity, Retirement Plan); Term Life has one-time payout with high predictability; annuity products have ongoing payout with shorter duration subject to longevity risk.
new_option: long duration — historically set portfolio return objectives with time horizons of 20 to 40 years.

original_option: shorter duration and more volatile — involves events with lower probability of occurrence and potentially higher cost (natural disasters like hurricanes); volatile claims; short-duration high-uncertainty liability stream; benefit from global diversified portfolios that support statistical analysis via law of large numbers.
new_option: shorter duration and more volatile — involves events with lower probability of occurrence and potentially higher cost (natural disasters like hurricanes).

original_option: regulates vesting, funding requirements, and payouts for US corporate pension plans; includes fiduciary code of conduct and required disclosures; established the Pension Benefit Guaranty Corporation (PBGC) to collect premiums from plan sponsors and pay benefits to participants in terminated plans.
new_option: regulates vesting, funding requirements, and payouts for US corporate pension plans.

original_option: established minimum funding standards for DB plans; later revisions raised rates corporations could use to discount liabilities (high-grade bond yields averaged over 25 years); potential consequence: higher discount rates mean DB plans must generate higher returns to remain sustainable, typically requiring taking on greater investment risk.
new_option: established minimum funding standards for DB plans.

original_option: tax-deferred treatment — participants make pre-tax contributions and do not pay tax on investment earnings; benefit payments are taxed as ordinary income; early withdrawals before age 59½ are taxed additional 10% to encourage savings retention.
new_option: tax-deferred treatment — participants make pre-tax contributions and do not pay tax on investment earnings.

original_option: (1) allows charitable organizations flexibility in spending decisions that could be adjusted for market fluctuations, letting endowments meet fiduciary standard of prudence by maintaining purchasing power; (2) modernizes standard of prudence for management of charitable funds by adopting principles of Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) established by UPIA (1994).
new_option: (1) allows charitable organizations flexibility in spending decisions that could be adjusted for market fluctuations, letting endowments meet fiduciary standard of prudence by maintaining purchasing power.

original_option: (1) Gifts and donations usually tax-deductible up to certain percentage of adjusted gross income; (2) Income and capital gains on assets usually tax-exempt when tied to non-profit, tax-exempt organizations; (3) Payouts tax-exempt if receiving institution is exempt from income tax (payouts to for-profit business are taxable to the business).
new_option: (1) Gifts and donations usually tax-deductible up to certain percentage of adjusted gross income.

original_option: governance, risk management, and disclosure — key functions defined (e.g., internal audit), standards for those executing key functions (must not carry similar function for plan sponsor); requires 'own risk assessment' covering risk of not meeting benefit obligations and operational risk (including administrative error or fraud); greater harmonization of pension benefit statements.
new_option: governance, risk management, and disclosure — key functions defined (e.g., internal audit), standards for those executing key functions (must not carry similar function for plan sponsor).

original_option: higher pension surplus or higher funded status implies potentially greater risk tolerance — a well-funded plan has cushion to absorb investment losses; underfunded plans face pressure to grow assets faster than liabilities.
new_option: higher pension surplus or higher funded status implies potentially greater risk tolerance — a well-funded plan has cushion to absorb investment losses.

original_option: lower debt ratios and higher current and expected profitability imply greater risk tolerance; large sponsor company size relative to pension plan size implies greater risk tolerance — sponsor can absorb potential contribution volatility.
new_option: lower debt ratios and higher current and expected profitability imply greater risk tolerance.

original_option: the lower the correlation of sponsor operating results with pension asset returns, the greater the risk tolerance, all else equal — DB plan asset allocation should be modified to avoid significant exposure to assets highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry.
new_option: the lower the correlation of sponsor operating results with pension asset returns, the greater the risk tolerance, all else equal.

original_option: reducing the duration of plan liabilities, implying lower risk tolerance, all else equal — such optionality shortens effective liability duration and requires more liquid, shorter-duration matching assets.
new_option: reducing the duration of plan liabilities, implying lower risk tolerance, all else equal.

original_option: increasing tolerance — younger workforce and higher proportion of active lives means greater duration of plan liabilities and greater risk tolerance; more mature plans (higher proportion of retirees) have lower risk tolerance and shorter horizons.
new_option: increasing tolerance — younger workforce and higher proportion of active lives means greater duration of plan liabilities and greater risk tolerance.

original_option: (a) small surplus (€8M, 8% of €100M plan assets); (b) strong sponsor balance sheet (low debt); (c) sponsor is profitable despite operating in cyclical industry; (d) average age of workforce is 39 (young); (e) essentially active-lives plan with no retirees.
new_option: (a) small surplus (€8M, 8% of €100M plan assets).

original_option: if sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, avoid having plan investments highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry — plan sponsor doesn't want funded status to deteriorate when core business suffers from a cyclical downturn.
new_option: if sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, avoid having plan investments highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry.

original_option: is the risk that an individual will live longer than expected and draw more in benefit payments than the amount determined in plan liabilities; pooled in DB plans (early deaths leave assets for those who live longer); pooling doesn't mitigate the effect of rising life expectancies, which implies all else equal increases total DB plan liabilities.
new_option: is the risk that an individual will live longer than expected and draw more in benefit payments than the amount determined in plan liabilities.

original_option: over-optimistic long-term projections about ability to make contributions decades into the future; balancing adequacy (will benefits be sufficient) with sustainability (what contribution rate is sustainable, what investment return is realistic); using pension design as employee retention tool or to address worker unrest by increasing future benefits instead of immediate pay raises.
new_option: over-optimistic long-term projections about ability to make contributions decades into the future.

original_option: every 3-5 years — with Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of scenarios for asset returns and factors driving pension liabilities (importantly the discount rate) aimed at producing probability distributions for funded ratios and contribution rates at different horizons; key metrics include expected funded ratio in 10 or 15 years, surplus volatility, surplus-at-risk, and volatility of contribution rates.
new_option: every 3-5 years — with Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of scenarios for asset returns and factors driving pension liabilities (importantly the discount rate) aimed at producing probability distributions for funded ratios and contribution rates at different horizons.

original_option: review annually; revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or market environment — since IPS serves as foundation for investment program; asset allocation policy and investment guidelines typically included in an appendix that can be modified more easily.
new_option: review annually; revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or market environment — since IPS serves as foundation for investment program.

original_option: 'wishing to maximize the long-term return on investments subject to, in its opinion, an acceptably low likelihood of failing to achieve an ongoing 105% funding level' — balancing sustainable return with acceptable funded ratio risk.
new_option: 'wishing to maximize the long-term return on investments subject to, in its opinion, an acceptably low likelihood of failing to achieve an ongoing 105% funding level'.

original_option: 'to build an investment portfolio to outperform inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods while accepting a high level of risk that is expected to generate 4-6 negative annual returns over any 20-year period' — captures return, horizon, and risk transparency.
new_option: 'to build an investment portfolio to outperform inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods while accepting a high level of risk that is expected to generate 4-6 negative annual returns over any 20-year period'.

original_option: 80% of prior year's spending adjusted for inflation (CPI in range 0-6%) plus 20% of long-term spending rate (5.0%) applied to 12-quarter rolling average of market values — hybrid rule providing budget stability while adjusting to fluctuating market value.
new_option: 80% of prior year's spending adjusted for inflation (CPI in range 0-6%) plus 20% of long-term spending rate (5.0%) applied to 12-quarter rolling average of market values.

original_option: Global equity 20-40% (MSCI World benchmark); Private equity & VC 15-25% (Cambridge Associates US PE Index); Private real estate 5-15% (NCREIF); Real assets 5-15% (CPI-U + 5%); Absolute return 15-25% (HFRI Fund of Funds); FI & cash 5-15% (Citigroup US Treasury Index).
new_option: Global equity 20-40% (MSCI World benchmark).

original_option: (1) Equity dominance (highest-performing over long term); (2) Illiquid assets (private illiquid expected to outperform more-liquid public by exploiting market inefficiencies); (3) Global orientation (broadest possible set of investment opportunities); (4) Diversification (thoughtful diversification within/between asset classes lowers volatility and raises compound returns).
new_option: (1) Equity dominance (highest-performing over long term).

original_option: performance measurement and attribution for quarter and trailing periods in absolute terms and relative to established benchmarks, asset allocation of the total portfolio, and market value of the total portfolio — provided to Board of Trustees, IC, and other stakeholders.
new_option: performance measurement and attribution for quarter and trailing periods in absolute terms and relative to established benchmarks, asset allocation of the total portfolio, and market value of the total portfolio.

original_option: achieve a long-term target return (usually defined in nominal terms) over specified horizon (3-5 years or as long as 10 or 25 years) with an appropriate level of risk that allows the plan to meet its contractual liabilities; secondary objective could be minimize present value of expected cash contributions.
new_option: achieve a long-term target return (usually defined in nominal terms) over specified horizon (3-5 years or as long as 10 or 25 years) with an appropriate level of risk that allows the plan to meet its contractual liabilities.

original_option: large differences by country — Australia and US have largest proportions of DC pension assets and largest allocations to equities; US, Australia, and Netherlands have highest proportions of equity allocations in domestic markets (significant home bias); Japan has been slowest to increase alternatives.
new_option: large differences by country — Australia and US have largest proportions of DC pension assets and largest allocations to equities.

original_option: desire to reduce their funded ratio volatility (LDI-driven) despite low expected returns — offset against US public pension plans that reduced FI allocations overall while increasing allocations to high yield (riskier) bonds in FI space, driven by the large gap between expected rate of return and yield on long-term government securities.
new_option: desire to reduce their funded ratio volatility (LDI-driven) despite low expected returns.

original_option: producing equity-like returns over the long run with relatively low drawdowns; low or negative correlations with traditional investments; hedge funds have lower volatility than equity markets; private assets have historically shown lower drawdowns partly due to lack of mark-to-market and appraisal-based valuations.
new_option: producing equity-like returns over the long run with relatively low drawdowns.

original_option: significantly increased allocation to alternatives from 39% in 2005 to 60% in 2020; came at the expense of public equities (reduced from 45% to 30%) and fixed income (reduced from 14% to 11%).
new_option: significantly increased allocation to alternatives from 39% in 2005 to 60% in 2020.

original_option: 50-60 bps higher annual returns compared to smaller endowments (< $500M) over 10-year period ending June 2020; compounds to significant gap; higher returns allowed larger endowments to pay out larger part of assets to support universities.
new_option: 50-60 bps higher annual returns compared to smaller endowments (< $500M) over 10-year period ending June 2020.

original_option: significant liquidity challenges — several large endowments were forced to (1) sell portions of private investment portfolios in secondary markets, (2) reduce payouts to universities, or (3) issue bonds to bridge liquidity needs; David Swensen has warned against blanket application of the Endowment Model.
new_option: significant liquidity challenges — several large endowments were forced to (1) sell portions of private investment portfolios in secondary markets, (2) reduce payouts to universities, or (3) issue bonds to bridge liquidity needs.

original_option: supports national economic development through investing in essential infrastructure, innovation, or key industries; implicit real return target to raise real domestic GDP growth and productivity; some initiatives long-term (infrastructure), others medium-term (medical research fund).
new_option: supports national economic development through investing in essential infrastructure, innovation, or key industries.

original_option: long-term wealth transfer to future generations after natural wealth is depleted; long-term liabilities; may have real return objective or explicit spending policy (like endowments); invests in risky and illiquid assets (equities, alternatives); should avoid assets highly correlated with the non-renewable resources being diversified from.
new_option: long-term wealth transfer to future generations after natural wealth is depleted.

original_option: typically hold 50-70% in equity or equity-equivalent to achieve return targets; remainder invested in liquid FI that could be readily sold if central bank reserves need to be replenished; long horizons but liquidity needs higher than savings funds but lower than stabilization funds; examples: CIC, KIC, GIC (Singapore).
new_option: typically hold 50-70% in equity or equity-equivalent to achieve return targets.

original_option: invest a significant portion in cash and high-grade fixed-income instruments that are very liquid with little risk of significant drawdown — because they may need to support the government budget on short notice during commodity price shocks or other external shocks.
new_option: invest a significant portion in cash and high-grade fixed-income instruments that are very liquid with little risk of significant drawdown.

original_option: during accumulation phase hold significant equities and relatively illiquid investments; once decumulation phase begins, allocation gradually shifts toward more liquid, high-quality fixed-income investments; Australia's Future Fund targets CPI + 4-5% per year over long term with acceptable risk.
new_option: during accumulation phase hold significant equities and relatively illiquid investments.

original_option: similar investment philosophy despite important differences: foundations legally required to pay out 5% of assets annually (US private grant-making) plus investment expenses vs endowments with more-flexible spending rules; foundations generally have higher liquidity needs; foundations may pursue mission-related/impact investing (Ford Foundation US$1B over 10 years).
new_option: similar investment philosophy despite important differences: foundations legally required to pay out 5% of assets annually (US private grant-making) plus investment expenses vs endowments with more-flexible spending rules.

original_option: skill sets — university endowments typically have alumni sitting on boards (people with special relationship + potentially significant financial market skills like PE or HF); foundation board members are typically individuals involved with grant making and not necessarily investment professionals; this difference may affect quality of board oversight, delegation, and investment decision quality.
new_option: skill sets — university endowments typically have alumni sitting on boards (people with special relationship + potentially significant financial market skills like PE or HF).

original_option: loans as the largest component (typically comprising up to 50% or more of assets of large international banks that dominate the sector); next largest is debt securities (typically 25% or more of total assets); remainder consists of currency, deposits with central banks, receivables, and bullion.
new_option: loans as the largest component (typically comprising up to 50% or more of assets of large international banks that dominate the sector).

original_option: deposits are largest component (usually more than half of total liabilities), consisting of time deposits (savings, CDs) and demand deposits (checking, savings accessible via online/ATM); wholesale funding (Fed Funds, public funds); long-term debt 10-15%; trading/repurchase payables 10-20%.
new_option: deposits are largest component (usually more than half of total liabilities), consisting of time deposits (savings, CDs) and demand deposits (checking, savings accessible via online/ATM).

original_option: tactical horizon directly impacted by nature and maturities of asset base and liability structure — instruments held tend to have far shorter maturities than those held by other financial institutions; strategic horizon is perpetuity because of corporate structure (as long as or longer than DB plans, endowments, foundations, SWFs).
new_option: tactical horizon directly impacted by nature and maturities of asset base and liability structure — instruments held tend to have far shorter maturities than those held by other financial institutions.

original_option: Liquidity Coverage Ratios (LCRs) require highly liquid assets to constitute more than 100% of highly probable near-term expected cash outflows; Net Stable Funding Ratios (NSFRs) set minimum requirements for stable funding sources (including capital, long-term debt, non-volatile deposits) relative to assets.
new_option: Liquidity Coverage Ratios (LCRs) require highly liquid assets to constitute more than 100% of highly probable near-term expected cash outflows.

original_option: USD 10.1B revolving credit facility with 5-year term from 26 leading global financial institutions (led by ING and Santander) — pricing mechanism incentivizes AB InBev on 4 sustainability KPIs: (1) water efficiency at breweries; (2) PET recycled content; (3) sourcing renewable electricity per RE100; (4) reducing GHG emissions per Climate Action Goal.
new_option: USD 10.1B revolving credit facility with 5-year term from 26 leading global financial institutions (led by ING and Santander) — pricing mechanism incentivizes AB InBev on 4 sustainability KPIs: (1) water efficiency at breweries.

original_option: Whole life, term life, universal life insurance, and fixed annuities → company bears investment risk, general account; variable life insurance and variable annuities → policyholder bears investment risk, separate account.
new_option: Whole life, term life, universal life insurance, and fixed annuities → company bears investment risk, general account.

original_option: external parties (shareholders, derivatives counterparties, policyholders, creditors, regulators, rating agencies including A.M. Best, S&P, Moody's, Fitch) and internal parties (employees, management, boards); tightly regulated in most countries by state or national authorities.
new_option: external parties (shareholders, derivatives counterparties, policyholders, creditors, regulators, rating agencies including A.M. Best, S&P, Moody's, Fitch) and internal parties (employees, management, boards).

original_option: (1) hazard (severity and frequency); (2) exposure (building's physical properties); (3) vulnerability (destruction rates as function of hazard and exposure); as of end 2020, AXA estimated annual average losses (AAL) for its real estate portfolio at EUR 4.3M from floods and EUR 6.2M from windstorms; considers IPCC's Representative Concentration Pathways 2.6 and 8.5 scenarios.
new_option: (1) hazard (severity and frequency).

original_option: direct costs of trading — broker commission costs, transaction taxes, stamp duties, and fees paid to exchanges; costs for which a trader could receive a receipt.
new_option: direct costs of trading — broker commission costs, transaction taxes, stamp duties, and fees paid to exchanges.

original_option: (1) bid-ask spread, (2) market impact/price impact, (3) delay costs/slippage, (4) opportunity costs/unrealized P&L — indirect costs caused by the market impact of trading and price concessions impatient traders make to complete trades.
new_option: (1) bid-ask spread, (2) market impact/price impact, (3) delay costs/slippage, (4) opportunity costs/unrealized P&L.

original_option: the ask price minus the bid price — the price at which a trader will sell a specified quantity minus the price at which a trader will buy a specified quantity; traders who want to trade quickly buy at higher prices and sell at lower prices than those willing to wait.
new_option: the ask price minus the bid price — the price at which a trader will sell a specified quantity minus the price at which a trader will buy a specified quantity.

original_option: is the effect of the trade on transaction prices — traders who want to fill large orders often must move prices to encourage others to trade with them; small market orders have small market impact because filled by traders willing to trade at quoted prices; larger orders require larger price concessions.
new_option: is the effect of the trade on transaction prices — traders who want to fill large orders often must move prices to encourage others to trade with them.

original_option: arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately — traders fail to profit when they fill their orders after prices move as they expect; often measured on the portion of the order carried over from one day to the next; delay is costly when price moves away from an order (up for buy, down for sell), often because information leaks into the market.
new_option: arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately — traders fail to profit when they fill their orders after prices move as they expect.

original_option: the failure to execute a trade promptly — traders fail to profit when their orders fail to trade and prices move as they expect; often significant for large orders; when delays cause a portion of the order to go unfilled, associated cost is opportunity cost.
new_option: the failure to execute a trade promptly — traders fail to profit when their orders fail to trade and prices move as they expect.

original_option: costs of employing buy-side traders, costs of equipping them with proper trading tools (electronic systems and data), costs of office space (trading rooms or corners); small buy-side institutions often avoid these by not employing buy-side traders and having portfolio managers submit orders directly to brokers.
new_option: costs of employing buy-side traders, costs of equipping them with proper trading tools (electronic systems and data), costs of office space (trading rooms or corners).

original_option: 1.64 = best ask 100.49 (Dealer C) - best bid 98.85 (Dealer A); lower than any individual dealer's spread because the dealer with highest bid is not the dealer with lowest ask.
new_option: 1.64 = best ask 100.49 (Dealer C) - best bid 98.85 (Dealer A).

original_option: for buy: 2 × (Trade price − Midquote at order entry); for sell: 2 × (Midquote at order entry − Trade price); estimates the spread that traders would have observed if the quoted ask (buy) or bid (sell) equaled the trade price.
new_option: for buy: 2 × (Trade price − Midquote at order entry).

original_option: trade execution prices are better than quoted prices — buy fills below the ask price or sell fills above the bid; effective spread is less than the quoted spread when this happens.
new_option: trade execution prices are better than quoted prices — buy fills below the ask price or sell fills above the bid.

original_option: traders split large orders into many parts filled over time — orders often move the market and cause bid/ask prices to rise or fall; the market impact makes trading expensive (especially for the last parts to fill) but effective spread will not fully identify this cost if computed separately for each trade.
new_option: traders split large orders into many parts filled over time — orders often move the market and cause bid/ask prices to rise or fall.

original_option: C$0.06 = 2 × (C$10.18 midpoint − C$10.15 trade price) — less than quoted spread of C$0.12; the trade received price improvement because it sold above the bid.
new_option: C$0.06 = 2 × (C$10.18 midpoint − C$10.15 trade price) — less than quoted spread of C$0.12.

original_option: for buy: Trade size × (Trade VWAP − VWAP benchmark); for sell: Trade size × (VWAP benchmark − Trade VWAP); VWAP typically computed as sum of dollar volume of trades divided by total quantity within the interval; often called 'interval VWAP'.
new_option: for buy: Trade size × (Trade VWAP − VWAP benchmark).

original_option: ~C$266 = 10,000 × (C$10.0946 − C$10.0680); the VWAP benchmark is C$131,230/13,000 = C$10.0946 (all trades that day); trade VWAP is C$10.0680 (only fund's 10,000 shares).

when trades being evaluated are substantial fraction of all trades in benchmark, or trades took place at same rate as other trades, Trade VWAP and VWAP benchmark will be nearly equal — suggesting trades were not costly; but conclusion misleading if trade had substantial price impact; bias toward zero explains why measure is popular.
new_option: when trades being evaluated are substantial fraction of all trades in benchmark, or trades took place at same rate as other trades, Trade VWAP and VWAP benchmark will be nearly equal — suggesting trades were not costly.

original_option: greater than the quoted spread — because the large order size that exceeds the bid size causes the order to walk down the limit order book; the average sale price is less than the bid so effective spread is higher than quoted spread.
new_option: greater than the quoted spread — because the large order size that exceeds the bid size causes the order to walk down the limit order book.

original_option: the total cost of implementing an investment decision by capturing all explicit and implicit costs — includes market impact costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs which are often significant for large orders; views trading from an investment management perspective.
new_option: the total cost of implementing an investment decision by capturing all explicit and implicit costs — includes market impact costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs which are often significant for large orders.

original_option: the values of the actual portfolio with the paper portfolio (constructed on assumption trades could be arranged at prices that prevailed when the decision to trade was made); the prevailing price is the decision price/arrival price/strike price, generally the midquote at the time of decision.
new_option: the values of the actual portfolio with the paper portfolio (constructed on assumption trades could be arranged at prices that prevailed when the decision to trade was made).

original_option: orders are large and split over time — IS captures market impact costs, delay costs, opportunity costs that arise as prices move away during protracted execution; effective spread misses these when computed per trade.
new_option: orders are large and split over time — IS captures market impact costs, delay costs, opportunity costs that arise as prices move away during protracted execution.

original_option: trades could be arranged at prices prevailing when the decision to trade was made — a hypothetical benchmark measuring what would have been achieved without market impact, delay, or opportunity cost.
new_option: trades could be arranged at prices prevailing when the decision to trade was made.

original_option: the implementation shortfall — a positive number represents the total costs of implementation; can be decomposed into explicit costs, market impact/spread costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs.

original_option: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts — 'in the long run, regulations catch up to you and the cost of pre-treatment or appropriate handling of waste is much lower than the penalties.'
new_option: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts.

original_option: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river; can we inform their thinking?' The CIO explores whether investing has broader impact utility.
new_option: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river.

original_option: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach; 'we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'
new_option: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach.

original_option: what to liquidate — and in what order — in a crisis to meet cash flows and how to rebalance the portfolio in a crisis; sharing it with the board to get buy-in and mitigate risk of board pressure for suboptimal short-term decisions.
new_option: what to liquidate — and in what order — in a crisis to meet cash flows and how to rebalance the portfolio in a crisis.

original_option: (1) Establish liquidity risk parameters; (2) Assess the liquidity of current portfolio and monitor evolution over time; (3) Develop a cash flow model and project future cash flows; (4) Stress test liquidity needs and cash flow projections; (5) Develop an emergency action plan.
new_option: (1) Establish liquidity risk parameters.

original_option: secondary sales (as done with ABC — sold to strategic buyer for $27M), passing to distressed-asset team (proposed for ABC), reducing commitments to further vintage-year investments, and being prepared for longer exit horizons than public equity.
new_option: secondary sales (as done with ABC.

original_option: positive collaboration — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator'; if delays are found to be government's liability, request concession extension and 'sit down with AOG to positively collaborate on retooling the whole picture.'
new_option: positive collaboration — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator'.

original_option: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor; ±10% CapEx sensitivity applied in modeling.
new_option: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor.

original_option: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations; break clauses for out-of-line performance.
new_option: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations.

original_option: 'we're not just investing and then forgetting things' — before modernization starts, research what issues are in the minds of the people of Atsui, design new community programs, make a positive impact first, and support employees through training.
new_option: 'we're not just investing and then forgetting things'.

original_option: the strategic buyer had a natural fit — an international beverage company exporting to Atsui whose sales were affected by weaker Atsui currency saw producing locally as advantageous (natural FX hedge); R-SWF avoided diplomatic crisis and reputational damage at $27M.
new_option: the strategic buyer had a natural fit — an international beverage company exporting to Atsui whose sales were affected by weaker Atsui currency saw producing locally as advantageous (natural FX hedge).

original_option: dedicating an entire internal investment committee meeting to identifying and discussing potential risks of two relatively small investment opportunities — ample time was taken to allow senior management to express concerns and discuss mitigation.
new_option: dedicating an entire internal investment committee meeting to identifying and discussing potential risks of two relatively small investment opportunities.

original_option: too little effort in quantifying various risks and agreeing on specific actions if risks materialized — the team could have done a better job of scenario analysis (base/optimistic/pessimistic) and put together an action plan for risk mitigation and potential hedging tools prior to making the investments.
new_option: too little effort in quantifying various risks and agreeing on specific actions if risks materialized.

original_option: the board setting overall risk tolerance for the organization consistent with its objectives and constraints — risk tolerance captures amount of market risk the institution is willing and able to take to maximize expected returns; informs the strategic asset allocation.
new_option: the board setting overall risk tolerance for the organization consistent with its objectives and constraints — risk tolerance captures amount of market risk the institution is willing and able to take to maximize expected returns.

original_option: asset-only terms (for SWFs, endowments, and foundations) or asset/liability terms (for pension funds and insurance companies) — depending on investor type.
new_option: asset-only terms (for SWFs, endowments, and foundations) or asset/liability terms (for pension funds and insurance companies).

original_option: implement the SAA and invest assets either internally or through external managers; manage and monitor risks associated with SAA implementation; report to the board; perform risk factor analysis (equity/interest rate/credit/inflation/currency/liquidity risks) with both quantitative and qualitative approaches.
new_option: implement the SAA and invest assets either internally or through external managers.

original_option: whether returns achieved on the investment adequately compensated the fund for giving up liquidity — one way is comparing PE returns with public equity returns.
new_option: whether returns achieved on the investment adequately compensated the fund for giving up liquidity.

original_option: sometimes overlooked — currency risk can have an outsized and unexpected impact on the overall return; while it can be hedged in some cases, doing so is typically costly or even impossible when investing in emerging and frontier markets.
new_option: sometimes overlooked — currency risk can have an outsized and unexpected impact on the overall return.

original_option: identifying and measuring risks and monitoring how they change over time; ensuring alignment with IPS guidelines and more granular internal guidelines; risk reporting to internal investment committee and board; taking action to bring risk exposures back into alignment when needed.
new_option: identifying and measuring risks and monitoring how they change over time.

original_option: the active risk budget accurately reflects areas where excess return can be expected; most active risk comes from stock picking, not loading on equity risk factors (growth, momentum, quality).
new_option: the active risk budget accurately reflects areas where excess return can be expected.

original_option: perform scenario analysis for both investments — present a base case, an optimistic case, and a pessimistic case; agree on specific actions that could be taken if some of those risks materialized (action plan conditional on bad outcomes).
new_option: perform scenario analysis for both investments — present a base case, an optimistic case, and a pessimistic case.

original_option: combine strengths (dedicated committee time, senior management input, systematic voting) with improvements: quantitative risk quantification, formal scenario analysis (base/optimistic/pessimistic), pre-investment action plans conditional on bad outcomes, and systematic identification of reputational and ESG risks — enabling more proactive, less reactive risk management.
new_option: combine strengths (dedicated committee time, senior management input, systematic voting) with improvements: quantitative risk quantification, formal scenario analysis (base/optimistic/pessimistic), pre-investment action plans conditional on bad outcomes, and systematic identification of reputational and ESG risks.

original_option: performance measurement — what was the portfolio's performance?; performance attribution — how was the performance achieved?; performance appraisal — was the performance achieved through manager skill or luck?
new_option: performance measurement — what was the portfolio's performance?.

original_option: provides an overall indication of portfolio's performance, typically relative to a benchmark; in its simplest form, calculation of investment returns for both portfolio and benchmark; must consider risk incurred to achieve return; risk measured using ex post (looking back in time) and ex ante (looking forward in time) techniques (ex post: volatility/SD, appraisal ratios; ex ante: VaR at a point in time).
new_option: provides an overall indication of portfolio's performance, typically relative to a benchmark.

original_option: builds on foundation of investment returns and risk; helps explain how performance was achieved or how risk was incurred; can be used to explain either absolute returns or relative returns; understand what portion of returns was driven by active manager decisions vs exposures not specifically targeted; decompose excess return into component sources; risk attribution can decompose risk incurred.
new_option: builds on foundation of investment returns and risk.

original_option: third component of performance evaluation; makes use of risk, return, and attribution analyses to draw conclusions regarding quality of a portfolio's performance; attempts to distinguish manager skill from luck; did the manager's decisions help achieve a better outcome, or was the outcome due to market changes outside manager's control; if superior results can be attributed to skill, higher likelihood manager will generate superior performance in future.
new_option: third component of performance evaluation.

original_option: investment return tells us what the portfolio achieved over a specific period irrespective of peer or benchmark performance (absolute return); performance measurement also provides basis to understand difference between portfolio return and its benchmark return (excess return); this is different from 'excess return' used in Sharpe ratio context (return above risk-free rate).
new_option: investment return tells us what the portfolio achieved over a specific period irrespective of peer or benchmark performance (absolute return).

original_option: (1) account for all of the portfolio's return or risk exposure; (2) reflect the investment decision-making process; (3) quantify the active decisions of the portfolio manager; (4) provide a complete understanding of the excess return/risk of the portfolio.
new_option: (1) account for all of the portfolio's return or risk exposure.

original_option: at best the attribution is incomplete and at worst the quality of the attribution analysis is brought into doubt; if the attribution does not reflect the investment decision-making process, then the analysis will be of little value to either the portfolio manager or the client.
new_option: at best the attribution is incomplete and at worst the quality of the attribution analysis is brought into doubt.

original_option: if portfolio manager is a genuine bottom-up stock picker who ignores sector benchmark weights, then measuring the impact of sector allocation against these weights is not measuring decisions made as part of the investment process; sector effects are merely a byproduct of the manager's investment decisions.
new_option: if portfolio manager is a genuine bottom-up stock picker who ignores sector benchmark weights, then measuring the impact of sector allocation against these weights is not measuring decisions made as part of the investment process.

original_option: used by senior management, client relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations staff, portfolio managers, and sales/marketing professionals; provides insights to investment decision-making process; when it accurately reflects the investment decision-making process, return attribution provides quality control for the investment process and provides senior management with a tool to manage a complex business with multiple investment strategies; especially important when performance is weak (portfolio managers must demonstrate understanding of their performance).
new_option: used by senior management, client relationship specialists, risk controllers, operations staff, portfolio managers, and sales/marketing professionals.

original_option: performance attribution includes both return attribution and risk attribution (although in practice, 'performance attribution' is often used to mean 'return attribution'); return attribution analyzes impact of active investment decisions on returns; risk attribution analyzes the risk consequences of those decisions; depending on purpose of analysis, risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms.
new_option: performance attribution includes both return attribution and risk attribution (although in practice, 'performance attribution' is often used to mean 'return attribution').

original_option: measures the effect of the sponsor's choice to deviate from the strategic asset allocation, including effect of 'gaps' between strategic asset allocation and its implementation (e.g., where sum of the managers' benchmarks is equal to something other than the benchmark index); attribution used to evaluate the asset owner's tactical asset allocation and manager selection decisions.
new_option: measures the effect of the sponsor's choice to deviate from the strategic asset allocation, including effect of 'gaps' between strategic asset allocation and its implementation (e.g., where sum of the managers' benchmarks is equal to something other than the benchmark index).

original_option: provides good starting point for a conversation with clients, explaining both positive and negative aspects of recent performance; return attribution analysis is particularly important when performance is weak; portfolio managers must demonstrate understanding of their performance, provide rationale for their decisions, and generate confidence in their ability to add value in the future.
new_option: provides good starting point for a conversation with clients, explaining both positive and negative aspects of recent performance.

original_option: may be either macro attribution or micro attribution; attribution analysis at the portfolio level is not automatically one or the other; macro attribution measures sponsor's decisions to deviate from strategic allocation and manager selection decisions; micro attribution measures individual portfolio manager decisions on total fund performance.
new_option: may be either macro attribution or micro attribution.

original_option: uses only the total portfolio returns over a period to identify the components of the investment process that have generated the returns; most appropriate when underlying portfolio holding information is not available with sufficient frequency at required level of detail (e.g., hedge funds because can be difficult to obtain underlying holdings); easiest method to implement but because it does not use underlying holdings, it is the least accurate of the three approaches and the most vulnerable to data manipulation.
new_option: uses only the total portfolio returns over a period to identify the components of the investment process that have generated the returns.

original_option: references the beginning-of-period holdings of the portfolio; calculated with monthly, weekly, or daily data — accuracy improves when using data with shorter time intervals; for longer evaluation periods, link together attribution results for shorter measurement periods; because it fails to capture the impact of any transactions made during the measurement period, may not reconcile to actual portfolio return; residual might be described as a timing or trading effect; most appropriate for investment strategies with little turnover (e.g., passive strategies).
new_option: references the beginning-of-period holdings of the portfolio.

original_option: uses both the holdings of the portfolio and the transactions (purchases and sales) that occurred during the evaluation period; weights and returns reflect all transactions during period including transaction costs; most accurate type of attribution analysis but also most difficult and time-consuming to implement; underlying data must be complete, accurate, and reconciled from period to period; entire excess return can be quantified and explained; return will reconcile with return presented to client; can be used as diagnostic tool to identify errors.
new_option: uses both the holdings of the portfolio and the transactions (purchases and sales) that occurred during the evaluation period.

original_option: ignoring transactions creates a timing or trading effect; e.g., in a daily holdings-based attribution, securities are included at end of day they are purchased and excluded at end of day they are sold; if transaction price is significantly different from closing price, attribution analysis can differ significantly from actual performance.
new_option: ignoring transactions creates a timing or trading effect.

original_option: (1) allocation effect A_i = (w_i - W_i) × B_i; (2) selection effect S_i = W_i × (R_i - B_i); (3) interaction effect I_i = (w_i - W_i) × (R_i - B_i); where w_i = portfolio sector weight, W_i = benchmark sector weight, R_i = portfolio return in sector, B_i = benchmark return in sector.
new_option: (1) allocation effect A_i = (w_i - W_i) × B_i.

original_option: all overweight positions in sectors with positive returns will generate positive allocation effects irrespective of overall benchmark return; overweighting sector i that earns positive return B_i > 0 results in positive allocation A_i = (w_i - W_i) × B_i > 0 even when sector return is less than overall benchmark return (B_i < B); when sector return is negative, overweighting produces negative allocation; but if portfolio manager is overweight in negative market that has outperformed overall benchmark, effect should be positive — this is the BHB flaw.
new_option: all overweight positions in sectors with positive returns will generate positive allocation effects irrespective of overall benchmark return.

original_option: solves BHB problem by modifying asset allocation factor to compare returns with overall benchmark: A_i = (w_i - W_i) × (B_i - B); allocation effect at portfolio level (BS - B) unchanged from BHB model; applied to Exhibit 2 data: Energy 0.0%, Health care (30%-20%)×(-2%-8.2%) = -1.02%, Financials (20%-30%)×(12%-8.2%) = -0.38%; total still -1.4% at portfolio level but individual sector effects change.
new_option: solves BHB problem by modifying asset allocation factor to compare returns with overall benchmark: A_i = (w_i - W_i) × (B_i - B).

original_option: R_p - R_f = a_p + b_p1 × RMRF + b_p2 × SMB + b_p3 × HML + b_p4 × WML + E_p; explains excess return on portfolio in terms of portfolio's sensitivity to a market index (RMRF), a market-capitalization factor (SMB), a book-value-to-price factor (HML), and a momentum factor (WML); commonly used equity attribution model per Carhart 1997.
new_option: R_p - R_f = a_p + b_p1 × RMRF + b_p2 × SMB + b_p3 × HML + b_p4 × WML + E_p.

original_option: (1) exposure decomposition — duration based; (2) yield curve decomposition — duration based; (3) yield curve decomposition — full repricing based; candidates are not responsible for calculating fixed-income attribution but should be able to interpret results.
new_option: (1) exposure decomposition — duration based.

original_option: top-down attribution approach that seeks to explain active management of portfolio relative to benchmark; typically working through hierarchy of decisions from top to bottom (portfolio duration bets, yield curve positioning, sector bets, each relative to benchmark); 'exposure decomposition' relates to decomposition of portfolio risk exposures by grouping portfolio's component bonds by specified characteristics (duration, bond sector); 'duration based' relates to typical use of duration to represent interest rate exposure decisions; similar to Brinson-type equity attribution models but groups by market value weights in duration buckets; used primarily for marketing and client reports.
new_option: top-down attribution approach that seeks to explain active management of portfolio relative to benchmark.

original_option: can be either top-down or built bottom-up from security level; estimates return of securities, sector buckets, or years-to-maturity buckets using known relationship between duration and changes in YTM: % Total return = % Income return + % Price return, where % Price return ≈ -Duration × Change in YTM; change in YTM broken down into yield curve factors (level, slope, curvature) and spread factors; typically used when preparing reports for analysts and portfolio managers rather than in marketing or client reports; exchanges better transparency for more operational complexity.
new_option: can be either top-down or built bottom-up from security level.

original_option: instead of estimating price changes from changes in duration and yields to maturity, bonds repriced from zero-coupon curves (spot rates); more precise pricing and allows for broader range of instrument types and yield changes; supports greater variety of quantitative modeling beyond fixed-income attribution (e.g., ex ante risk); better aligned with how portfolio managers typically view instruments; requires full capability to reprice all financial instruments in portfolio and benchmark; complex nature can make it more difficult and costly to administer operationally and results more difficult to understand particularly for non-fixed-income professionals.
new_option: instead of estimating price changes from changes in duration and yields to maturity, bonds repriced from zero-coupon curves (spot rates).

original_option: Short: less than or equal to 5; Mid: greater than 5 and less than or equal to 10; Long: greater than 10; practitioner should take care when selecting upper and lower bands — grouping bonds of different durations in same bucket measures combined impact of those bonds relative to combined impact of similar bonds in benchmark (bond with duration 5.5 treated the same as bond with duration 9.5).
new_option: Short: less than or equal to 5.

original_option: practitioners should consider the investment decision-making process and the type of attribution analysis; risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms depending on purpose of analysis; when risk relative to a benchmark is the focus, risk attribution might identify and evaluate portfolio's deviations from a benchmark's exposures to risk factors.
new_option: practitioners should consider the investment decision-making process and the type of attribution analysis.

original_option: risk attribution to evaluate the marginal contribution to total risk for each position; may be appropriate when practitioner is focused on absolute risk decomposition, such as for a fixed-income manager focused on macroeconomic factors on credit markets who follows top-down investment process — but per practice problem answers, the top-down macro FI manager's process is best attributed by decomposing historical returns into a top-down factor framework.
new_option: risk attribution to evaluate the marginal contribution to total risk for each position.

original_option: depending on the purpose of the analysis, risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms; when risk relative to a benchmark is the focus, risk attribution analysis might identify and evaluate a portfolio's deviations from a benchmark's exposures to risk factors; long-only equity manager benchmarked against cap-weighted index may prefer relative risk measure; long/short equity manager benchmarked against cash plus target usually prefers absolute risk measure.
new_option: depending on the purpose of the analysis, risk may be viewed in absolute or benchmark-relative terms.

original_option: one of several risk attribution approaches; particularly appropriate for benchmark-relative mandates where investment decision-making process involves top-down allocation and bottom-up selection; but for a top-down fixed-income manager focused on macroeconomic factors, decomposing returns into a top-down factor framework more accurately reflects the investment process than allocation/selection tracking risk decomposition.
new_option: one of several risk attribution approaches.

original_option: at fund sponsor level, first decision is to allocate certain weight to asset classes (strategic asset allocation); if fund sponsor does not manage funds internally, delegates second investment decision to investment managers regarding tactical deviations from strategic asset allocation; sponsor might also select multiple portfolio managers to manage against specific mandates within given asset class; macro attribution measures fund sponsor decisions; micro attribution measures individual portfolio manager decisions.
new_option: at fund sponsor level, first decision is to allocate certain weight to asset classes (strategic asset allocation).

original_option: Allocation = (78% - 75%) × [0.32 - (-0.03)] = 3% × 0.35 = 0.01; Selection + Interaction = [75% × (0.99 - 0.32)] + [(78% - 75%) × (0.99 - 0.32)] = 0.5025 + 0.0201 = 0.52; sponsor overweighted value equities, value equities outperformed aggregate benchmark, decision added to portfolio return; value manager outperformed benchmark so manager selection decision independent of overweight also added value.

Allocation = (22% - 25%) × [-1.08 - (-0.03)] = -3% × -1.05 = 0.03; Selection + Interaction = [25% × (0.82 - (-1.08))] + [(22% - 25%) × (0.82 - (-1.08))] = 0.475 + -0.057 = 0.42; sponsor underweighted growth equities, growth underperformed aggregate benchmark, decision to underweight added to return; growth manager outperformed growth benchmark so selection decision added value; interaction effect negative (underweighted a manager who outperformed).

at segment level (small-cap value, large-cap value, large-cap growth): Allocation = (w_i - W_i) × (B_i - B); Selection + Interaction = W_i × (R_i - B_i) + (w_i - W_i) × (R_i - B_i); small-cap value example: Allocation = (20% - 25%) × [1.52 - (-0.03)] = -0.08; Selection + Interaction = [25% × (2.39 - 1.52)] + [(20% - 25%) × (2.39 - 1.52)] = 0.17.

same allocation and selection formulas can be used to calculate contribution of individual security decisions within sectors; if pricing sources used in portfolio and benchmark are identical, any difference in return caused by transaction activity; example: Chevron (24% portfolio, 30% benchmark, 10% return in both) — allocation (24% - 30%) × (10% - 7.05%) = -0.18%; ExxonMobil transaction (41% × (5% - 6%) = -0.41% transaction cost/timing effect).

focus on the cash flows that the asset must generate; most often used when assets are required to pay a specific future liability (e.g., in a defined benefit pension plan); allow asset owner to track fund's progress toward fully funded status (assets ≥ liabilities) or track performance of assets relative to changes in liabilities; performance relative to liabilities important because it would be possible for portfolio to outperform a market index but still not meet liabilities.
new_option: focus on the cash flows that the asset must generate.

original_option: duration of cap-weighted index is usually shorter than duration of most pension plans' liabilities; many fixed-income indexes heavily weighted toward corporate bonds in short maturities which may represent greater degree of credit risk than plan desires; well-diversified portfolio of individual bonds that minimizes idiosyncratic risk could be used as benchmark; more recent innovation is liability-driven investment (LDI) indexes (Bloomberg Barclays LDI Index Series — six investible indexes designed for pension-liability hedging).
new_option: duration of cap-weighted index is usually shorter than duration of most pension plans' liabilities.

original_option: average number of years to retirement in workforce; percentage of workforce that is retired; average participant life expectancy; whether benefits are indexed to inflation; whether plan offers early retirement option; whether sponsor could increase plan contributions; correlation between plan assets and sponsoring company's operating assets (lower correlation desired); whether plan is going concern.
new_option: average number of years to retirement in workforce.

original_option: nominal bonds, real return bonds, and common shares are assets most commonly found in liability-driven portfolios; allocation to each asset class is driven by proportion of accrued versus future obligations, whether benefits are inflation indexed, and whether plan is growing; younger workforce means more allocated to equities; greater inflation indexing of benefits implies more inflation-indexed bonds.
new_option: nominal bonds, real return bonds, and common shares are assets most commonly found in liability-driven portfolios.

original_option: (1) absolute (including target) return benchmarks; (2) broad market indexes; (3) style indexes; (4) factor-model-based benchmarks; (5) returns-based (Sharpe style analysis) benchmarks; (6) manager universes (peer groups); (7) custom security-based (strategy) benchmarks.
new_option: (1) absolute (including target) return benchmarks.

original_option: minimum target return manager is expected to beat; return may be stated minimum (e.g., 9%), stated as spread above market index (e.g., Euro Interbank Offered Rate + 4%), or determined from actuarial assumptions; example: 20% per annum return for private equity investment; market-neutral long-short equity funds often have absolute return benchmarks — such funds consist of long and short positions in perceived undervalued and overvalued equities; expected to be insensitive to broad equity market movements (market neutral with beta of zero); market-neutral benchmarks may be specified as three-month Treasury bill return.
new_option: minimum target return manager is expected to beat.

original_option: broad market indexes are measures of broad asset class performance (JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index EMBI for emerging market bonds, MSCI World Index for global developed market equities) — well known, readily available, easily understood; style indexes represent investment styles within asset classes; investment style is natural grouping of investment disciplines that has predictive power in explaining future dispersion of returns across portfolios (Brown and Goetzmann 1997); in late 1970s researchers found that stock valuation (P/E) and market cap explained much of stock return variation, leading to style versions of broad indexes (Russell 2000 Value, Russell 1000 Growth).
new_option: broad market indexes are measures of broad asset class performance (JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index EMBI for emerging market bonds, MSCI World Index for global developed market equities) — well known, readily available, easily understood.

original_option: can be constructed to more closely capture the investment decision-making process; building a factor model identifies relative explanatory powers of each factor in portfolio return; examples of factors include Fama-French/Carhart factors; suitable when manager's approach attempts to create alpha by timing exposure to factors (systematic risks).
new_option: can be constructed to more closely capture the investment decision-making process.

original_option: built to more precisely reflect investment discipline of an investment manager; developed through discussions with manager and analysis of past portfolio exposures; benchmark is constructed by selecting securities and weightings consistent with process and client restrictions; if allocation to cash is key component of process, appropriate cash weight incorporated into benchmark; rebalanced on periodic basis to ensure consistent with manager's investment practice; also referred to as strategy benchmarks because they should reflect manager's strategy; particularly appropriate when manager's strategy cannot be closely matched to broad market index or style index; costly to calculate and maintain.
new_option: built to more precisely reflect investment discipline of an investment manager.

original_option: (1) unambiguous — individual securities and weights clearly identifiable; (2) investable — possible to replicate and hold benchmark to earn its return; (3) measurable — possible to measure benchmark return on reasonably frequent and timely basis; (4) appropriate — consistent with manager's investment style or area of expertise; (5) reflective of current investment opinions — manager should be familiar with securities and be able to develop opinion; (6) specified in advance — constructed prior to evaluation period; (7) accountable — manager accepts ownership of benchmark and its securities.
new_option: (1) unambiguous — individual securities and weights clearly identifiable.

original_option: P = M + S + A where P is portfolio return, M is market index return, S is style return (B - M where B is benchmark return), A is active management return (P - B); if manager's portfolio is broad market index where S = 0 and A = 0, portfolio earns broad market return; if benchmark is broad market index and manager has style differences, style return will be lumped together with measured active management component, obscuring true added value.

correlation between active management return A = (P - B) and style return S = (B - M) — a good benchmark should not reflect systematic biases; correlation between A and S should not be statistically different from zero; define E = (P - M) as difference between portfolio and broad market index; when manager's style (S) is in (out of) favor relative to market, expect both benchmark and account to outperform (underperform) the market; a good benchmark will have a statistically significant positive correlation coefficient between S and E.
new_option: correlation between active management return A = (P - B) and style return S = (B - M) — a good benchmark should not reflect systematic biases.

original_option: S = B - M = -5.5% - (-2.8%) = -2.7% (return due to style); A = P - B = -5.3% - (-5.5%) = 0.2% (return due to active management); style is out of favor relative to market by -2.7%.
new_option: S = B - M = -5.5% - (-2.8%) = -2.7% (return due to style).

original_option: benchmark meets criteria of unambiguous (individual securities and weights clearly identifiable), investable (shares freely tradeable), measurable (index returns published daily), reflective of current investment opinions (selected by manager), and specified in advance (not created after fact); does NOT meet criterion of appropriate — liquidity and capitalization constraints imposed by client are not consistent with manner in which manager runs other portfolios managed by firm; manager may choose to be accountable if liquidity/capitalization constraints not expected to interfere with strategy execution.
new_option: benchmark meets criteria of unambiguous (individual securities and weights clearly identifiable), investable (shares freely tradeable), measurable (index returns published daily), reflective of current investment opinions (selected by manager), and specified in advance (not created after fact).

original_option: when benchmarks are misspecified, subsequent performance measurement will be incorrect; both attribution and appraisal analyses will be useless; fundamentally, any further performance attribution against investor's benchmark will also be useless; by using incorrect benchmark, attribution would attempt to explain underperformance rather than true active return.
new_option: when benchmarks are misspecified, subsequent performance measurement will be incorrect.

original_option: sponsor uses MSCI Pacific Index (includes Japan + Australia + New Zealand + Hong Kong SAR + Singapore); MSCI Japan Index more closely represents manager's normal portfolio; Manager return 18.0%, MSCI Pacific 20.0%, MSCI Japan 9.0%; True Active Return = 18.0 - 9.0 = 9.0%; Investor Mismeasured Active Return = 18.0 - 20.0 = -2.0% (True + Misfit = 9.0 + -11.0 = -2.0); manager outperformed when correctly benchmarked but underperformed when measured against wrong benchmark.

'normal portfolio' or 'normal benchmark' is portfolio that most closely represents manager's typical positions in investment universe; manager's 'true' active return equals return minus normal portfolio return; most investors tend to use broad market benchmark for manager evaluation, so manager's active return measured as manager's return minus investor's benchmark return; mismatch between broad market benchmark and manager's 'normal' portfolio is not manager's 'true' active return but 'misfit active return'; using broad market index typically misses manager's style (creates style bias).
new_option: 'normal portfolio' or 'normal benchmark' is portfolio that most closely represents manager's typical positions in investment universe.

original_option: peer group benchmarking particularly susceptible to selection problems; practitioners must select appropriate peers without suggesting to portfolio managers that median peer group performance is the target; peer group benchmarks provide an incentive not to underperform peer group median, often leading to herding around median return; investment decisions of fund manager can be biased by structure of benchmarks chosen; sometimes benchmarks chosen for wrong reasons — underperforming managers have been known to change benchmarks to improve measured excess return which is both inappropriate and unethical.
new_option: peer group benchmarking particularly susceptible to selection problems.

original_option: hedge funds encompass broad range of possible strategies, may have unlimited investment universe, use leverage, sell assets short, take positions in derivatives, opportunistic in choice of strategy; broad market indexes are unsuitable as hedge fund benchmarks; risk-free rate (Treasury yield) plus spread (3%-6%) sometimes advocated for arbitrage strategies; both broad market indexes and risk-free rate will be weakly correlated or uncorrelated with hedge fund returns failing benchmark quality test; hedge fund manager universes from CSFB/Tremont often used but subject to survivorship bias and other limitations.
new_option: hedge funds encompass broad range of possible strategies, may have unlimited investment universe, use leverage, sell assets short, take positions in derivatives, opportunistic in choice of strategy.

original_option: uses cash flow data to replicate the general partner's capital calls and distributions, assuming these same cash flows were invested in the chosen equity index; comparing performance of PME index with net IRR of fund reveals extent of over- or underperformance of PME index relative to public index; several PME methodologies exist, most common being Long-Nickels PME, PME+, Kaplan and Schoar PME, and Direct Alpha PME; important to choose appropriate PME for each private equity fund — poorly chosen PME raises risk of leading investor to incorrect conclusion.
new_option: uses cash flow data to replicate the general partner's capital calls and distributions, assuming these same cash flows were invested in the chosen equity index.
