original_option: commodity benchmarks tend to use indexes based on performance of futures-based commodity investments (Reuters/Jefferies CRB Index, S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index GSCI, Bloomberg Commodity Index BCOM); indexes use futures rather than actual assets; because cost-of-carry model ensures return on fully margined position in futures contract mimics return on underlying spot deliverable, futures contract returns often used as surrogate for cash market performance; challenges include use of derivatives to represent actual commodity assets, varying degrees of leverage among funds, discretionary weighting of exposures; market-cap-weighting cannot be carried over to commodity indexes because every long futures position has corresponding short futures position — market cap of futures contract is always zero.
new_option: commodity benchmarks tend to use indexes based on performance of futures-based commodity investments (Reuters/Jefferies CRB Index, S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index GSCI, Bloomberg Commodity Index BCOM).

original_option: illiquid and almost non-marketable at time of purchase making it very difficult to find suitable benchmarks; if companies' prospects improve, values may go up gradually and liquidity may improve; typically takes relatively long time for strategy to play out — valuing holdings may be challenge; difficult to estimate true market values, stale pricing almost inevitable; possible to use Barclay Distressed Securities Index but constructed from multiple strategies making it difficult to discern whether index is suitable for given investment approach; valuations calculated at random intervals so doesn't correct for valuation issues.
new_option: illiquid and almost non-marketable at time of purchase making it very difficult to find suitable benchmarks.

original_option: modification of Sharpe ratio that penalizes only those returns lower than user-specified return (MAR); ex ante Sortino = [E(r_p) - r_T] / σ_D; instead of standard deviation, uses target semi-standard deviation σ_D = sqrt(Σ min(r_t - r_T, 0)² / N); MAR (minimum acceptable return) sometimes referred to as target rate of return; Sortino penalizes managers only for 'harmful' volatility — measure of return per unit of downside risk.
new_option: modification of Sharpe ratio that penalizes only those returns lower than user-specified return (MAR).

original_option: AR = α / σ_ε; annualized alpha divided by annualized residual risk (standard deviation of ε); both alpha and residual risk computed from factor regression; measures reward of active management relative to risk of active management; also referred to as Treynor-Black ratio; first introduced by Treynor and Black (1973); example: Portfolio Y returned 9%, market 8%, beta 1.40, risk-free 3%, SD portfolio 20%, SD market 12% — Jensen's alpha = 9% - [3% + 1.40(8% - 3%)] = -1%; non-systematic risk σε² = 0.20² - 1.40² × 0.12² = 0.011776 → σε = 10.85%; AR = -0.01/0.1085 ≈ -0.0922.

UC(m,B,t) = R(m,t)/R(B,t) if R(B,t) ≥ 0; DC(m,B,t) = R(m,t)/R(B,t) if R(B,t) < 0; upside capture greater (less) than 100% generally suggests outperformance (underperformance); downside capture less (greater) than 100% generally suggests outperformance (underperformance); when manager and benchmark returns are opposite sign, ratio negative (manager 1% return when market down 1% → downside capture -100%).
new_option: UC(m,B,t) = R(m,t)/R(B,t) if R(B,t) ≥ 0.

original_option: CR = UC/DC; measures asymmetry of return (like bond convexity and option gamma); capture ratio > 1 indicates positive asymmetry or convex return profile; capture ratio < 1 indicates negative asymmetry or concave return profile; concave return profile resembles downward-facing bowl; convex return profile resembles upward-facing bowl; as benchmark returns increase, portfolio returns increase at decreasing rate for concave and increasing rate for convex.
new_option: CR = UC/DC; measures asymmetry of return (like bond convexity and option gamma).

original_option: drawdown measured as cumulative peak-to-trough loss during a continuous period; Maximum DD(m,t) = min([V(m,t) - V(m,t*)] / V(m,t*), 0) where V(m,t*) is peak portfolio value; drawdown duration is total time from start of drawdown until cumulative drawdown recovers to zero; segmented into drawdown phase (start to trough) and recovery phase (trough-to-zero cumulative return); S&P 500 COVID example: drawdown 0% until January 2020 (-0.04%), worsening to maximum -19.6% in March 2020, positive returns Apr-Jul 2020 reverse drawdown, total duration 7 months with 4-month recovery.
new_option: drawdown measured as cumulative peak-to-trough loss during a continuous period.

original_option: the use of standard deviation as a measure of risk assumes investors are indifferent between upside and downside volatility; for investor looking for potentially high-rewarding investment, volatility on upside is not necessarily a negative; risk-averse investors concerned about preservation of capital are most concerned with downside risk; Sortino ratio addresses this by using downside deviation.
new_option: the use of standard deviation as a measure of risk assumes investors are indifferent between upside and downside volatility.

original_option: depends on whether systematic risk or total risk is most appropriate in evaluating performance; because of reliance on beta, Treynor ratio shows how fund performed in relation not to its own volatility but to volatility it would bring to well-diversified portfolio; ranking based on Treynor ratio most useful if portfolios whose performance is being evaluated are being combined in broader fully diversified portfolio; ratio most informative when portfolios being evaluated are compared with same benchmark index; unlike Sharpe, requires explicit choice of market benchmark against which to measure systematic risk.
new_option: depends on whether systematic risk or total risk is most appropriate in evaluating performance.

original_option: Sortino ratio formula is not a risk premium; it is the return manager generates that is greater than what is minimally acceptable to investor; penalizes manager when portfolio return is lower than MAR; most relevant when one of investor's primary objectives is capital preservation; Sharpe ratio has been much more widely used partly because of comfort level with standard deviation; cross-sectional comparisons of Sortino ratios are difficult to make applicable to every investor because MAR is investor-specific.
new_option: Sortino ratio formula is not a risk premium.

original_option: it takes proportionally larger gains to recover from increasingly large losses; portfolio decline of 50% must be followed by gain of 100% to return to previous value; asymmetry arises from basis drift, from change in denominator when calculating returns, or from practical problem of recovering from smaller asset base after large loss; asymmetrical return profile or avoiding large drawdowns particularly during periods when market is not trending strongly upward can result in higher risk-adjusted returns.
new_option: it takes proportionally larger gains to recover from increasingly large losses.

original_option: investment manager's record for any specific period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and bad luck (unanticipated bad developments); paradox of skill — as people become more knowledgeable about an activity, difference between worst and best performers becomes narrower; ever-increasing aggregate skill level of investment managers may lead to narrower performance differentials and greater likelihood that differentials can be explained by luck; financial market returns have large element of randomness; hypothesis that manager's underlying mean return exceeds benchmark may require many years of observations to confirm with reasonably high degree of confidence.
new_option: investment manager's record for any specific period will reflect good luck (unanticipated good developments) and bad luck (unanticipated bad developments).

original_option: ability of portfolio manager to add value on risk-adjusted basis through investment analysis and insights; in everyday language, typically viewed as ability to 'beat the market' or assigned benchmark with some consistency; skill in any profession can be thought of as ability to influence outcomes in desired directions.
new_option: ability of portfolio manager to add value on risk-adjusted basis through investment analysis and insights.

original_option: 5-year performance 9.42% vs benchmark 9.25% = 17 bps outperformance; sample attribution against MSCI Pacific shows allocation lost 43 bps (overweighted Australia lost 24 bps because Australia underperformed 4.10% vs 9.25%; underweighted Japan lost 21 bps because Japan outperformed 12.4% vs 9.25%); stock selection added 59 bps (Japanese stocks +47 bps, Australian stocks +31 bps, Hong Kong SAR -18 bps); manager is good stock picker especially Japan/Australia but has not been successful in choosing markets to allocate assets.
new_option: 5-year performance 9.42% vs benchmark 9.25% = 17 bps outperformance.

original_option: evaluation of investment manager skill requires use of broad range of analysis tools with fundamental understanding of how tools work, how they complement each other, and their specific limitations; appraisal ratios must be used with care noting assumptions of each ratio and affording appropriateness to measured investment process, risk tolerance, and investor time horizon; although appraisal ratios help identify manager skill (as opposed to luck), often based on investment return data which are often limited and subject to error.
new_option: evaluation of investment manager skill requires use of broad range of analysis tools with fundamental understanding of how tools work, how they complement each other, and their specific limitations.

original_option: investigation and analysis in support of an investment action, decision, or recommendation; focus on understanding how investment results were achieved and assessing likelihood that investment process that generated returns will produce superior or at least satisfactory investment results going forward; also entails evaluation of firm's integrity, operations, and personnel; involves both quantitative and qualitative analysis.
new_option: investigation and analysis in support of an investment action, decision, or recommendation.

original_option: (1) the universe (defining feasible set of managers, suitability, style, active vs passive); (2) quantitative analysis of the manager's performance track record (attribution and appraisal, capture ratio, drawdown); (3) qualitative analysis of the manager's investment process (investment due diligence + operational due diligence).
new_option: (1) the universe (defining feasible set of managers, suitability, style, active vs passive).

original_option: process and procedure (is back office strong, safeguarding assets, able to issue accurate reports in timely manner); firm (is firm profitable with healthy culture and likely to remain in business); investment vehicle (is vehicle suitable for portfolio need); terms (acceptable and appropriate for strategy and vehicle); monitoring (does manager continue to be best fit).
new_option: process and procedure (is back office strong, safeguarding assets, able to issue accurate reports in timely manner).

original_option: philosophy (what market inefficiency does manager seek to exploit); process (is investment process capable of exploiting this inefficiency); people (do investment personnel possess expertise and experience necessary to effectively implement investment process); portfolio (is portfolio construction consistent with stated investment philosophy and process).
new_option: philosophy (what market inefficiency does manager seek to exploit).

original_option: a consistent, robust investment process will generate a similar return distribution relative to risk factors through time, assuming underlying dynamics of the market have not dramatically changed; one important goal of manager due diligence is to understand whether manager's investment process, people, and portfolio construction satisfy this assumption — that is, will investment process generate expected return from expected sources.
new_option: a consistent, robust investment process will generate a similar return distribution relative to risk factors through time, assuming underlying dynamics of the market have not dramatically changed.

original_option: process begins by defining universe of feasible managers that potentially satisfy identified portfolio need; objective is to reduce manager universe to manageable size relative to resources and time available to evaluate it; involves balancing risks of too narrow a search (excluding interesting managers) and too broad a search (little gain in reducing list); IPS and reason for manager search largely determine universe of managers considered and benchmark against which they are compared.
new_option: process begins by defining universe of feasible managers that potentially satisfy identified portfolio need.

original_option: new search based on strategic or tactical view (adding new strategy or risk exposure) will examine broad universe of comparable managers and select best within universe; adding manager to increase capacity or diversification within strategy already held will look for complement to current holdings; replacing single manager in particular strategy will look for best manager within strategy universe; IPS in part determines what relative terms 'best,' 'complement,' and 'cost/benefit' mean.
new_option: new search based on strategic or tactical view (adding new strategy or risk exposure) will examine broad universe of comparable managers and select best within universe.

original_option: hiring or retaining a manager who subsequently underperforms expectations; rejecting the null hypothesis of no skill when it is correct; a false positive.
new_option: hiring or retaining a manager who subsequently underperforms expectations.

original_option: not hiring or firing a manager who subsequently outperforms, or performs in line with, expectations; not rejecting the null hypothesis when it is incorrect; a false negative.
new_option: not hiring or firing a manager who subsequently outperforms, or performs in line with, expectations.

original_option: typically starts with the null hypothesis (hypothesis assumed to be true until demonstrated otherwise) that the manager is NOT skillful; if this null is rejected when correct, Type I error; if this null is not rejected when incorrect, Type II error.
new_option: typically starts with the null hypothesis (hypothesis assumed to be true until demonstrated otherwise) that the manager is NOT skillful.

original_option: psychologically people seek to avoid feelings of regret — Type I errors are mistaken rejections whereas Type II errors are failures to detect true relationship; Type I errors create explicit costs, Type II errors create opportunity costs — individuals put less weight on opportunity costs; Type I errors relatively straightforward to measure and often directly linked to decision maker's compensation; Type I errors more transparent to investors so entail regret plus pain of explaining to investor; Type II errors less transparent unless investor tracks fired managers.
new_option: psychologically people seek to avoid feelings of regret — Type I errors are mistaken rejections whereas Type II errors are failures to detect true relationship.

original_option: cost driven by size, shape, mean, and dispersion of return distributions of skilled and unskilled managers within universe; smaller the difference in sample size and distribution mean and wider dispersion of distributions, smaller expected cost of Type I or Type II error; more efficient markets likely exhibit smaller differences in distributions of skilled and unskilled managers indicating lower opportunity cost of retaining and lower cost of hiring an unskilled manager.
new_option: cost driven by size, shape, mean, and dispersion of return distributions of skilled and unskilled managers within universe.

original_option: consistent pattern of Type II errors can highlight weaknesses in manager selection process; approach is to monitor not only managers currently held but also managers evaluated and not hired as well as managers that were fired; goal is to determine identifiable factors that differentiate managers hired and not hired, factors driving decision to retain or fire, added value of decisions to retain or fire; objective is to avoid decisions based on short-term performance (trend following) and identify behavioral biases (regret, loss aversion).
new_option: consistent pattern of Type II errors can highlight weaknesses in manager selection process.

original_option: cost of Type I errors is holding a manager without skill; cost of Type II errors is not holding managers with skill; less distinct distribution of skilled managers from unskilled managers → lower opportunity cost of retaining and cost of hiring unskilled manager; smaller perceived difference between distributions → lower cost and incentive to fire manager; a study of institutional plan sponsor allocation decisions found investment products receiving contributions subsequently underperformed products experiencing withdrawals — study estimated more than $170 billion was lost during period examined (Stewart, Neumann, Knittel, and Heisler 2009).
new_option: cost of Type I errors is holding a manager without skill.

original_option: important component of performance appraisal and manager selection; understanding manager's risk exposures relative to benchmark and how they evolve over time; helps define universe of potential managers and monitoring of selected managers; manager's self-reported risk exposures (portfolio concentration, industry exposure, capitalization exposure) are starting point; results of RBSA and HBSA should be consistent with manager's philosophy and investment process — if not, process might not be repeatable or might be implemented inconsistently.
new_option: important component of performance appraisal and manager selection.

original_option: meaningful (risks reported must represent important sources of performance return and risk); accurate (reported values must reflect manager's actual risk exposures); consistent (methodology must allow for comparison over time and across multiple managers); timely (report must be available in timely manner so useful for making informed investment decisions).
new_option: meaningful (risks reported must represent important sources of performance return and risk).

original_option: top-down approach that involves estimating portfolio's sensitivities to security market indexes representing a range of distinct factors; adds analytical step of estimating risk factors as opposed to using third-party or self-reported style categorization; straightforward, typically does not require large amount of additional or difficult to acquire data; identifies important drivers of return and risk factors for period analyzed; can be estimated even for complicated strategies; comparable across managers and through time; use of returns data provides objective style check not subject to window dressing; can be run immediately after data available.
new_option: top-down approach that involves estimating portfolio's sensitivities to security market indexes representing a range of distinct factors.

original_option: RBSA is imprecise tool; accuracy may be compromised because RBSA effectively attributes performance to unchanging average portfolio during period; limits ability to identify impact of dynamic investment decisions and may distort decomposition across sources of added value; portfolio being analyzed might not reflect current or future portfolio exposures; if portfolio contains illiquid securities, stale prices may understate risk exposure of strategy — particular problem for PE and VC managers.
new_option: RBSA is imprecise tool; accuracy may be compromised because RBSA effectively attributes performance to unchanging average portfolio during period.

original_option: bottom-up approach that estimates risk exposures from actual securities held in portfolio at a point in time; allows for estimation of current risk factors; similar to RBSA identifies all important drivers of return and risk factors, comparable across managers and through time, provides accurate view of manager's risk exposures (although potentially subject to window dressing), can be estimated immediately after data become available.
new_option: bottom-up approach that estimates risk exposures from actual securities held in portfolio at a point in time.

original_option: computational effort increases with complexity of strategy and depends on timing and degree of transparency provided by manager; challenging for hedge fund, PE, and VC managers averse to or unable to provide position-level pricing; even with mutual funds, necessary transparency may come with time lag; usefulness may be compromised because portfolio reflects snapshot in time and might not reflect portfolio going forward particularly for high-turnover strategies; some factors difficult to estimate if strategy is complex; typically easier with equity strategies particularly ETFs (holdings published daily); if portfolio has illiquid securities, stale pricing may underestimate risk exposure.
new_option: computational effort increases with complexity of strategy and depends on timing and degree of transparency provided by manager.

original_option: results of RBSA and HBSA should be tracked over time in order to ascertain if risk trends or exposures are out of line with expectations or manager's stated style; deviations may signal issues such as style drift are developing; style analysis is most useful with strategies that hold publicly traded securities where pricing is frequent; can be applied to other strategies (hedge funds and PE) but insights from style analysis of such strategies more likely to be used for designing additional lines of inquiry in due diligence rather than for confirmation of investment process.
new_option: results of RBSA and HBSA should be tracked over time in order to ascertain if risk trends or exposures are out of line with expectations or manager's stated style.

original_option: large losses require proportionally greater gains to reverse or offset; manager that experiences larger drawdowns may be less suitable for investor closer to end of investment horizon; capture ratio helps assess manager suitability relative to investor's IPS especially in relation to investor's time horizon and risk tolerance.
new_option: large losses require proportionally greater gains to reverse or offset.

original_option: (1) UC measures capture when benchmark return is positive; UC greater than 100% suggests outperformance relative to benchmark; (2) DC measures capture when benchmark return is negative; DC less than 100% generally suggests outperformance relative to benchmark; (3) CR = UC / DC, measures asymmetry of return; (4) drawdown is cumulative peak-to-trough loss during a particular continuous period; drawdown duration is total time from start of drawdown until cumulative drawdown recovers to zero.
new_option: (1) UC measures capture when benchmark return is positive.

original_option: long-only: UC 100%, DC 100%, ratio 1.0; positive asymmetry: UC 75%, DC 25%, ratio 3.0; low beta: UC 50%, DC 50%, ratio 1.0; negative asymmetry: UC 25%, DC 75%, ratio 0.3; based on realized monthly returns Jan 2000 to Dec 2013 (encompasses tech bubble burst of early 2000s and extreme drawdown of Global Financial Crisis in 2008-2009).
new_option: long-only: UC 100%, DC 100%, ratio 1.0.

original_option: positive asymmetry profile is allocated 75% to S&P 500 for months when S&P 500 return is positive and 25% when S&P 500 return is negative; remainder allocated to 90-day T-bills; results in UC 75%, DC 25%, capture ratio 3.0 (convex return profile).
new_option: positive asymmetry profile is allocated 75% to S&P 500 for months when S&P 500 return is positive and 25% when S&P 500 return is negative.

original_option: long-only profile is 100% allocated to S&P 500 throughout period (UC 100%, DC 100%, CR 1.0); low-beta profile is allocated 50% to S&P 500 throughout period (UC 50%, DC 50%, CR 1.0); both have capture ratio of 1.0 (symmetric return profile) but low-beta has lower absolute return and lower absolute risk; both experience proportional participation in market moves.
new_option: long-only profile is 100% allocated to S&P 500 throughout period (UC 100%, DC 100%, CR 1.0).

original_option: profiles chosen to illustrate potential effects of capture ratio and drawdown on return performance and highlight why understanding capture ratio and drawdown is important for manager selection; asymmetrical return profile or avoiding large drawdowns particularly during periods when market is not trending strongly upward can result in higher risk-adjusted returns; time period 2000-2013 chosen specifically because it encompasses extreme drawdown of 2008-2009.
new_option: profiles chosen to illustrate potential effects of capture ratio and drawdown on return performance and highlight why understanding capture ratio and drawdown is important for manager selection.

original_option: quadrants based on tracking risk (high vs low) and diversification (high vs low): sector rotation strategies (high tracking risk, low diversification); concentrated stock pickers (high tracking risk, low diversification of stock picks); closet indexer (low tracking risk despite claiming to be active); diversified stock pickers (low tracking risk from many small active bets).
new_option: quadrants based on tracking risk (high vs low) and diversification (high vs low): sector rotation strategies (high tracking risk, low diversification).

original_option: foundation of the investment process; every investment strategy is based on set of assumptions about factors that drive performance and manager's beliefs about ability to successfully exploit these sources of return; investment manager should have clear and concise investment philosophy; every manager makes assumptions about market efficiency including degree and time frame; index-based strategies assume markets are sufficiently efficient and active management cannot add value after transaction costs and fees.
new_option: foundation of the investment process.

original_option: behavioral inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by actions of other market participants, usually associated with biases such as trend following or loss aversion; these inefficiencies are temporary, lasting long enough for manager to identify and exploit them before market price and perceived intrinsic value converge; structural inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by external or internal rules and regulations; these inefficiencies can be long lived and assume continuation of rules and regulations rather than convergence.
new_option: behavioral inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by actions of other market participants, usually associated with biases such as trend following or loss aversion.

original_option: can manager clearly and consistently articulate investment philosophy?; are assumptions credible and consistent?; how has philosophy developed over time (ideally unchanged, changes ideally reasonable responses to changing market conditions rather than ad hoc reactions)?; are return sources linked to credible and consistent inefficiencies (informational advantage, behavioral, structural)?
new_option: can manager clearly and consistently articulate investment philosophy?.

original_option: level of assets strategy or opportunity can absorb without dilution of returns, number of opportunities or securities available, ability to transact in timely manner at or near market price (liquidity); key questions: does inefficiency provide sufficient frequency of opportunity and level of return to cover transaction costs and fees (if so, does this require leverage)?; does inefficiency provide repeatable source of return?; is inefficiency sustainable (function of market's depth and liquidity as well as how much capital allocated to inefficiency — more well-known inefficiency more likely to be arbitraged away).
new_option: level of assets strategy or opportunity can absorb without dilution of returns, number of opportunities or securities available, ability to transact in timely manner at or near market price (liquidity).

original_option: investment signal is data point or fact that can be observed early enough to implement as investment position; efficient market hypothesis posits key to exploiting inefficiencies is to have information that is: (1) unique (does strategy rely on unique information?); (2) timely (does strategy possess information timing advantage?); (3) interpreted differently (does manager possess unique way of interpreting information?).
new_option: investment signal is data point or fact that can be observed early enough to implement as investment position.

original_option: (1) signal creation (idea generation); (2) signal capture (idea implementation); (3) portfolio construction; (4) portfolio monitoring.
new_option: (1) signal creation (idea generation).

original_option: how are portfolio allocations set and adjusted (allocation process should be consistent with investment philosophy and process; e.g., turnover should agree with frequency of signals, payoff horizon of signals, securities' liquidity)?; do portfolio allocations incorporate manager's conviction?; how have portfolio characteristics changed with asset growth?; does portfolio use stop-losses to manage risk (hard: positions automatically sold; soft: positions evaluated when threshold reached)?; what types of securities are used?
new_option: how are portfolio allocations set and adjusted (allocation process should be consistent with investment philosophy and process.

original_option: does investment team have sufficient expertise and experience to effectively execute investment process (especially instructive to see how manager responded to stressed markets and poor performance)?; does team have sufficient depth?; what is level of key person risk (overreliance on individual whose departure would negatively affect strategy's performance)?; what agreements (non-compete) and incentives (ownership, bonus, pay) exist to retain and attract key employees?; what has been turnover of firm personnel (high turnover risks loss of institutional knowledge)?
new_option: does investment team have sufficient expertise and experience to effectively execute investment process (especially instructive to see how manager responded to stressed markets and poor performance)?.

original_option: in 2021 team-managed mutual funds accounted for 64% of all US active mutual funds (Karagiannidis and Booth 2022); primary motivation is idea that 'two heads are better than one' — application of number of individuals each with different skills and experiences can provide for more effective decision making; secondary motivation is to mitigate key person risk; research is mixed on whether team-managed outperform individual-managed funds — several studies find underperformance, several find opposite, some find no difference; there is nothing magical about a team, so managers should focus on creating effective structure and culture.
new_option: in 2021 team-managed mutual funds accounted for 64% of all US active mutual funds (Karagiannidis and Booth 2022).

original_option: occurs when a team minimizes conflict and dissent in reaching and maintaining a consensus; pursuit of harmony overrides individual expression and may encourage individuals to withhold information and perspectives from the team; common symptoms are closed-mindedness and confirmation bias, where fresh sources of information, different interpretations, and dissent are ignored or minimized; example: portfolio management team may become convinced recession is imminent based on inverted yield curve and falling commodity prices and seek to position defensively — team must remain open to possibility it isn't correct.
new_option: occurs when a team minimizes conflict and dissent in reaching and maintaining a consensus.

original_option: involves groups deferring to a group member that is a subject matter expert or in a position of authority (e.g., the senior-most group member); example: research analyst recommends semiconductor equipment stock to team of three portfolio managers, one of whom is former technology research analyst and votes not to buy — the other two vote the same way, deferring to subject matter expertise of the other manager; while this may be right call, it undermines reason for team in the first place: synthesis of diverse perspectives and skills.
new_option: involves groups deferring to a group member that is a subject matter expert or in a position of authority (e.g., the senior-most group member).

original_option: well-known phenomenon in which disproportionate attention is given to trivial issues at expense of important but harder-to-grasp or contested topics; also known as Parkinson's law of triviality or colloquially 'bike-shedding' — named after C. Northcote Parkinson's 1957 story of hypothetical committee organized to approve plans for nuclear power plant that spent more time discussing design of bicycle storage shed outside plant than design of nuclear reactor; survey of 120+ investment committees revealed committees spent great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant at expense of important issues (asset allocation, capital market expectations, manager selection).
new_option: well-known phenomenon in which disproportionate attention is given to trivial issues at expense of important but harder-to-grasp or contested topics.

original_option: team size between three and five is optimal (larger teams introduce coordination issues and increase odds of groupthink per Patel and Sarkissian 2017); using secret ballot may be preferable to open deliberation because reduces groupthink and authority bias; meeting agendas should be agreed on in advance with most important topics covered first and allotted longest time for discussion (to avoid complexity aversion and availability bias); diverse investment teams (in terms of social and cognitive categories) reduce groupthink and errors — CFA Institute long advocated for DEI in investment industry.
new_option: team size between three and five is optimal (larger teams introduce coordination issues and increase odds of groupthink per Patel and Sarkissian 2017).

original_option: research suggests team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility; members of diverse teams may devote more effort to tasks because less concerned with socially 'fitting in' and conflict avoidance which is common in homogeneous groups.
new_option: research suggests team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility.

original_option: survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank tasks from most to least important and then to keep track of how they actually allocated their time; survey revealed committees spent great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant at expense of important and more difficult issues such as asset allocation, capital market expectations, and manager selection (Payne and Wood 2002).
new_option: survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank tasks from most to least important and then to keep track of how they actually allocated their time.

original_option: individual separate accounts and pooled (or commingled) vehicles; separate accounts offer additional control, customization, tax efficiency, reporting, and transparency advantages but these come at higher cost; in a pooled or commingled vehicle, money from multiple investors is held as a single portfolio and managed without potential customization for any investor — includes open-end funds, closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, exchange-traded notes, and hedge funds.
new_option: individual separate accounts and pooled (or commingled) vehicles.

original_option: ownership (investor owns individual securities directly, additional safety in liquidity event, decisions not influenced by redemption demand of other investors, clear legal ownership for recovery in bankruptcy); customization (allows investor to potentially express individual constraints or preferences within portfolio); tax efficiency (potentially improved tax efficiency — in US investors pay taxes only on realized capital gains); transparency (real-time position-level detail to investor providing complete transparency and accurate attribution).
new_option: ownership (investor owns individual securities directly, additional safety in liquidity event, decisions not influenced by redemption demand of other investors, clear legal ownership for recovery in bankruptcy).

original_option: cost (SMAs represent additional operational burden on manager translating into higher costs; SMAs do not scale as easily as pooled vehicles — with SMA new account must be established for each investor; likely to face higher transaction costs to degree trades cannot be aggregated); tracking risk (customization creates tracking risk relative to benchmark which can confuse attribution); investor behavior (transparency combined with control and customization allows for potential micromanagement by investor including performance chasing, familiarity bias, loss aversion).
new_option: cost (SMAs represent additional operational burden on manager translating into higher costs.

original_option: is vehicle structure consistent with investment process?; does manager have operational infrastructure necessary to manage SMA?; is there benefit to holding securities in separate account (if so, are benefits sufficient to compensate for additional costs)?; is tax efficiency important objective of IPS?; are there concerns that available transparency and ability to customize will result in decisions by investor that do not add value?
new_option: is vehicle structure consistent with investment process?.

original_option: most liquid vehicles are closed-end funds and ETFs — as listed securities can be bought and sold intra-day and price received depends on trading volume and depth of fund; obvious advantage is ease of trading although can be some price uncertainty for less liquid funds particularly when trying to buy or sell large number of shares; open-end funds are slightly less liquid providing daily liquidity but also price certainty (shares bought and sold at end-of-day NAV).
new_option: most liquid vehicles are closed-end funds and ETFs — as listed securities can be bought and sold intra-day and price received depends on trading volume and depth of fund.

original_option: four basic features: (1) redemption frequency (how often investor can withdraw capital from fund); (2) notification period (how far in advance of redemption investors must tell fund of intention to redeem); (3) lockup (initial period after making investment during which investors cannot redeem holding — hard lock allows no redemptions, soft lock charges fee paid into fund for redemptions; mutual fund redemption fee is equivalent to hedge fund soft lock); (4) gates (limit amount of fund assets or investor assets that can be redeemed at one redemption date).
new_option: four basic features: (1) redemption frequency (how often investor can withdraw capital from fund).

original_option: PE and VC funds provide least liquidity; investors are contractually obligated to contribute specific amounts (capital calls) during investment phase and then receive distributions and capital as investments are harvested during remaining term of fund; typical investment phase is 5 years; typical life of a fund is 10 years with option to extend term for two 1-year periods; disadvantage of partnership liquidity is reduced flexibility to adjust allocations; advantage is such terms lock up capital for longer horizons, allowing funds to take long-term views and hold less liquid securities (start-ups, buyouts, turnarounds, real estate, natural resources) with reduced risk of having to sell portfolio holdings at inopportune times.
new_option: PE and VC funds provide least liquidity.

original_option: because SMA assets are held in investor's name, securities in portfolio can be sold at any time; as a result SMA's liquidity will depend on liquidity of securities held; SMA holding listed large-cap stocks will likely be highly liquid, whereas investor in SMA that holds unlisted or illiquid securities will have to accept a discount when selling.
new_option: because SMA assets are held in investor's name, securities in portfolio can be sold at any time.

original_option: prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement are in essence contract between investor and manager, outlining each party's rights and responsibilities; documents cover numerous topics but liquidity and fees are focus of evaluation; objective of decision maker is to determine whether liquidity and fee structure make manager suitable for investor's needs and 'best' manager for expressing particular portfolio need.
new_option: prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement are in essence contract between investor and manager, outlining each party's rights and responsibilities.

original_option: disadvantage of partnership liquidity terms is reduced flexibility to adjust portfolio allocations in light of changing market conditions or investor circumstances and reduced ability to meet unexpected liquidity needs; advantage of such terms is they do lock up capital for longer horizons, allowing funds to take long-term views and hold less liquid securities (start-ups, buyouts, turnarounds, real estate, natural resources) with reduced risk of having to sell portfolio holdings at inopportune times; additional advantage apparent during 2008 financial crisis: limited liquidity imposes long horizon view on investors, reducing or removing their ability to overreact.
new_option: disadvantage of partnership liquidity terms is reduced flexibility to adjust portfolio allocations in light of changing market conditions or investor circumstances and reduced ability to meet unexpected liquidity needs.

original_option: also called 'ad valorem fees' (from Latin for 'according to value'); result from applying stated percentage rates to assets under management; reward managers who attract and retain assets, generate added value, and experience benefits from rising markets; managers primarily grow assets through skillful investing, hard work, and effective marketing; also results partly from luck especially in short term; decline in ad valorem percentages as assets grow helps reduce fee impact on investors from rising markets but does not eliminate it.
new_option: also called 'ad valorem fees' (from Latin for 'according to value').

original_option: once manager's assets are large, might not want to risk losing them; assets typically 'sticky' — once investors allocate to manager, manager often does not need to generate same level of returns to retain assets as they did to attract them; to motivate managers to work harder or discourage them from closet indexing, incentive fee determined by future performance may be useful.
new_option: once manager's assets are large, might not want to risk losing them.

original_option: average asset-weighted expense ratios incurred by mutual fund investors have fallen substantially; in 2000 equity mutual fund investors incurred expense ratios of 0.99% on average or 99 cents for every $100 invested; by 2021 that average had fallen to 0.47%, a decline of 53%; hybrid fell 0.89% (2000) to 0.57% (2021), 36% reduction; bond fell 0.76% to 0.39%, 49% decline; average expense ratio of actively managed equity mutual funds declined from 1.06% to 0.68%; index-based equity and bond funds declined from 27 bps and 21 bps in 2000 respectively to 6 bps each in 2021.
new_option: average asset-weighted expense ratios incurred by mutual fund investors have fallen substantially.

original_option: fee structures can influence which managers will be willing to accept particular investment mandate and can strongly affect manager behavior; economic theory suggests principal-agent problem is complicated by fact that agent's skills and actions are not fully visible to principal; although principals control asset availability, agents control both expenditure of effort and portfolio risk; agent and principal may have different preferences (different time horizons, agents might not view losses same way principals do); total performance to some extent beyond control of either party; managers motivated to work hard even without incentive fees to retain current clients and expand client base — incentives useful to help ensure managers routinely act in clients' best interest.
new_option: fee structures can influence which managers will be willing to accept particular investment mandate and can strongly affect manager behavior.

original_option: mutual fund managers charge fees based on fund's assets under management; institutional managers frequently offer declining percentage fees on increasing account sizes for separate or commingled pool accounts; institutional accounts frequently specify minimum account sizes or minimum dollar fees; fixed-percentage fees facilitate managers' and investors' planning for future cash flows whereas dollar fees are subject to variability of asset values; individual investor does not benefit from investing more money in mutual fund unless extra money qualifies investor for lower-fee fund class.
new_option: mutual fund managers charge fees based on fund's assets under management.

original_option: expenses are paid for certain whereas added value of active strategy compared with passive strategy is uncertain; suppose active strategy expected to generate gross return 2% greater than passive but cost of active is 2% greater than passive — risk-averse investor would likely prefer passive strategy (expected net return of strategies is same but uncertainty of outperformance would be unappealing); riskier the active strategy, greater the return volatility and greater volatility of added value relative to passive; added value of active strategy has to be sufficiently large and certain to justify higher cost of the strategy.
new_option: expenses are paid for certain whereas added value of active strategy compared with passive strategy is uncertain.

original_option: (1) symmetrical structure — manager is fully exposed to both downside and upside; Computed fee = Base + Sharing of performance; (2) bonus structure — manager is not fully exposed to downside but is fully exposed to upside; Computed fee = Higher of either Base or Base plus sharing of positive performance; (3) bonus structure — manager is not fully exposed to either downside or upside; Computed fee = Higher of Base or Base plus sharing of performance, to a limit.
new_option: (1) symmetrical structure — manager is fully exposed to both downside and upside.

original_option: common for hedge funds and less common for long-only strategies; determined by portfolio returns and designed to reward managers with share of return for their skill in creating value; performance can be calculated using either total or relative return, and return shared can be percentage of total performance or performance net of a base or fixed fee; performance fees paid annually or in some cases less frequently.
new_option: common for hedge funds and less common for long-only strategies.

original_option: performance fees may include maximum and high-water mark (or clawback) features that protect investors from situations such as paying for current positive performance before negative effects of prior underperformance have been offset; private equity, hedge fund, and real estate partnerships commonly earn performance fees on total returns and typically do not limit amount of performance fee; hedge funds commonly include high-water mark features.
new_option: performance fees may include maximum and high-water mark (or clawback) features that protect investors from situations such as paying for current positive performance before negative effects of prior underperformance have been offset.

original_option: for private equity partnerships, base fees are commonly applied to committed (not just invested) capital; performance fees earned as profits are realized and invested capital is returned to investors; common provision that helps protect PE limited partners (investors) is requirement that limited partners receive their principal and share of profits before performance fees are distributed to general partner (manager).
new_option: for private equity partnerships, base fees are commonly applied to committed (not just invested) capital.

original_option: bonus-style fees are close equivalent of manager's call option on share of active return, for which base fee is strike price; option payoff modified by maximum fee feature; graph illustrates three fee components: base fee, plus a long call option on active return with strike price equal to minimum (base) fee, minus another (less valuable) call option with strike price equal to maximum fee; based on option pricing theory, higher volatility leads to higher option value, which encourages managers to assume higher portfolio risk — this behavior has been observed in the marketplace.
new_option: bonus-style fees are close equivalent of manager's call option on share of active return, for which base fee is strike price.

original_option: investors and managers may have different incentives when performance-based fees are used; according to utility maximization model, fully symmetric fees in which manager is fully exposed to downside tend to yield closer alignment in risk and effort than bonus-style fees; understandably, symmetrical fee structures are unpopular with managers because of their impact on bankruptcy risk.
new_option: investors and managers may have different incentives when performance-based fees are used.

original_option: performance-based fee structures may lead to misestimates of portfolio risk; convert symmetrical gross active return distributions into asymmetrical net active return distributions, reducing variability on upside but not the downside; as a result, single standard deviation calculated on return series that incorporates active returns above and below the base fee can lead to underestimation of downside risk; management fee lowers level of realized return without affecting standard deviation, whereas performance fee has added effect of lowering realized standard deviation.
new_option: performance-based fee structures may lead to misestimates of portfolio risk.

original_option: standard fee 0.50%; base fee 0.25%; sharing 20% (on active return, beyond base fee); breakeven active return 1.50%; maximum annual fee 0.75%; specifies base fee below which computed fee can never fall (manager protected against sharing for performance below 25 bps); to make result symmetrical around commonplace 50 bps fee, manager does not share in active performance beyond 2.75%.
new_option: standard fee 0.50%; base fee 0.25%.

original_option: active return ≤ 0.25%: billed fee 0.25%, net active return ≤ 0.00%; active return 1.00%: billed fee 0.40%, net active return 0.60%; active return 1.50%: billed fee 0.50%, net active return 1.00%; active return 2.00%: billed fee 0.60%, net active return 1.40%; active return ≥ 2.75%: billed fee 0.75%, net active return ≥ 2.00%.
new_option: active return ≤ 0.25%: billed fee 0.25%, net active return ≤ 0.00%.

original_option: if investment outcomes result from mix of skill and luck (probability distribution around positive mean alpha), then performance fees constitute risk sharing; fee structures must be designed carefully to avoid favoring one party over other; performance-based fees work to align interests of managers and investors because both parties share in investment results; investors benefit by paying performance-based fees rather than standard fees when active returns are low; managers may work harder to earn performance-based fees inspiring term 'incentive based.'
new_option: if investment outcomes result from mix of skill and luck (probability distribution around positive mean alpha), then performance fees constitute risk sharing.

original_option: empirical evidence suggests correlation between performance-based fees and higher alphas (also lower fees) for mutual funds and higher risk-adjusted returns for hedge funds (Elton, Gruber, and Blake 2003; Ackermann, McEnally, and Ravenscraft 1999); asset managers may consider performance-based fees attractive because provide opportunity to enhance profits on upside and ensure guaranteed although perhaps minimal streams of revenue from base fees when performance is poor.
new_option: empirical evidence suggests correlation between performance-based fees and higher alphas (also lower fees) for mutual funds and higher risk-adjusted returns for hedge funds (Elton, Gruber, and Blake 2003.

original_option: investors must pay base fees even when managers underperform; management firm revenues decline when cash is needed to invest in operations or retain talent; failure rate for poor-performing and even zero-alpha managers may tend to be higher when performance-based rather than standard fees are used.
new_option: investors must pay base fees even when managers underperform.

original_option: managers must retain clients year to year, avoid poor performance, and not violate management guidelines; managers also tend to have interest in increasing risk which may conflict with these goals; based on option pricing theory, higher volatility leads to higher option value which encourages managers to assume higher portfolio risk; this behavior has been observed in marketplace; as a result, investors when possible should carefully select benchmarks and monitor risk in portfolios; senior management at investment firms should ensure that compensation systems penalize portfolio managers for assuming excessive risk as well as reward them for earning superior returns.
new_option: managers must retain clients year to year, avoid poor performance, and not violate management guidelines.

original_option: presence of positive significant average excess return is evidence for manager skill; this excess return however must be net of fees and expenses for benefit of this skill to accrue to investor; the preference is for more linear compensation to the manager to reduce the incentives to change the portfolio's risk profile at inflection points.
new_option: presence of positive significant average excess return is evidence for manager skill.

original_option: (1) promote investor interests and instill investor confidence; (2) ensure accurate and consistent data; (3) obtain worldwide acceptance of a single standard for calculating and presenting performance; (4) promote fair, global competition among investment firms; and (5) promote industry self-regulation on a global basis.
new_option: (1) promote investor interests and instill investor confidence.

original_option: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms; asset owner that manages investments directly and/or through external managers on behalf of participants/beneficiaries/organization itself but does not compete for business would comply with GIPS Standards for Asset Owners; consultants, software houses, or third-party performance measurement providers such as custodians may not claim to be GIPS-compliant; GIPS compliance cannot be claimed for only some of a firm's products, nor for specific composites, pooled funds, or portfolios — compliance can be achieved only on a firm-wide basis.
new_option: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms.

original_option: present returns only for best-performing portfolios; base portfolio values on own unsubstantiated estimates; inflate returns by annualizing partial-period results; select most favorable measurement period (low to high point); present simulated returns as actually earned; choose particular index that selected portfolios have outperformed by greatest margin; portray growth of assets to mask difference between investment returns and client contributions; use marketing department's graphic design expertise to underplay unfavorable performance.
new_option: present returns only for best-performing portfolios.

original_option: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy; prospective investor is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's pooled funds and qualifies to invest in the pooled fund, again irrespective of any other current investments with firm.
new_option: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy.

original_option: GIPS standards well accepted by plan sponsors so non-compliance is serious competitive impediment; RFPs routinely ask if firm is GIPS-compliant and independently verified; global recognition helps compete in international markets — compliance characterized as investment management firm's 'passport to the international marketplace'; firms may realize internal benefits — implementing GIPS standards may identify opportunities to strengthen managerial controls, improve oversight of investment operations, provide management additional comfort in accuracy of performance reporting, and improve quality of information available to firm through technological enhancements.
new_option: GIPS standards well accepted by plan sponsors so non-compliance is serious competitive impediment.

original_option: reading is based on 2020 edition of GIPS standards, which are effective as of 1 January 2020; GIPS Reports that include performance for periods ending on or after 31 December 2020 must be prepared in accordance with the 2020 edition; 2020 edition contains three chapters: GIPS Standards for Firms, GIPS Standards for Asset Owners, and GIPS Standards for Verifiers, each with its own glossary.
new_option: reading is based on 2020 edition of GIPS standards, which are effective as of 1 January 2020.

original_option: contains 39 requirements and seven recommendations; several core principles create the foundation for GIPS standards including properly defining the firm, providing GIPS Reports to all prospective clients and prospective pooled fund investors, adhering to applicable laws and regulations, and ensuring that information presented is not false or misleading.
new_option: contains 39 requirements and seven recommendations.

original_option: firm must be defined as investment firm, subsidiary, or division held out to public as distinct business entity; distinct business entity is 'unit, division, department, or office that is organizationally and functionally segregated from other units, divisions, departments, or offices and that retains discretion over the assets it manages and that should have autonomy over the investment decision-making process'; possible criteria for identifying distinct business entity: organization being a legal entity, having distinct market or client type, or using separate and distinct investment process.
new_option: firm must be defined as investment firm, subsidiary, or division held out to public as distinct business entity.

original_option: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business); each division has own investment team, traders, marketing, admin, accounting; institutional division decides to achieve GIPS compliance, private client division decides not to; institutional division may become GIPS-compliant because it holds itself out as distinct business unit with own autonomous investment management, research, trading, and administrative team.
new_option: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business).

original_option: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility; total firm assets include assets managed by sub-advisors that firm has authority to select but do NOT include advisory-only assets or uncalled committed capital.
new_option: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility.

original_option: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design; changes in firm's organization are NOT permitted to lead to alteration of historical performance; general rule: apart from correcting errors, historical performance is not to be altered.
new_option: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design.

original_option: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite; discretionary, fee-paying pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; key term is 'discretionary' although GIPS standards do not define term itself; generally, portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy; although both discretionary and non-discretionary portfolios are included in total firm assets, only discretionary portfolios are included in composites; if client restrictions impede investment process to such extent that strategy cannot be implemented as intended, may be presumed to render portfolio non-discretionary.
new_option: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite.

original_option: minimum number of years required to initially claim compliance; documenting policies and procedures related to compliance; complying with laws and regulations; avoiding false or misleading performance information; requirements concerning distribution of GIPS Reports and lists of firm composites and pooled funds; use of total return benchmarks reflective of investment strategy; requirement to correct material errors in GIPS Report and redistribution to appropriate parties; maintenance of data and information necessary to support elements of GIPS Reports; conditions under which performance may be used or linked to that of another firm.
new_option: minimum number of years required to initially claim compliance.

original_option: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report; firms are strongly encouraged to comply with GIPS standards in addition to applicable regulatory requirements.
new_option: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report.

original_option: GIPS standards mandate use of time-weighted return (TWR); money-weighted returns (MWRs) may be used for portfolios meeting certain conditions; TWR is method of calculating period-by-period returns that reflects change in value and negates effects of external cash flows; except for private market investment portfolios, portfolios using TWR must be valued monthly and TWR must be calculated at least monthly as of calendar month end or last business day of month; if returns are not calculated daily and portfolio receives intra-month large cash flow, portfolio must be valued and sub-period return calculated at time of large cash flow; private market investment portfolios must be valued quarterly.
new_option: GIPS standards mandate use of time-weighted return (TWR).

original_option: what constitutes a large cash flow is defined by the firm; usually an external cash flow of such size that it may distort return if portfolio is not valued and sub-period return is not calculated at time of cash flow; large cash flow may be defined either relative to an absolute monetary threshold or as a percentage of the portfolio or composite assets; GIPS standards do not specify quantitative definition of large external cash flows — firms must make own determinations for each composite taking into account liquidity of market segments/asset classes and nature of investment strategy.
new_option: what constitutes a large cash flow is defined by the firm.

original_option: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy; annualized, since-inception MWRs must be calculated at least annually; for periods beginning with effective date of 2020 GIPS Standards, daily external cash flows must be used (external cash flows for periods prior to this date must be reflected on at least a quarterly basis); portfolios for which MWR is calculated must be valued at least annually and as of period end for which performance is calculated.
new_option: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy.
true TWR (intra-period valuation) = 0.41%: sub-period 1 return = (109,000 - 10,000 - 100,000)/100,000 = -0.01.

original_option: returns from cash and cash equivalents held in portfolios must be included in all total return calculations; portfolio return will be affected by how much cash manager elects to hold, and thus return calculations must reflect contribution of cash and cash equivalents to investment results; even if management of cash balances is handled by another firm (as often in manager-of-manager arrangements), cash and cash equivalents must be included in total return calculation.
new_option: returns from cash and cash equivalents held in portfolios must be included in all total return calculations.

original_option: returns for periods of less than one year must NOT be annualized; extrapolating partial-year returns by annualizing them would amount to a prediction about investment results for the rest of the year.
new_option: returns for periods of less than one year must NOT be annualized.

original_option: returns must be calculated after deduction of transaction costs incurred during period; transaction costs are costs of buying or selling investments (brokerage commissions, exchange fees/taxes, or spreads from internal or external brokers); custody fees should NOT be considered transaction costs even when charged per-transaction; some portfolios may pay bundled fees (any combination of investment management fees, transaction costs, custody fees, and/or administrative fees); if transaction costs cannot be identified and segregated from bundle fee, composites for institutional investors must reduce gross-of-fees return by entire amount of bundled fee or by that portion of bundled fee that includes transaction costs.
new_option: returns must be calculated after deduction of transaction costs incurred during period.

original_option: amount at which an investment could be sold in an orderly, arm's-length transaction between willing parties; valuation must be determined using objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date if available; fair value must include any accrued income on fixed-income securities and all other investments that earn interest income (firm may choose to recognize income on cash and cash equivalents on cash basis rather than accrual basis).
new_option: amount at which an investment could be sold in an orderly, arm's-length transaction between willing parties.

original_option: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available); this is the top of the hierarchy and takes precedence over all other valuation approaches.
new_option: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available).

original_option: in declining order of preference: (1) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets; (2) quoted prices for identical or similar investments in markets that are not active (markets in which there are few transactions for the investment, prices are not current, or price quotations vary substantially over time and/or between market makers); (3) market-based inputs, other than quoted prices, that are observable for the investment; (4) subjective, unobservable inputs.
new_option: in declining order of preference: (1) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets.

original_option: lowest in valuation hierarchy — used only when no observable inputs are available; below (in declining order): (1) objective observable unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market; (2) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets; (3) quoted prices for identical/similar investments in markets that are not active; (4) market-based inputs other than quoted prices that are observable.
new_option: lowest in valuation hierarchy — used only when no observable inputs are available.

original_option: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific; for investment strategies employing plain-vanilla securities trading in robust markets, quoted prices are readily available; other composites may represent strategies that materially make use of securities that trade infrequently in relatively illiquid markets where values must be imputed or estimated (real estate and private equity obvious examples).
new_option: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific.

original_option: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request; for assets valued using quantitative models, useful to list input factors such as discount rates and risk-adjusted cash flow projections and to review basis for estimating them; portfolio managers, security analysts, quantitative analysts, and traders should participate in these discussions.
new_option: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request.

original_option: (1) asset-weighting individual portfolio returns using beginning-of-period values; (2) using a method that reflects both beginning-of-period values and external cash flows; (3) using the Aggregate Return method.
new_option: (1) asset-weighting individual portfolio returns using beginning-of-period values.

original_option: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite; composite return is sum of individual portfolio returns weighted in proportion to their respective percentages of aggregate beginning assets.
new_option: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite.

original_option: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_pi / Σ V_pi)] where V_pi = V_0 + Σ(CF_i × w_i); V_pi is denominator of Modified Dietz formula (sum of beginning assets and weighted external cash flows for portfolio i); cash flows weighted by w_i = (CD - D_i)/CD, where CD is total calendar days in period and D_i is calendar days from beginning of period to time cash flow occurred.
new_option: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_pi / Σ V_pi)] where V_pi = V_0 + Σ(CF_i × w_i).

original_option: Portfolio A begins $100.00K, ends $110.55K; Portfolio B begins $97.40K, ends $105.20K; Portfolio C begins $112.94K, ends $113.30K; Portfolio D begins $124.47K, ends $100.50K; total begins $434.81K, ends $429.55K; with various cash flows during June; under beginning-of-period weighting method, weight assigned to Portfolio A is 100/434.81 = 0.23 = 23%; determining composite return when there are external cash flows is more complex — cash flows weighted following methodology introduced in Modified Dietz.

composite time-weighted returns, except for private market investment composites, must be calculated at least monthly; the less frequently the asset-weighting exercise is conducted, the greater the likelihood that composite returns will inaccurately reflect the constituent portfolios' aggregate performance.
new_option: composite time-weighted returns, except for private market investment composites, must be calculated at least monthly.

original_option: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite; all actual, fee-paying, discretionary pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; non-discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds must NOT be included in composites; non-fee-paying discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds may be included but additional disclosures may be required; if a strategy is offered as a pooled fund and a segregated account, the pooled fund must be included in a composite for that strategy; firm does not need to create a composite for a strategy if offered only as a pooled fund.
new_option: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite.

original_option: w_i = (CD - D_i)/CD where CD is total number of calendar days in period and D_i is number of calendar days since beginning of period until cash flow CF_i occurs; e.g., contribution on 5 June with 30 days in June: w_i = (30-5)/30 = 25/30 = 0.83; assumes cash flows occur at end of day; firm must consistently apply a policy for weighting of cash flows.

portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy; if constraints impede investment process to such extent that strategy cannot be implemented as intended, may be presumed to render portfolio non-discretionary; because discretion is key variable that determines inclusion in or exclusion from composite, firm implementing GIPS standards must have clear, written definition of discretion, and must consistently apply its definition.
new_option: portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy.

original_option: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries; others might have restrictions that prohibit sale of company stock; additionally, legal restrictions may apply (e.g., public fund might be statutorily precluded from investing in non-domestic securities); NONE of these constraints automatically renders a portfolio non-discretionary; portfolio manager must determine whether client-imposed constraints are likely to materially affect ability to execute investment strategy; if not material, could include in composite with unrestricted portfolios; if material, may include in composite with similarly constrained portfolios or classify as non-discretionary.
new_option: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries.

original_option: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains; retaining asset in portfolio may skew performance; if holding assets hinders manager's ability to implement intended strategy, either entire portfolio should be considered non-discretionary and excluded from firm's composites OR individual assets should be removed and remaining assets for which manager has full discretion should be included in composite; alternately, firm might include materiality threshold enabling it to consider portfolio discretionary if non-discretionary assets consist of less than certain percentage of portfolio assets.
new_option: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains.

original_option: client's investment policy might prohibit use of derivative securities such as futures, swaps, and options; firm must consider whether the restriction is pertinent; if portfolio manager is managing domestic mid-cap stock portfolio, fact that client prohibits use of derivatives may be irrelevant if manager's typical investment approach simply buys, holds, and sells common stocks; if use of derivative securities is central to firm's implementation of the investment mandate, however, then client's policy may render the portfolio non-discretionary.
new_option: client's investment policy might prohibit use of derivative securities such as futures, swaps, and options.
