original_option: in some cases, pattern of external cash flows might make a portfolio non-discretionary; if client frequently makes large withdrawals, perhaps on regular schedule, portfolio manager might have to maintain such a high level of liquidity that he cannot truly implement the investment strategy as he does for other portfolios with similar stated investment mandate, objective, or strategy.
new_option: in some cases, pattern of external cash flows might make a portfolio non-discretionary.

original_option: no model or hypothetical portfolios may be included in any composite; firms may not link theoretical performance of simulated or model portfolios with actual performance (model, hypothetical, backtested, or simulated returns are all considered theoretical performance and can be shown as supplemental information but cannot be linked to actual composite or pooled fund returns); on the other hand, if firm created new strategy and managed portfolios in this strategy with its own assets (sometimes called seed money), it could include those portfolios from inception in appropriate composites, subject to any presentation and reporting requirements related to inclusion of non-fee-paying portfolios in composites.
new_option: no model or hypothetical portfolios may be included in any composite.

original_option: under GIPS standards, composites must be defined according to investment mandate, objective, or strategy; composites must include all portfolios that meet composite definition; composite definition, including detailed criteria that determine assignment of portfolios to composite, must be documented in firm's policies and procedures; well-defined composites will be objectively representative of firm's products and consistent with firm's marketing strategy; firms are NOT permitted to include portfolios with different investment mandates, objectives, or strategies in the same composite.
new_option: under GIPS standards, composites must be defined according to investment mandate, objective, or strategy.

original_option: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics; composite based on investment mandate bears summary product/strategy description like 'Global Equities'; composite based on asset class (equity or fixed income) may be acceptable; asset-class modifier may indicate composite's investment style or strategy.
new_option: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics.

original_option: portfolios might be classified according to a defined style; equity style matrix classifies portfolios by capitalization (large cap, mid-cap, and small cap) and by style (value; core, also called neutral, market oriented, or blend; and growth); might be starting point for defining a set of composites; fixed-income portfolios may be classified by combination of duration and/or market segment that generally aligns with major fixed-income indexes.
new_option: portfolios might be classified according to a defined style.

original_option: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings; e.g., portfolio holding stocks with average market cap of $5 billion along with relatively high price-to-earnings multiple, relatively high price-to-book ratio, and relatively low dividend yield would likely be identified as mid-cap growth portfolio; alternately, portfolio's historical monthly or quarterly returns might be regressed against returns of pertinent capital market indexes to determine which style-specific benchmarks best explain portfolio's performance.
new_option: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings.

original_option: useful guideline is to build set of composites that will accurately represent firm's distinct investment strategies; with too few composites, firm risks overlooking significant differences and grouping diverse portfolios together into single, overly broad composite subject to wide dispersion of portfolio returns; with too many composites, in addition to incurring unnecessary costs, firm runs risk of creating narrowly defined groupings that are too much alike, contain too few portfolios or assets to be useful, or compromise client confidentiality.
new_option: useful guideline is to build set of composites that will accurately represent firm's distinct investment strategies.

original_option: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified; (2) review firm's existing marketing materials to determine which strategies are offered as segregated account; also review competitors' materials and RFPs; (3) construct provisional framework using descriptive captions to identify possible composites; (4) test how well firm's fee-paying, discretionary portfolios would fit provisional framework; (5) review proposed set to ensure they satisfy GIPS requirements in aggregate; (6) document composite definitions in detail and circulate for final review.
new_option: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified.

original_option: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics; this approach particularly appropriate if portfolios are limited to holding stocks that are held in the index; portfolios sharing distinctive risk/return profiles may reasonably be grouped together — for example, enhanced index funds with benchmark-specific targeted excess returns and tracking error tolerances might fall into natural groups; fixed-income composites can be meaningfully defined by asset classes/market segments (government debt, mortgage-backed, convertibles, high-yield), investment strategies (fundamental credit analysis, sector rotation, interest rate anticipation), or investment styles (indexing, core-plus).
new_option: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics.

original_option: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management; firms required to establish, document, and consistently apply policy of including new portfolios in appropriate composites on timely basis; for many strategies, new portfolios should be included as of beginning of next full performance measurement period after firm receives funds; if composite returns are calculated monthly and portfolio is funded on 20 May, composite should include new portfolio as of beginning of June; may take time to invest assets particularly when portfolio funded in kind or when securities to be purchased are relatively illiquid; GIPS standards give firms some discretion — firm must establish policy on composite-by-composite basis and apply it consistently.
new_option: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management.

original_option: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy; in many cases, firm loses discretion over portfolio upon being notified of pending termination; if firm calculates performance monthly and is informed on 20 May that management contract is being terminated effective 31 May and is instructed to stop trading forthwith, firm should include portfolio in composite only through 30 April; historical performance of terminated portfolios remains in the appropriate composite.
new_option: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy.

original_option: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate; historical performance of the portfolio must remain with the original composite; this is important requirement because if firms could transfer portfolios from one composite to another at will, unethical firm might identify and exploit opportunities to improve reported performance of selected composites by re-populating them with portfolios whose investment results were most advantageous during measurement period.
new_option: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate.

original_option: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor); (2) portfolio can be reassigned to another composite if the original composite is redefined in such way that portfolio no longer fits it (redefinition of existing composite should be highly unusual event; generally, if strategy changes over time, most appropriate to create new composite).
new_option: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor).

original_option: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy; significant cash flows are more likely to be an issue for strategies such as fixed income and emerging markets, wherein liquidity of underlying securities inhibits ability to quickly invest incoming cash or to conduct orderly sale of portfolio securities to meet cash outflow; firms must define 'significant' on ex ante, composite-specific basis and must consistently follow composite-specific significant cash flow policy; in event of significant cash flows, portfolio may be temporarily removed from composite; firms may alternatively use temporary new accounts to remove effect.
new_option: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy.

original_option: firm may set minimum asset level above which portfolios are eligible for inclusion in composite; portfolios below minimum asset level considered non-discretionary with respect to that composite; if firm establishes minimum asset level, must document policies addressing how portfolios treated if fall below minimum; firm may elect to remove portfolios month after they fall below minimum; alternatively firm may determine minimum asset level required to add portfolio is $1 million but portfolio not removed unless assets fall below $900,000; GIPS standards state any changes to composite-specific minimum asset level must NOT be applied retroactively; if portfolio removed because fell below minimum, prior performance must remain in composite.
new_option: firm may set minimum asset level above which portfolios are eligible for inclusion in composite.

original_option: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate. The portfolio shall be removed from the original composite at the end of the last calendar month before the event causing the removal occurred and shall be added to the appropriate new composite at the beginning of the calendar month following the date on which the portfolio is substantially invested. The historical performance of the portfolio shall remain in the original composite.'
new_option: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate.

original_option: (1) GIPS Composite Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific composite; (2) GIPS Pooled Fund Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific pooled fund; firms claiming compliance must make every reasonable effort to provide GIPS Report to all prospective clients and limited distribution pooled fund investors; GIPS Report must be one that represents strategy being marketed to prospect.
new_option: (1) GIPS Composite Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific composite.

original_option: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented; if composite has been in existence for less than 5 years, firm may present returns since inception and build over time to 10 years of required returns.
new_option: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented.

original_option: composite and benchmark annual returns for all years; number of portfolios (if six or more) in composite at each period end; amount of assets in composite; amount of total firm assets at end of each period; measure of internal dispersion of individual portfolio returns for each annual period if composite contains six or more portfolios for the full year; if monthly composite returns are available, three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of composite and benchmark returns as of each annual period end.
new_option: composite and benchmark annual returns for all years.

original_option: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented; requirement intended to allow users to see how consistently firm implemented its strategy across individual portfolios; wide range of results should prompt recipient to inquire about possible causes of variability of returns to portfolios purportedly managed in accordance with same strategy; may suggest composite is defined too broadly; only portfolios in composite for entire year are included in calculation of this dispersion measure; GIPS standards do not limit firms to one measure of internal dispersion — method chosen should fairly represent range of returns for each annual period.
new_option: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented.

original_option: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns; where monthly composite returns are available, firms must present, as of each annual period end, three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the monthly returns of both the composite and the benchmark; rationale is to give prospective clients an indication of risk of investment strategy as executed by firms under consideration; because all GIPS Composite Reports include same risk measure and that measure is based on historical experience rather than subjective inputs, GIPS standards allow for some degree of comparability among firms claiming compliance.
new_option: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns.

original_option: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Spinning Top Investments has been independently verified for the periods 1 January 2011 to 31 December 2020. The verification report is available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.'
new_option: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards.

original_option: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return; gross-of-fees returns are presented before management and custodial fees but after all trading expenses; composite and benchmark returns are presented gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes.
new_option: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return.

original_option: per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 3: 'The Large Cap Growth Composite includes all institutional portfolios that invest in large-cap US stocks that are considered to have growth in earnings prospects that are superior to that of the average company within the XYZ Large Cap Growth Index. Key material risks include the risks that stock prices will decline and that the composite will underperform its benchmark. The account minimum for the composite is $5 million. Prior to July 2016, the account minimum was $2 million. Prior to March 2020, the name of the composite was the Growth Composite.'
new_option: per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 3.

original_option: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm; (2) decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within new or acquiring firm; (3) new or acquiring firm has records that document and support reported performance; (4) there must be NO break in the track record between past firm or affiliation and new or acquiring firm.
new_option: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm.

original_option: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm — but the two performance records may NOT be linked.
new_option: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm.

original_option: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'; disclosure b: 'Spinning Top investments acquired the composite through an acquisition of ABC Capital in May 2014. Firm assets prior to 2014 are not presented because the composite was not part of the firm.'
new_option: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'.

original_option: portability requirements apply on composite-specific basis (not firm-wide); performance can be ported for specific composites where all requirements are met, but is not automatically applied firm-wide; each composite must be evaluated for portability independently based on whether substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new firm, decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent, new firm has records documenting and supporting reported performance, and no break in track record.
new_option: portability requirements apply on composite-specific basis (not firm-wide).

original_option: decision-making process must remain substantially intact AND independent within new or acquiring firm; both continuity (substantially intact) and independence within the new firm are required — if the process is subsumed into a different process at the new firm or ceases to operate independently, portability may fail; combined with requirement that substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new firm — key personnel must remain and continue to make decisions in essentially the same way.
new_option: decision-making process must remain substantially intact AND independent within new or acquiring firm.

original_option: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards; verification is intended to provide firm and users of its GIPS Reports greater confidence in its claim of compliance with GIPS standards; verification does NOT provide assurance as to accuracy of any particular composite or pooled fund presentation; GIPS standards RECOMMEND that firms undergo verification (voluntary).
new_option: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards.

original_option: verification must be performed by qualified and independent third party; provides assurance on whether firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance have been designed in compliance with GIPS standards and have been implemented on firm-wide basis; verifier must conduct verification in accordance with GIPS Standards for Verifiers; single verification report is issued only with respect to whole firm — verification CANNOT be carried out on a single composite or pooled fund; if firm does not meet all requirements of GIPS standards, it may not state or represent that it is in compliance — firm cannot claim single composite is 'in compliance' or all equity strategies are 'in compliance.'
new_option: verification must be performed by qualified and independent third party.

original_option: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite; performance examination is separate from verification (which is firm-wide) and applies to specific composite(s) or pooled fund(s).
new_option: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite.

original_option: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards; verifiers must not only obtain copy of firm's GIPS-related policies and procedures but also ensure that all applicable policies and procedures are properly included and adequately documented; finally, verifiers must understand policies, procedures, and methodologies the firm uses to value portfolios and compute investment performance.
new_option: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards.

original_option: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing; when selecting sample portfolios, verifiers must take into consideration number of composites and pooled funds at firm and number of portfolios in each composite; in addition, verifiers must take into account total assets under management, internal control structure at firm, number of years being verified, systems used in construction and maintenance of composites, method of calculating performance, and whether firm uses external performance measurement services; if verifier encounters errors or discovers that firm's record-keeping is deficient, larger sample or additional verification procedures may be warranted.
new_option: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing.

original_option: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages; above all, verification supports guiding principles of fair representation and full disclosure of investment performance.
new_option: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages.

original_option: F₀(T)/(1+r)^T + p₀ = c₀ + X/(1+r)^T — identifies equivalence between buying a fiduciary call (call + risk-free bond) and a synthetic protective put (put + forward on the underlying expiring at same time as put).
new_option: F₀(T)/(1+r)^T + p₀ = c₀ + X/(1+r)^T.

original_option: long forward position — the long call creates the upside and the short put creates the downside on the underlying; whatever the stock price at expiration, one of the two options will be in the money and the investor effectively buys the underlying at the strike price.
new_option: long forward position — the long call creates the upside and the short put creates the downside on the underlying.

original_option: short stock (or short forward/futures) + long call — the symmetric payoff of a short stock position becomes asymmetrical if transformed into a synthetic long put by buying a call; payoffs are identical to a long put at the same strike.
new_option: short stock (or short forward/futures) + long call — the symmetric payoff of a short stock position becomes asymmetrical if transformed into a synthetic long put by buying a call.

original_option: to exploit an ARBITRAGE opportunity presented by the actual forward price OR the need for an ALTERNATIVE to the outright purchase of a long forward position; frequently a forward contract is used instead of futures to acquire a stock position because it allows for contract customization.
new_option: to exploit an ARBITRAGE opportunity presented by the actual forward price OR the need for an ALTERNATIVE to the outright purchase of a long forward position.

original_option: the market maker exercises her call, pays the strike × shares, receives the shares, then delivers to client and receives the forward price × shares; if BELOW strike, the long put owner exercises, the market maker receives the shares for the strike, then delivers to the client and receives the forward price — either way the position is fully hedged.
new_option: the market maker exercises her call, pays the strike × shares, receives the shares, then delivers to client and receives the forward price × shares.

original_option: long stock + SHORT call — a long position in 100 shares of the underlying is required for each short call contract; no additional cash margin is needed if the long position in the underlying is maintained; if the stock price exceeds strike at expiry, shares are 'called away' from the writer to the option holder at the strike price.
new_option: long stock + SHORT call — a long position in 100 shares of the underlying is required for each short call contract.

original_option: CASH GENERATION in anticipation of limited upside moves in the underlying; the call option writer keeps the premium regardless of what happens; some view the premium as an additional source of income similar to cash dividends; essentially expressing the view that volatility of the underlying will be LOWER than the pricing of the option suggests.
new_option: CASH GENERATION in anticipation of limited upside moves in the underlying.

original_option: Max gain = (X − S₀) + c₀; Max loss = S₀ − c₀; Breakeven = S₀ − c₀ — the maximum gain is appreciation to exercise price plus premium; maximum loss occurs if stock becomes worthless (equals original price minus premium); breakeven occurs when the stock falls by the premium received.
new_option: Max gain = (X − S₀) + c₀; Max loss = S₀ − c₀.

original_option: Expiration value = S_T − Max[(S_T − X), 0]; Profit at expiration = S_T − Max[(S_T − X), 0] + c₀ − S₀ — at option expiration, the value is the stock price minus the exercise value of the call; any appreciation beyond the exercise price belongs to the option buyer.
new_option: Expiration value = S_T − Max[(S_T − X), 0].

original_option: with a covered call, the breakeven price and the maximum loss are THE SAME VALUE (both equal S₀ − c₀); the breakeven represents where the position exactly recovers the stock price; the maximum loss is the initial cost of the position (stock price less premium received) when the stock goes to zero.
new_option: with a covered call, the breakeven price and the maximum loss are THE SAME VALUE (both equal S₀ − c₀).

original_option: (X − S₀) + c₀ = 30.00 − 25.00 + 1.55 = 6.55 — the covered call writer participates in gains up to the exercise price, after which further appreciation is lost to the call buyer; X − S₀ = 5.00 in appreciation to the strike, plus 1.55 premium.
new_option: (X − S₀) + c₀ = 30.00 − 25.00 + 1.55 = 6.55 — the covered call writer participates in gains up to the exercise price, after which further appreciation is lost to the call buyer.

original_option: S₀ − c₀ = 25.00 − 1.55 = 23.45 — the call premium of 1.55 offsets a decline in the stock price by the amount of the premium received.
S₀ − c₀ = 25.00 − 1.55 = 23.45 — the stock price can fall to zero, causing a loss of the entire investment, but the option writer still keeps the option premium.
16.64 — she receives 1.64 when she writes the option and 15 when the option is exercised; a price improvement of 0.80 per share vs the original 15.84 market price (about 5.05% in a month).

Call Premium = Time Value + Intrinsic (Exercise) Value = Time Value + Max(0, S − X); the covered call writer who writes to improve on the market is capturing the TIME VALUE, which augments the stock selling price; giving up part of the return distribution would result in an opportunity loss if the underlying goes up.
new_option: Call Premium = Time Value + Intrinsic (Exercise) Value = Time Value + Max(0, S − X).

original_option: the protective put is a classic example of buying INSURANCE; the exercise price of the put is similar to the coverage amount for an insurance policy; the insurance policy DEDUCTIBLE is similar to the difference between the current asset price and the strike price of the put; a lower-strike put is like a higher-deductible policy — less expensive but greater price exposure before payoff kicks in.
new_option: the protective put is a classic example of buying INSURANCE.

original_option: Max gain = UNLIMITED (theoretically); Max loss = S₀ − X + p₀ (depreciation to strike + premium); Breakeven = S₀ + p₀ — the stock can rise to any level; losses are 'cut off' once stock falls to exercise price; at expiration value is greater of stock price or exercise price.
new_option: Max gain = UNLIMITED (theoretically).

original_option: Value of Protective Put at Expiration = S_T + Max[(X − S_T), 0]; Profit of Protective Put at Expiration = S_T + Max[(X − S_T), 0] − S₀ − p₀; the value is the greater of the stock price or the exercise price because the stock can rise to any level but has a floor value of the put exercise price.
new_option: Value of Protective Put at Expiration = S_T + Max[(X − S_T), 0].

original_option: the portfolio manager can REDUCE the cost of insurance by INCREASING the size of the deductible (current stock price minus put exercise price) — for example, using a lower exercise price; a lower-strike put has a lower premium but does not protect against losses until the stock falls to that lower strike.
new_option: the portfolio manager can REDUCE the cost of insurance by INCREASING the size of the deductible (current stock price minus put exercise price) — for example, using a lower exercise price.

original_option: there is NOT a linear relationship between the put value and its time until expiration; a two-month option does NOT sell for twice the price of a one-month option, nor does a three-month option sell for three times the price of a one-month option.
new_option: there is NOT a linear relationship between the put value and its time until expiration.

original_option: all theta values are NEGATIVE, approximating daily losses on option positions as time passes; a NOV 15 put (90 days) has theta of −0.009 while a SEP 15 put (30 days) has theta of −0.015; the shorter-term option has GREATER daily erosion of value than the longer-term option; if PBR remains at 15.84 for the last 10 days to maturity, the BSM put value erodes at varying rates averaging about −0.03/day.
new_option: all theta values are NEGATIVE, approximating daily losses on option positions as time passes.

original_option: a price shock to the underlying may INCREASE the market's expectations of future volatility, thereby likely increasing the put premium; by selling the option early, the investor would capture this increase; once the adverse event has occurred there may be no reason to continue holding the insurance — sell the put and recapture some of its cost.
new_option: a price shock to the underlying may INCREASE the market's expectations of future volatility, thereby likely increasing the put premium.

original_option: such continuous purchasing of puts to protect against a possible stock price decline is an EXPENSIVE strategy that would wipe out most of the long-term gain on an otherwise good investment; the occasional purchase of a protective put to manage a temporary situation, however, can be a sensible risk-reducing activity.
new_option: such continuous purchasing of puts to protect against a possible stock price decline is an EXPENSIVE strategy that would wipe out most of the long-term gain on an otherwise good investment.

original_option: +50 — the long stock delta is +100, the ATM short call has delta approximately −0.5 × 100 = −50 (short call loses money as underlying rises); overall +100 − 50 = +50.

+50 — futures and forwards on non-dividend-paying stocks are essentially proxies for the stock, so their deltas are +1.0 (long) or −1.0 (short); short 50-share forward 'cancels' half the long stock position, giving position delta 50.

for SMALL movements in the price of the underlying asset, these positions will show VERY SIMILAR GAINS AND LOSSES (all have position delta = +50); however they differ in their FULL payoff profiles, gamma, vega, and theta characteristics — so they behave the same only locally.

the long call OFFSETS portfolio losses when the share price INCREASES; as the share price rises above the strike, the call payoff offsets losses in the short position; portfolio losses are BOUNDED (never exceed strike − short entry + premium paid).
new_option: the long call OFFSETS portfolio losses when the share price INCREASES.

original_option: as the stock drops in value, the investor profits from the short stock position but portfolio gains will be REDUCED by the short put; when share price increases, short position loses money, the put expires worthless and the investor keeps the put premium — the loss on the short can still be substantial but is somewhat reduced by the put option premium.
new_option: as the stock drops in value, the investor profits from the short stock position but portfolio gains will be REDUCED by the short put.

original_option: long call + short stock: portfolio delta = 0.516 − 1 = −0.484 (bearish, less bearish than short alone due to call); short put + short stock: portfolio delta = − (−0.335) − 1 = −0.665 (still bearish, only slightly less due to put premium); both remain bearish overall.
new_option: long call + short stock: portfolio delta = 0.516 − 1 = −0.484 (bearish, less bearish than short alone due to call).

original_option: long call + short stock: the investor is LONG VEGA (from purchasing call) and exposed to negative theta (time decay hurts); short put + short stock: the investor is SHORT VEGA (from writing put) and BENEFITS from time decay (positive theta on short put).
new_option: long call + short stock: the investor is LONG VEGA (from purchasing call) and exposed to negative theta (time decay hurts).

original_option: the two strategies are very similar due to PUT-CALL PARITY; recall P = C − S + PV of X, implying that −P ≈ −C + S (ignoring PV of X), thus showing that a SHORT PUT payoff profile is similar to that of a COVERED CALL.
new_option: the two strategies are very similar due to PUT-CALL PARITY.

original_option: in a money spread, the two options differ only by EXERCISE PRICE; the investor buys an option with a given expiration and exercise price and sells an option with the SAME expiration but a DIFFERENT exercise price; the term 'spread' is used because the payoff is based on the difference or spread between option exercise prices.
new_option: in a money spread, the two options differ only by EXERCISE PRICE.

original_option: if establishing the spread requires a cash payment by the investor, it is a DEBIT SPREAD (long option value exceeds short option value); if the spread initially results in a cash INFLOW, it is a CREDIT SPREAD (short option value exceeds long option value).
new_option: if establishing the spread requires a cash payment by the investor, it is a DEBIT SPREAD (long option value exceeds short option value).

original_option: buy the LOWER-strike call (more expensive, c_L) and write the HIGHER-strike call (less expensive, c_H); because c_L > c_H, a bull call spread involves an INITIAL CASH OUTFLOW (DEBIT SPREAD); net premium V₀ = c_L − c_H.
new_option: buy the LOWER-strike call (more expensive, c_L) and write the HIGHER-strike call (less expensive, c_H).

original_option: Max gain = X_H − X_L − (c_L − c_H) (occurs when S_T ≥ X_H); Max loss = c_L − c_H (occurs when S_T ≤ X_L; both expire OTM); Breakeven: S_T* = X_L + c_L − c_H (lower exercise price plus cost of spread).
new_option: Max gain = X_H − X_L − (c_L − c_H) (occurs when S_T ≥ X_H).

original_option: buy the HIGHER-strike put (more expensive, p_H) and write the LOWER-strike put (less expensive, p_L); a bear put spread is a DEBIT SPREAD with initial cash outlay = p_H − p_L, which equals the maximum loss.
new_option: buy the HIGHER-strike put (more expensive, p_H) and write the LOWER-strike put (less expensive, p_L).

original_option: 3.88 — the initial cash outlay is the cost of OCT 50 put minus the premium received from writing OCT 45 put: 6.80 − 2.92 = 3.88.

a long straddle buys both a put AND a call with the SAME exercise price and SAME expiration date on the SAME underlying; a long straddle is long VOLATILITY — betting that volatility will INCREASE from current levels; because the buyer pays premium for two options, a large price movement (up or down) is needed to make a profit.
new_option: a long straddle buys both a put AND a call with the SAME exercise price and SAME expiration date on the SAME underlying.

original_option: very LOW DELTA (near zero when ATM) with HIGH GAMMA; trader does not initially favor an increase or decrease in the share price but knows the delta may change quickly once a direction asserts itself.
new_option: very LOW DELTA (near zero when ATM) with HIGH GAMMA.

original_option: V_T = Max(0, S_T − X) + Max(0, X − S_T); Profit Π = V_T − c₀ − p₀; two breakeven points: LOWER = X − c₀ − p₀ and UPPER = X + c₀ + p₀; the range in between produces a loss.
new_option: V_T = Max(0, S_T − X) + Max(0, X − S_T).

original_option: MAXIMUM GAIN is theoretically UNLIMITED on the upside from the long call; MAXIMUM LOSS is limited to the total premium paid (c₀ + p₀), which occurs when the stock closes exactly at the strike price (both options expire worthless).
new_option: MAXIMUM GAIN is theoretically UNLIMITED on the upside from the long call.

original_option: a collar is an option position in which the investor is LONG the underlying, BUYS a put with exercise price BELOW current stock price (downside protection), and WRITES a call with exercise price ABOVE current stock price (finances put); the profit and loss on the equity position is limited by the option positions.
new_option: a collar is an option position in which the investor is LONG the underlying, BUYS a put with exercise price BELOW current stock price (downside protection), and WRITES a call with exercise price ABOVE current stock price (finances put).

original_option: a ZERO-COST COLLAR involves the purchase of a put and sale of a call with the SAME PREMIUM; a collar written in the over-the-counter market can be easily structured to provide a precise offset of the put premium with the call premium; most collars are structured and transacted OTC because the exercise price on the call must be a specific one.
new_option: a ZERO-COST COLLAR involves the purchase of a put and sale of a call with the SAME PREMIUM.

original_option: V_T = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2), where X_2 > X_1 (X_1 = long put strike, X_2 = short call strike); profit Π = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2) − S₀ − p₀ + c₀.
new_option: V_T = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2), where X_2 > X_1 (X_1 = long put strike, X_2 = short call strike).

original_option: as chosen put and call exercise prices move successively FARTHER in opposite directions from the current price, the collar begins to replicate the underlying gain/loss pattern of a LONG POSITION in the underlying security; conversely, as the strikes APPROACH each other, expected returns and volatility become less equity-like and converge on a RISK-FREE FIXED-INCOME return; a collar is economically INTERMEDIATE between pure equity and fixed-income exposure.
new_option: as chosen put and call exercise prices move successively FARTHER in opposite directions from the current price, the collar begins to replicate the underlying gain/loss pattern of a LONG POSITION in the underlying security.

original_option: for a bear put spread with lower strike X_L (short put p_L) and higher strike X_H (long put p_H), breakeven S_T* = X_H − p_H + p_L; profit = X_H − S_T − p_H + p_L when X_L < S_T < X_H; max loss (occurs above X_H) = −(p_H − p_L) = p_L − p_H; max gain (occurs at or below X_L) = X_H − X_L − p_H + p_L.
new_option: for a bear put spread with lower strike X_L (short put p_L) and higher strike X_H (long put p_H), breakeven S_T* = X_H − p_H + p_L.

original_option: a strategy in which one SELLS an option and BUYS the same type of option (put or call) but with DIFFERENT EXPIRATION DATES on the SAME underlying asset and with the SAME STRIKE; when the investor buys the more distant option and sells the near-term option, it is a LONG calendar spread; buying near-term and selling longer-dated is a SHORT calendar spread.
new_option: a strategy in which one SELLS an option and BUYS the same type of option (put or call) but with DIFFERENT EXPIRATION DATES on the SAME underlying asset and with the SAME STRIKE.

original_option: taking advantage of TIME DECAY; time decay is more PRONOUNCED for a SHORT-TERM option than for a long-term option; a long calendar spread seeks to exploit this by purchasing a longer-term option and writing a shorter-term option so that the short option's time value erodes faster.
new_option: taking advantage of TIME DECAY.

original_option: a directional opinion on the stock but not believing the price movement is IMMINENT; sees an opportunity to capture time value in one or more shorter-lived options that are expected to expire worthless; combines a longer-term bullish or bearish outlook with a near-term neutral outlook.
new_option: a directional opinion on the stock but not believing the price movement is IMMINENT.

original_option: a long calendar spread is a LONG VOLATILITY trading strategy because the longer-term option has a HIGHER VEGA than the shorter-term option; maximum profit is obtained when the short-term option expires worthless, then implied volatility surges, increasing the price of the remaining long-term option.
new_option: a long calendar spread is a LONG VOLATILITY trading strategy because the longer-term option has a HIGHER VEGA than the shorter-term option.

original_option: a big move in the underlying market or a DECREASE in implied volatility will help a SHORT calendar spread; a stable market or an INCREASE in implied volatility will help a LONG calendar spread; calendar spreads are sensitive to movement of the underlying but also to changes in implied volatility.
new_option: a big move in the underlying market or a DECREASE in implied volatility will help a SHORT calendar spread.

original_option: implied volatility is derived from an option pricing model (such as BSM) and is the value that EQUATES THE MODEL PRICE OF AN OPTION TO ITS MARKET PRICE; all other BSM inputs (strike, underlying price, time to expiration, risk-free rate) are observable; implied volatilities incorporate investors' expectations about future asset returns and market uncertainty.
new_option: implied volatility is derived from an option pricing model (such as BSM) and is the value that EQUATES THE MODEL PRICE OF AN OPTION TO ITS MARKET PRICE.

original_option: out-of-the-money (OTM) puts TYPICALLY COMMAND HIGHER implied volatilities than ATM or OTM calls; this phenomenon is attributed to investors' reassessments of the probabilities of negative 'fat-tailed' market events such as the 2007-2009 global financial crisis.
new_option: out-of-the-money (OTM) puts TYPICALLY COMMAND HIGHER implied volatilities than ATM or OTM calls.

original_option: when implied volatilities of both OTM puts and OTM calls trade at a premium to ATM implied volatilities, the curve is U-shaped — a VOLATILITY SMILE; the more common shape is a VOLATILITY SKEW where implied volatility INCREASES for OTM puts and DECREASES for OTM calls as strike moves from current price; skew persists because investors have less interest in OTM calls while OTM puts have universal demand as portfolio insurance.
new_option: when implied volatilities of both OTM puts and OTM calls trade at a premium to ATM implied volatilities, the curve is U-shaped — a VOLATILITY SMILE.

original_option: when the implied volatility is significantly higher (relative to historical levels) for PUTS with strike prices below the underlying's price, it means an IMBALANCE in the supply and demand for options; a sharp increase in skew accompanied by a surge in absolute implied volatility is an INDICATOR THAT MARKET SENTIMENT IS TURNING BEARISH; in contrast, higher IVs for OTM CALLS indicate BULLISH sentiment.
new_option: when the implied volatility is significantly higher (relative to historical levels) for PUTS with strike prices below the underlying's price, it means an IMBALANCE in the supply and demand for options.

original_option: the 90% moneyness option is a put with strike (X) equal to 90% of the current underlying price (X/S = 90%); the 110% moneyness of the call is calculated similarly (110%); the volatility skew is calculated as the DIFFERENCE between the IVs of the 90% put and the 110% call; a positive skew number (put IV > call IV) reflects the fat-tail insurance premium demanded for downside protection.
new_option: the 90% moneyness option is a put with strike (X) equal to 90% of the current underlying price (X/S = 90%).

original_option: a THREE-DIMENSIONAL plot, for put and call options on the same underlying, of days to expiration (x-axis), option strike prices (y-axis), and implied volatilities (z-axis); it SIMULTANEOUSLY shows the volatility skew (smile) and the term structure of implied volatility; typically NOT FLAT as BSM would suggest.
new_option: a THREE-DIMENSIONAL plot, for put and call options on the same underlying, of days to expiration (x-axis), option strike prices (y-axis), and implied volatilities (z-axis).

original_option: WRITE CALLS — bearish view + declining volatility; writing calls generates premium income and profits from the market not rising, with vega working in the writer's favor as volatility decreases.
new_option: WRITE CALLS — bearish view + declining volatility.

original_option: WRITE PUTS — bullish view + declining volatility; writing puts represents an alternative to take advantage of a bullish market view with declining volatility; as the stock rises and implied volatility decreases, the short put moves farther OTM and its price falls, benefiting the writer.
new_option: WRITE PUTS — bullish view + declining volatility.

original_option: BUY STRADDLE — neutral on direction + expecting vol increase; the long straddle takes advantage of an increase in call/put option prices in response to the expected surge in volatility, vega, and gamma; profitable if price of call (put) exceeds upper (lower) breakeven.
new_option: BUY STRADDLE — neutral on direction + expecting vol increase.

original_option: CALENDAR SPREAD — combines longer-term directional outlook with near-term neutral outlook; the calendar spread is typically implemented in option markets characterized by LOW implied volatility when the underlying stock is expected to remain in a trading range, but only until the maturity of the short-term option; delta approximately zero, gamma not zero.
new_option: CALENDAR SPREAD — combines longer-term directional outlook with near-term neutral outlook.

original_option: long calls + short puts; used to implement a BULLISH view (the investor buys the calls) while LOWERING the cost (the investor sells the puts) of the long position in the underlying asset that will be established upon exercising the options; combined position aligned with bullish view + declining volatility outlook.
new_option: long calls + short puts; used to implement a BULLISH view (the investor buys the calls) while LOWERING the cost (the investor sells the puts) of the long position in the underlying asset that will be established upon exercising the options.

original_option: the purchase of call or put SPREADS is most appropriate when the investor has a bullish view (call bull spread) or a bearish view (put bear spread) but the underlying market is NOT clearly trending upward or downward; spreads are a way to REDUCE the total cost by buying one option and writing another.
new_option: the purchase of call or put SPREADS is most appropriate when the investor has a bullish view (call bull spread) or a bearish view (put bear spread) but the underlying market is NOT clearly trending upward or downward.

original_option: if the implied volatility curve is SKEWED — with the implied volatility of OTM puts relatively higher than for nearer-to-the-money options — the cost of a BEARISH SPREAD is EVEN LOWER; this is because in the put bear spread, the lower exercise price, more OTM (but relatively more expensive) put is SOLD and the higher exercise price, nearer-to-the-money (but relatively less expensive) put is PURCHASED.
new_option: if the implied volatility curve is SKEWED — with the implied volatility of OTM puts relatively higher than for nearer-to-the-money options — the cost of a BEARISH SPREAD is EVEN LOWER.

original_option: the 170 call generates $32,250 (adequate cash), and given the firm's bearish outlook plus Parker's willingness to sell soon, the risk of shares being called at 170 is acceptable; the 165 call is in-the-money with high exercise likelihood (undesirable given target is to write, not to be assigned); 175 call would only generate $20,150 (insufficient); 180 call generates $12,500 (also insufficient).
new_option: the 170 call generates $32,250 (adequate cash), and given the firm's bearish outlook plus Parker's willingness to sell soon, the risk of shares being called at 170 is acceptable.

original_option: net cost 0.45, breakeven 16.05 — net cost = 2.00 − 1.55 = 0.45 per contract; at expiry the strategy is profitable if VIX futures increase above 16.05 (call strike 15.60 + net cost 0.45); below 14.75 loses proportionally to short put exposure.
new_option: net cost 0.45, breakeven 16.05 — net cost = 2.00 − 1.55 = 0.45 per contract.

original_option: Option B (£60 strike) — breakeven £63 = £60 + £3, so at option expiry profitable at any stock price above £63 (below the £70 target); Option A breakeven £62 but profit per unit premium is lower; Option C breakeven £70.40, ABOVE target so unusable; £60 strike offers largest profit per unit of premium if stock reaches £70: (£70 − £63)/£3 = 2.3, best of the three.

Option B (€42.50 strike) — breakevens: A €38.55, B €40.78, C €41.54; if stock hits €37.80: Option A profit €0.75 (limited); Option B profit-per-premium = (€40.78 − €37.80)/€1.72 = 1.73 vs Option C = (€41.54 − €37.80)/€3.46 = 1.08; also B has more gamma than C (0.0460 vs 0.0346), so it benefits more from a stock drop.
new_option: Option B (€42.50 strike) — breakevens: A €38.55, B €40.78, C €41.54.

original_option: the choice of derivative depends on the OUTLOOK for the underlying asset, the INVESTMENT HORIZON, and EXPECTATIONS FOR IMPLIED VOLATILITY over that horizon; options have asymmetric payoffs while futures have symmetric; futures require margin but no premium; the relative advantage depends on differences in leverage, payoff profiles (asymmetrical vs symmetrical), the shape of the volatility futures term structure, and the cost of the options vs index futures.
new_option: the choice of derivative depends on the OUTLOOK for the underlying asset, the INVESTMENT HORIZON, and EXPECTATIONS FOR IMPLIED VOLATILITY over that horizon.

original_option: over-the-counter (OTC) contract between two parties that agree to exchange cash flows on specified payment dates — one based on variable (floating) interest rate and the other based on a fixed rate (the 'swap rate') determined at time swap is initiated; swap tenor is when the swap is agreed to expire; both interest rates applied to swap's notional value to determine size of each payment; normally two payments (one fixed, one floating) are in same currency but not equal, so typically netted with party owing greater amount paying difference to other party; party that currently has fixed (floating) risk can effectively convert it into floating (fixed) one.
new_option: over-the-counter (OTC) contract between two parties that agree to exchange cash flows on specified payment dates — one based on variable (floating) interest rate and the other based on a fixed rate (the 'swap rate') determined at time swap is initiated.

original_option: firm sells £20 million of three-year floating-rate bonds that pay semiannual coupon equal to six-month reference rate plus 50 bps; firm enters pay-fixed 1.25%, receive-floating six-month reference rate swap; at first coupon date reference rate is 0.75%: coupon on bonds = (0.75% + 0.50%) × £20M × 180/360 = £125,000; on swap: receive 0.75%×£20M×180/360 = £75,000, pay 1.25%×£20M×180/360 = £125,000, net payment £50,000; total = £175,000; at second date rate rises to 1.50%: bond coupon = £200,000, receive £25,000 net on swap, total still £175,000; firm has effectively fixed its all-in borrowing costs.
new_option: firm sells £20 million of three-year floating-rate bonds that pay semiannual coupon equal to six-month reference rate plus 50 bps.

original_option: modified duration of a swap is net of modified durations of the equivalent positions in fixed- and floating-rate bonds; position of pay-fixed party has modified duration of floating-rate bond MINUS modified duration of fixed-rate bond; because duration of fixed-rate bond is positive and larger than duration of floating-rate bond (which is near zero), pay-fixed receive-floating swap has NEGATIVE duration from perspective of fixed-rate payer (positive from perspective of receiver); makes sense — pay-fixed position benefits from rising interest rates.
new_option: modified duration of a swap is net of modified durations of the equivalent positions in fixed- and floating-rate bonds.

original_option: N_S = [(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) / MDUR_S] × MV_P where MDUR_T is target modified duration, MDUR_P is portfolio modified duration, MDUR_S is swap modified duration, MV_P is market value of bond portfolio; equivalence ΔP = N_S × ΔS becomes (MV_P)(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) = (N_S)(MDUR_S).
new_option: N_S = [(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) / MDUR_S] × MV_P where MDUR_T is target modified duration, MDUR_P is portfolio modified duration, MDUR_S is swap modified duration, MV_P is market value of bond portfolio.

original_option: N_S = [(4.5 - 5.5) / (-2.00)] × €50,000,000 = €25,000,000; manager pays fixed and receives floating on €25M notional to reduce duration; adds negative-duration position.

most of the time, hedging instrument and asset or portfolio to be hedged are imperfect substitutes; result is market risk called basis risk or spread risk — difference between market performance of asset and derivative instrument used to hedge it; when using interest rate swap to hedge, possible that changes in underlying rate of derivative contract and thus in value of swap do not perfectly mirror changes in value of bond portfolio; composition of bond portfolio could bear additional market risks other than interest rate risk (e.g., corporate bond hedged with IRS remains exposed to credit spread risk).
new_option: most of the time, hedging instrument and asset or portfolio to be hedged are imperfect substitutes.

original_option: OTC derivative instrument used mainly to hedge a loan expected to be taken out in the near future or to hedge against changes in the level of interest rates in the future; with advanced settled at maturity, FRA will settle only the discounted difference between the interest rate agreed on in the contract and the actual rate prevailing at time of settlement, applied on the notional amount of the contract.
new_option: OTC derivative instrument used mainly to hedge a loan expected to be taken out in the near future or to hedge against changes in the level of interest rates in the future.

original_option: forwards, like swaps, are OTC instruments and especially useful because they can be customized, but they have counterparty risk; in contrast, exchange-traded interest rate futures contracts are standardized and guaranteed by a clearinghouse, so counterparty risk is virtually zero; regulatory changes in global markets are moving both OTC swaps and forward contracts toward a clearing process — reduces risk that one counterparty will default (by requiring collateral for trades and by daily marking to market of open positions); however, still risk that major counterparty will fail to carry out its obligations (e.g., Lehman Brothers).
new_option: forwards, like swaps, are OTC instruments and especially useful because they can be customized, but they have counterparty risk.

original_option: when a bond portfolio is fully hedged, its value is immunized with respect to changes in yields; stated as ΔP = N_S × ΔS where ΔP is change in value of bond portfolio and ΔS is change in value of swap for a given change in interest rates; to reduce changes in value of fixed-rate portfolio, manager will want to lower the overall duration by exchanging part of this fixed-rate income stream for a floating-rate stream — done by entering interest rate swap where portfolio manager pays fixed and receives floating.
new_option: when a bond portfolio is fully hedged, its value is immunized with respect to changes in yields.

original_option: BPV_HR = [(BPV_T - BPV_P) / BPV_F] where BPV_F ≈ BPV_CTD / CF; more general Equation 9: BPV_HR = [(BPV_T - BPV_P) / BPV_CTD] × CF where BPV_T is target basis point value, BPV_P is portfolio basis point value, BPV_CTD is BPV of cheapest-to-deliver bond, CF is conversion factor.
new_option: BPV_HR = [(BPV_T - BPV_P) / BPV_F] where BPV_F ≈ BPV_CTD / CF.

original_option: BPV_CTD = MDUR_CTD × 0.0001 × [(price/100) × contract size] = 8.623 × 0.0001 × (98.14/100 × €100,000) = €84.63; conversion factor = 0.619489; this feeds into futures hedge ratio calculation.

hedging results are imperfect because (1) hedge is done with cheapest-to-deliver bond, and since CTD bond can change over holding period, duration of futures contract can also change; (2) relationship between interest rates and bond prices is not linear, owing to convexity; (3) term structure of interest rates often changes via non-parallel moves.
new_option: hedging results are imperfect because (1) hedge is done with cheapest-to-deliver bond, and since CTD bond can change over holding period, duration of futures contract can also change.

original_option: currency swap is similar to interest rate swap but different in two ways: (1) interest rates are associated with different currencies; (2) notional principal amounts may or may not be exchanged at beginning and end of swap's life; currency swaps help parties hedge against risk of exchange rate fluctuations and achieve better rate outcomes; some hedge transactions are designed to hedge only foreign interest cash flows and not principal payments so principal exchange would not be necessary.
new_option: currency swap is similar to interest rate swap but different in two ways: (1) interest rates are associated with different currencies.

original_option: exchanges notional principals because goal of transaction is to issue at more favorable funding rate and swap the amount back to currency of choice; firms that need foreign-denominated cash can obtain funding in local currency and then swap local currency for required foreign currency using cross-currency basis swap; swap periodically sets interest rate payments, mostly floating for floating, separately in two different currencies; net effect is to use loan in local currency to take out loan in foreign currency while avoiding foreign exchange risk; exchange rate is fixed.
new_option: exchanges notional principals because goal of transaction is to issue at more favorable funding rate and swap the amount back to currency of choice.

original_option: Canadian PE firm executing $40M LBO of US electronics manufacturer; rate on US dollar loan is semiannual USD reference floating + 100 bps; PE firm borrows CAD50M for three years at Canadian dollar reference floating rate + 65 bps; three-year CAD-USD cross-currency basis swap quoted at -15 bps at USD/CAD 0.8000; PE firm receives Canadian dollar reference rate minus 15 bps every six months in exchange for paying US dollar reference rate; effective spread paid = 65 bps + 15 bps = 80 bps vs 100 bps if borrowed in USD.
new_option: Canadian PE firm executing $40M LBO of US electronics manufacturer.

original_option: basis is quoted on non-USD leg of the swap; 'paying' the basis would mean borrowing the other currency versus lending US dollars, whereas 'receiving' the basis implies lending the other currency versus borrowing US dollars; if covered interest rate parity holds, forward exchange rate is determined by spot exchange rate and interest rate differential between foreign and domestic currencies over term of forward rate; however, usually covered interest rate parity does not hold and thus gives rise to the basis.
new_option: basis is quoted on non-USD leg of the swap.

original_option: at inception, Canadian PE firm delivers Canadian dollars (N) in exchange for US dollars (at rate of N × S_0); PE firm delivers CAD50 million to swap dealer, receives USD40 million (= CAD50 million × 0.8000 USD/CAD).
new_option: at inception, Canadian PE firm delivers Canadian dollars (N) in exchange for US dollars (at rate of N × S_0).

original_option: on first settlement date CAD reference rate 1.95%, basis -15 bps → CAD rate on swap is 1.80% (= 1.95% - 15 bps); assume USD rate 2.50%; PE firm receives CAD50M × 1.80% × 180/360 = CAD450,000; PE firm pays CAD50M × 0.8000 USD/CAD × 2.50% × 180/360 = USD500,000; interest on PE firm's loan is CAD50M × (1.95% + 0.65%) × 180/360 = CAD650,000; net Canadian dollar payment = CAD200,000 (= CAD650,000 - CAD450,000); at USD/CAD 0.8000 this equals USD160,000, plus USD500,000 on swap = USD660,000 total vs USD700,000 (= USD40M × 3.5% × 180/360) if firm had taken USD loan.
new_option: on first settlement date CAD reference rate 1.95%, basis -15 bps → CAD rate on swap is 1.80% (= 1.95% - 15 bps).

original_option: at maturity of swap (after successful exit from LBO via US IPO), Canadian PE firm swaps back US dollars in exchange for Canadian dollars at same rate: PE Firm returns USD40 million to swap dealer, receives CAD50 million (= USD40M × 1/0.8000); PE firm uses CAD50M to pay off local lender.
new_option: at maturity of swap (after successful exit from LBO via US IPO), Canadian PE firm swaps back US dollars in exchange for Canadian dollars at same rate: PE Firm returns USD40 million to swap dealer, receives CAD50 million (= USD40M × 1/0.8000).

original_option: risk that value of a current or future asset (liability) in a foreign currency will decrease (increase) when converted into the domestic currency; currency swaps, forwards, and futures can be used to effectively alter currency risk exposures.
new_option: risk that value of a current or future asset (liability) in a foreign currency will decrease (increase) when converted into the domestic currency.

original_option: N is notional principal of Canadian dollar leg of swap, and S_0 (agreed at start) is spot exchange rate for all payments (at inception, on interest payment dates, and at maturity); for Canadian PE firm this means N = CAD50 million and S_0 = USD/CAD 0.8000; the exchange rate is fixed for all cash flows over life of swap, avoiding FX risk on principal exchange.
new_option: N is notional principal of Canadian dollar leg of swap, and S_0 (agreed at start) is spot exchange rate for all payments (at inception, on interest payment dates, and at maturity).

original_option: if covered interest rate parity held perfectly, forward exchange rate would be determined solely by spot exchange rate and interest rate differential between currencies; deviations from covered interest rate parity give rise to the basis in cross-currency basis swaps; the basis represents difference between interest rates in the cross-currency basis swap and those implied by forward exchange rates; typically not zero.
new_option: if covered interest rate parity held perfectly, forward exchange rate would be determined solely by spot exchange rate and interest rate differential between currencies.

original_option: 'paying' the basis means borrowing the other currency versus lending US dollars; 'receiving' the basis means lending the other currency versus borrowing US dollars; in Example 7, three-year CAD-USD cross-currency swap quoted at -15 bps means Canadian PE firm as lender of Canadian dollars in swap will receive CAD reference rate minus 15 bps every six months in exchange for paying USD reference rate.
new_option: 'paying' the basis means borrowing the other currency versus lending US dollars.

original_option: currency swaps, forwards, and futures can all be used to effectively alter currency risk exposures; forwards are OTC and can be customized (but have counterparty risk), while futures are exchange-traded, standardized, and cleared (virtually no counterparty risk); swaps typically involve series of cash flow exchanges over time, whereas forwards and futures involve single settlement at maturity (or via marking to market for futures); currency swaps may involve principal exchange at beginning/end while forwards and futures typically do not.
new_option: currency swaps, forwards, and futures can all be used to effectively alter currency risk exposures.

original_option: investor holding large concentrated position paying dividends can enter total return swap requiring her to transfer total performance of stock to counterparty for prespecified fee; under swap she pays counterparty share price appreciation plus dividends received over life of contract; if stock price decreases, she receives share price depreciation but net of dividends; investor receives agreed-on floating-rate interest payment based on swap notional; allows temporary neutralization of long exposure without losing ownership or voting rights.
new_option: investor holding large concentrated position paying dividends can enter total return swap requiring her to transfer total performance of stock to counterparty for prespecified fee.

original_option: equity swaps that have single stock as underlying can be cash settled or physically settled; if cash settled, on termination date equity swap receiver will receive (pay) equity appreciation (depreciation) in cash; if physically settled, on termination date equity swap receiver will receive quantity of single stock specified in contract and pay notional amount.
new_option: equity swaps that have single stock as underlying can be cash settled or physically settled.

original_option: portfolio manager is receiver in six-month equity swap with notional principal €4.5 million and no interim cash flows requiring physical settlement at maturity of 300,000 shares of Italian insurer Generali; at maturity manager receives 300,000 shares and pays €4.5 million (purchase price per share €15 = €4.5M/300,000); also pays interest on swap; at swap maturity Generali price is €16 implying gain of €300,000 (= [€16 - €15] × 300,000); if cash settled instead of physical, manager would have received €300,000 = (€16 - €15) × 300,000.
new_option: portfolio manager is receiver in six-month equity swap with notional principal €4.5 million and no interim cash flows requiring physical settlement at maturity of 300,000 shares of Italian insurer Generali.

original_option: indispensable tool for many investment managers: low-cost instrument to implement tactical allocation decisions, achieve portfolio diversification, and attain international exposure; standardized contracts listed on exchange; when underlying is stock index, only cash settlement is available at contract expiration; single stock futures also available to acquire desired exposure to specific stock (this exposure also achievable with equity forwards which are OTC contracts used when counterparties need customized agreement); at expiration, single stock futures contract could require cash settlement or physical settlement using stock.
new_option: indispensable tool for many investment managers: low-cost instrument to implement tactical allocation decisions, achieve portfolio diversification, and attain international exposure.

original_option: N_f = [(β_T - β_S) / β_f] × (S/F) where β_T is target beta, β_S is portfolio beta, β_f is beta of futures, S is market value of stock portfolio, F is value per futures contract; if investor wants to increase portfolio's beta, β_T > β_S and N_f is positive (must buy futures); if she wants to decrease beta, β_T < β_S and N_f is negative (must sell futures); special case eliminating market risk β_T = 0 reduces to N_f = -(β_S/β_f)(S/F).
new_option: N_f = [(β_T - β_S) / β_f] × (S/F) where β_T is target beta, β_S is portfolio beta, β_f is beta of futures, S is market value of stock portfolio, F is value per futures contract.

original_option: N_f = [(1.10 - 0.85)/1.00] × (£40,000,000/£73,000) = 136.99 ≈ 137 contracts; must BUY 137 FTSE 100 futures; verification: FTSE rises 2%, portfolio rises 2% × 0.85 = 1.7% = £680,000, futures profit = 137 × (£74,460 - £73,000) = £200,020, total gain £880,020, effective return 2.20%, effective beta = 2.20%/2% = 1.10 (matches target).

also known as 'cash securitization' or 'cash overlay'; strategy designed to boost returns by finding ways to 'equitize' unintended cash holdings; by purchasing futures contracts, fund managers attempt to replicate performance of underlying market in which cash would have been invested; alternative solution could be to purchase calls and sell puts on underlying asset with same exercise price and expiry date; formula assumes β_S = 0 so N_f = (β_T/β_f)(S/F).
new_option: also known as 'cash securitization' or 'cash overlay'.

original_option: N_f = (β_T/β_f)(S/F) = (1.00/1.00)(JPY140,000,000/JPY23,000,000) = 6.087 rounded to 6 contracts; appropriate number of futures to buy to equitize portfolio's excess cash position is six contracts.

actual futures contract price = 2,700 × $250 = $675,000; assume portfolio beta 1.0; to hedge 30% ($30M) of $100M portfolio: sell $30,000,000 / (2,700 × $250) = 44.444 ≈ 44 S&P 500 futures contracts; if portfolio carried above-market risk with beta of 1.10, number of contracts needed would increase by this factor.
new_option: actual futures contract price = 2,700 × $250 = $675,000.

original_option: total return swap effectively removes risk associated with portion of equity portfolio allocation and converts it into money market equivalent returns; test of reasonableness: portfolio comprising 70% equities and 30% money market instruments assumed to earn 2% (1% over six months) would achieve return of 3.8% (= 0.7 × 5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or $3.8M on $100M in bullish scenario; in bearish scenario return would be -3.2% (= 0.7 × -5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or -$3.2M.
new_option: total return swap effectively removes risk associated with portion of equity portfolio allocation and converts it into money market equivalent returns.

original_option: effective beta of combined portfolio is calculated as combined return divided by market return; if combined position yields 2.2% return when market returns 2%, effective beta = 2.2%/2% = 1.10 which matches target beta; this verifies that hedge ratio formula worked correctly to achieve target market exposure.

if investor wants to increase portfolio's beta, β_T will exceed β_S and sign of N_f will be positive (must buy futures); if she wants to decrease beta, β_T will be less than β_S, sign of N_f will be negative (must sell futures); selling futures offsets some of risk of holding stock; buying futures adds risk; in special case of eliminating market risk β_T = 0 sign will always be negative — futures must be sold.
new_option: if investor wants to increase portfolio's beta, β_T will exceed β_S and sign of N_f will be positive (must buy futures).

original_option: long volatility exposure can be effective hedge against sell-off in long equity portfolio, notably during periods of extreme market movements; empirical studies have identified a negative correlation between volatility and stock index returns that becomes pronounced during stock market downturns; variance swaps have valuable convexity feature — as realized volatility increases (decreases), positive (negative) swap payoffs increase (decrease) — that makes them particularly attractive for hedging long equity portfolios.
new_option: long volatility exposure can be effective hedge against sell-off in long equity portfolio, notably during periods of extreme market movements.

original_option: CBOE Volatility Index or 'fear index' is a measure of investors' expectations of volatility in S&P 500 over next 30 days; calculated and published by Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) and based on prices of S&P 500 Index options; CBOE began publishing real-time VIX data in 1993, and in 2004 VIX futures were introduced; investors cannot invest directly in VIX but must use VIX futures contracts that offer investors pure play on level of expected stock market volatility regardless of direction of S&P 500.
new_option: CBOE Volatility Index or 'fear index' is a measure of investors' expectations of volatility in S&P 500 over next 30 days.

original_option: family of volatility indexes has been introduced for European equity markets designed to reflect market expectations of near-term to long-term volatility; most well known is VSTOXX index, based on real-time option prices on EURO STOXX 50 index; family also includes VDAX-NEW Index, based on DAX stock index options.
new_option: family of volatility indexes has been introduced for European equity markets designed to reflect market expectations of near-term to long-term volatility.

original_option: flat when volatility expected to remain stable over near to long term; backwardation typically signal that investors expect more volatility in short term and thus require higher prices for shorter-term contracts than for longer-term ones; contango is upward sloped curve — higher longer-term VIX futures prices interpreted as expectation VIX will rise because of increasing long-term volatility; shape reflects current volatility environment, investors' expectations, and buying/selling activity — can change from positively sloped to flat or inverted in months' time.
new_option: flat when volatility expected to remain stable over near to long term.
