original_option: VIX futures converge to spot VIX as expiration approaches, so two must be equal at expiration; when VIX futures curve is in contango (backwardation) and volatility expectations remain unchanged, VIX futures price will get 'pulled' closer to VIX spot price, decreasing (increasing) in price as they approach expiration; traders calculate daily roll as difference between front-month VIX futures price and VIX spot price divided by business days until VIX futures contract settles; assuming basis declines linearly until settlement, when term structure is in contango (backwardation), trader long in back-month VIX futures would realize roll-down losses (profits).
new_option: VIX futures converge to spot VIX as expiration approaches, so two must be equal at expiration.

original_option: in 2006 VIX options were introduced providing asymmetrical exposure to potential increases or decreases in anticipated volatility; VIX options are European style, and their prices depend on prices of VIX futures with similar expirations because market makers of VIX options typically hedge risk of their option positions using VIX futures; trader or investor would purchase VIX call options when he expects volatility will increase owing to significant sell-off in equity market; VIX put options would be bought to profit from expectation that volatility will decrease because of stable equity market conditions.
new_option: in 2006 VIX options were introduced providing asymmetrical exposure to potential increases or decreases in anticipated volatility.

original_option: instrument used by investors for taking directional bets on implied versus realized volatility for speculative or hedging purposes; term 'variance swap' refers to payoff analogous to that of a swap; buyer of contract will pay difference between fixed variance strike agreed on in contract and realized variance (annualized) on underlying over period specified applied to variance notional; no exchange of cash at contract inception or during life of swap; payoff at expiration of long variance position will be positive (negative) when realized variance is greater (less) than swap's variance strike.
new_option: instrument used by investors for taking directional bets on implied versus realized volatility for speculative or hedging purposes.

original_option: Settlement amount_T = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike); if payment amount is positive (negative), swap seller (buyer) pays swap buyer (seller); Realized variance = 252 × [Σ R_i² / (N-1)] where R_i = ln(P_i+1/P_i) and N is number of days observed.
new_option: Settlement amount_T = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike).

original_option: variance swap traders typically agree on: (1) variance swap trade size expressed in vega notional N_Vega (not in variance notional); (2) strike (X) which represents expected future variance of underlying, expressed as volatility (not variance); vega notional represents average profit and loss of variance swap for 1% change in volatility from strike; e.g., when vega notional is $50,000, profit and loss for one volatility point of difference between realized volatility and strike will be close to $50,000; Variance notional = Vega notional / (2 × Strike price); this is approximation because variance swap payoff is convex.
new_option: variance swap traders typically agree on: (1) variance swap trade size expressed in vega notional N_Vega (not in variance notional).

original_option: Variance notional = 50,000/(2 × 20) = 1,250; variance strike = 20² = 400; RealizedVol² = 16² = 256; ImpliedVol² = 19² = 361; PV factor = 1/(1 + 2.5% × 6/12) = 0.987654; VarSwap_t = 1,250 × 0.987654 × [(6/12) × 256 + (6/12) × 361 - 400] = -$112,962.93; since Santos is short, mark-to-market value is POSITIVE for her at $112,963.
new_option: Variance notional = 50,000/(2 × 20) = 1,250.

original_option: SettlementT = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike) = 1,250 × (18² - 20²) = 1,250 × (324 - 400) = -$95,000; since settlement is negative, swap buyer pays swap seller; Santos (seller) receives $95,000 from swap buyer.
new_option: SettlementT = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike) = 1,250 × (18² - 20²) = 1,250 × (324 - 400) = -$95,000.

original_option: variance swap payoff is convex in volatility; convexity occurs because being long a variance swap is equivalent to being long a basket of options and short the underlying asset (typically by selling a futures contract); long position in variance swap is thus long gamma and has convex payoff; this characteristic allows volatility sellers to sell variance swaps at higher price than at-the-money options because swap's convex payoff profile is attractive to investors who desire long volatility position as tail risk hedge.
new_option: variance swap payoff is convex in volatility.

original_option: sensitivity of a variance swap to changes in implied volatility diminishes over time; as swap approaches expiration, changes in implied volatility have less effect on swap value because remaining time period T - t decreases.
new_option: sensitivity of a variance swap to changes in implied volatility diminishes over time.

original_option: example: client holding 100,000 shares of Tundra at €14/share (position €1.4M) with low tax basis, exchange-traded options unavailable; solution: enter equity swap trading Tundra stock return for floating reference rate; agree to exchange total return on shares (price performance and dividends) for reference rate return on same sum of money; must determine time protection is needed and match swap tenor to that period; six-month tenor with reference rate 0.34% annually.
new_option: example: client holding 100,000 shares of Tundra at €14/share (position €1.4M) with low tax basis, exchange-traded options unavailable.

original_option: total return on stock = [(14 × 1.01) - 14 + 0.10]/14 = 1.71%; six-month reference rate return = 0.17% (half of 0.34%); Tundra total return exceeds reference rate: (1.71% - 0.17%) × €1.4M = €21,620 positive, so client pays swap counterparty €21,620.

total return on stock = [(14 × 0.99) - 14 + 0.10]/14 = -0.29%; Tundra total return less than six-month reference rate return: (-0.29% - 0.17%) × €1.4M = -€6,380 negative; client receives negative return + reference rate return from counterparty = positive cash inflow of €6,380.

N_f = (β_T/β_f)(S/F) = (1.0/1.0)(20,000,000/79,000) = 253.16 ≈ 253 contracts; McMillian buys 253 FTSE 100 futures to gain exposure on notional amount of £20M; both target and futures beta are 1.0; strategy attempts to minimize tracking error by replicating FTSE 100 performance on cash reserves.

FTSE 100 increases 5%: original £230M UK stocks increases to £241.5M; FTSE futures rise from £7,900 to £8,282.5; interest on cash at 0.63% floating rate = £20M × 0.63% × 90/360 = £31,500; futures profit = 382.5 index points × £10 × 253 contracts = £967,725; total portfolio value = £241.5M + £20M + £967,725 + £31,500 = £262,499,225; rate of return = £262,499,225/£250M - 1 = 5%; without strategy return would have been just over 4.6%.
new_option: FTSE 100 increases 5%: original £230M UK stocks increases to £241.5M.

original_option: €500M portfolio 70% stocks (all Italian, β=1.1 vs FTSE MIB) / 30% Italian govt bonds (BTPs, MDUR 6.45); target: 60% stocks split €150M Italian/€150M German (β 0.9 vs DAX) / 40% Italian bonds €200M; needs to remove €100M Italian stock exposure, add €50M German stock exposure, add €50M Italian bond exposure; can use futures on FTSE MIB, DAX, and BTP.
new_option: €500M portfolio 70% stocks (all Italian, β=1.1 vs FTSE MIB) / 30% Italian govt bonds (BTPs, MDUR 6.45).

original_option: want to reduce stock exposure by €3.6M (from €46M to €42.4M); N_f = [(0.0 - 1.2)/1.0] × (€3,604,000/€35,000) = -123.57 → sell 124 futures contracts (rounded).
new_option: want to reduce stock exposure by €3.6M (from €46M to €42.4M).

original_option: BPV_T = 9.5 × 0.0001 × €3,604,000 = €3,424; BPV_HR = [(BPV_T - 0)/BPV_CTD] × CF = [(€3,424 - 0)/€84.63] × 0.619489 = 25.06 ≈ 25 contracts; Grant should BUY 25 Euro-Bund futures contracts to gain €3.6M bond exposure at target duration 9.5.

derivatives can achieve targeted equity and interest rate risk exposures without disturbing underlying holdings; benefits include: preserving tax bases (avoiding realized gains), maintaining voting rights (no ownership transfer), avoiding transaction costs of physical rebalancing, achieving desired exposures quickly and efficiently, and reversing positions relatively easily; Rossi example: he wants to change allocations quickly, and TMM example: 'TMM decides to execute a series of swaps that would enable it to change its position temporarily but more easily and less expensively than by executing the physical transactions.'
new_option: derivatives can achieve targeted equity and interest rate risk exposures without disturbing underlying holdings.

original_option: e.g., TMM decides to increase allocation in large-cap sector by investing in S&P 500 Index, increase small-cap by investing in S&P SmallCap 600 Index (SPSC); to reduce allocation in fixed-income sector, replicate performance of Bloomberg Barclays US Treasury Index (BBT) for government bond sector and BofA Merrill Lynch US Corporate Index (BAMLC) for corporate; equity swap: receives total return of S&P 500, pays floating rate tied to MRR minus agreed-on spread; fixed-income swap: pays total return of BBT, receives floating payments tied to MRR minus agreed-on spread.
new_option: e.g., TMM decides to increase allocation in large-cap sector by investing in S&P 500 Index, increase small-cap by investing in S&P SmallCap 600 Index (SPSC).

original_option: transaction will not perfectly replicate performance of TMM's equity and fixed-income portfolios unless they are indexed to indexes selected as underlying of swaps; TMM could encounter cash flow problem if its fixed-income payments exceed its equity receipts and its portfolio does not generate enough cash to fund its net obligation; stock and bond portfolio will generate cash only from dividends and interest; capital gains will not be received in cash unless portion of portfolio is sold — but avoiding selling portion of portfolio is very reason why TMM wants to use swaps.
new_option: transaction will not perfectly replicate performance of TMM's equity and fixed-income portfolios unless they are indexed to indexes selected as underlying of swaps.

original_option: cash equitization used to minimize tracking error risk; portfolio manager without expectation on direction of underlying market implements cash equitization strategy attempting to replicate performance of relevant index on cash reserves; McMillian's motive was to accomplish goal of minimizing tracking error by following this strategy; comparing outcomes with and without strategy shows 5% return vs 4.6% return demonstrates strategy effectiveness.
new_option: cash equitization used to minimize tracking error risk.

original_option: TMM could decide to unwind position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing new swap with opposite payments for remainder of life of original swap; also if TMM wishes to extend period, would need to renegotiate swap at expiration; provides flexibility to reverse or extend positions as market conditions change.
new_option: TMM could decide to unwind position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing new swap with opposite payments for remainder of life of original swap.

original_option: modifying portfolio returns and risk exposures (hedging and directional bets) using interest rate/currency/equity swaps and futures, fixed-income futures, variance swaps; creating desired payoffs using forwards, futures, total return swaps; performing asset allocation and portfolio rebalancing using equity index futures, government bond futures, index swaps; inferring market expectations for interest rates, inflation, and volatility using Fed funds futures, inflation swaps, VIX futures.
new_option: modifying portfolio returns and risk exposures (hedging and directional bets) using interest rate/currency/equity swaps and futures, fixed-income futures, variance swaps.

original_option: Grant manages portfolio with target 40% stocks/60% bonds; portfolio grew from €100M to €106M so current €46M stocks (43.4%) and €60M bonds (56.6%); wants to rebalance back to target 40/60 (€42.4M stocks, €63.6M bonds); will use stock index futures and bond futures to achieve objective; once notional values to be traded are known, determines how many futures contracts should be purchased or sold to achieve desired asset allocation.
new_option: Grant manages portfolio with target 40% stocks/60% bonds.

original_option: commonly followed metric is probability of change in federal funds rate at upcoming FOMC meetings implied by prices of fed funds futures contracts; when US central bank began rate hiking cycle in 2015, declared intention to maintain 25 bp target range (lower and upper bounds) for federal funds rate; Fed regularly communicates 'forward guidance' along with 'dot plot' which shows where each FOMC meeting participant believes federal funds rate should be at end of year for next few years and in longer run; implied probabilities represent market's view and may diverge from Fed's guidance.
new_option: commonly followed metric is probability of change in federal funds rate at upcoming FOMC meetings implied by prices of fed funds futures contracts.

original_option: fed funds futures traded on Chicago Board of Trade; contract price quoted as 100 minus market's expectation for effective federal funds (FFE) rate: Fed funds futures contract price = 100 - Expected FFE rate; at expiration, contract is cash settled to simple average (overnight) effective federal funds rate for delivery month; overnight rate calculated and reported daily by Federal Reserve Bank of New York; underlying assumption is that implied futures market rates are predicting monthly average effective federal funds rate.

P(hike) = (FFE rate implied by futures - Current federal funds rate) / (Federal funds rate assuming rate hike - Current federal funds rate); current federal funds rate is midpoint of current target range.
new_option: P(hike) = (FFE rate implied by futures - Current federal funds rate) / (Federal funds rate assuming rate hike - Current federal funds rate).

original_option: FFE implied = 100 - 97.90 = 2.10%; current midpoint = 1.875%; assumed new range 2.00%-2.25% with midpoint 2.125%; P(hike) = (2.10% - 1.875%) / (2.125% - 1.875%) = 0.225/0.25 = 0.90 or 90%; high probability of 25 bp rate hike.

typical end-of-month (EOM) activity by large financial and banking institutions often induces 'dips' in FFE rate that create bias issues when using rate as basis for probability calculations of potential FOMC rate moves; if such activity increased price for relevant fed funds futures contract to 98.05, FFE rate would decline to 1.95% (= 100 - 98.05); using same equation, probability of FOMC rate hike decreases from 90% to just 30%: (1.950 - 1.875)/0.25 = 0.30; data providers implement smoothing methods.

one prominent data provider uses method that builds forward rate structure based on where market believes interest rates will settle in non-FOMC meeting months and then uses these forward rates to make appropriate adjustments; for each FOMC meeting month, assumed that effective rate prevails until meeting date and then some rate prevails after meeting, with average effective rate over month being implied by futures price.
new_option: one prominent data provider uses method that builds forward rate structure based on where market believes interest rates will settle in non-FOMC meeting months and then uses these forward rates to make appropriate adjustments.

original_option: implied market expectations can diverge significantly from Fed's forward guidance; implied probabilities represent market's view and may diverge from guidance provided by central banks in their regular communications about likely future course of monetary policy actions; especially for longer-term horizon, these inferred probabilities usually do not have strong predictive power; the 'dot plot' shows where each FOMC meeting participant believes federal funds rate should be at end of year for next few years and in longer run.
new_option: implied market expectations can diverge significantly from Fed's forward guidance.

original_option: important to emphasize that inferences from derivatives relate to current expectations of future events — they do NOT foretell what will actually happen — which can change with the arrival of new information; useful for gauging market sentiment and pricing expectations but not for predicting actual outcomes with certainty.
new_option: important to emphasize that inferences from derivatives relate to current expectations of future events — they do NOT foretell what will actually happen — which can change with the arrival of new information.

original_option: probability of rate hike or cut is represented as probability of move from current target range at specified meeting date, and probabilities of all three actions (hike/no change/cut) at a particular meeting date sum to 1; data providers show trends in implied probabilities over time.
new_option: probability of rate hike or cut is represented as probability of move from current target range at specified meeting date, and probabilities of all three actions (hike/no change/cut) at a particular meeting date sum to 1.

original_option: TMM invested $200M as 75% stocks, 25% bonds; wants 90% stocks/10% bonds; equity target: $27M more S&P 500 (large-cap 60%→65%), $3M more S&P SmallCap 600 (10% unchanged); bond target: sell $25M BBT (govt), sell $5M BAMLC (corporate); TMM structures swaps to have all payments occur on same dates six months apart with maturity of one year; if desired to extend, must renegotiate at expiration; unwinding possible by opposite swap.
new_option: TMM invested $200M as 75% stocks, 25% bonds.

original_option: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) - 1 where R_FC is foreign-currency return on foreign asset and R_FX is percentage change (appreciation) of foreign currency against domestic currency; two components are multiplicative not additive.
new_option: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) - 1 where R_FC is foreign-currency return on foreign asset and R_FX is percentage change (appreciation) of foreign currency against domestic currency.

original_option: R_DC = Σ ω_i × (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) - 1 where ω_i are portfolio weights (measured in common currency, the domestic currency) that sum to 1; if short selling is allowed some of ω_i can be less than zero; exchange rate notation must be consistently defined with domestic currency as price currency.
new_option: R_DC = Σ ω_i × (1 + R_FC,i)(1 + R_FX,i) - 1 where ω_i are portfolio weights (measured in common currency, the domestic currency) that sum to 1.

original_option: when R_FC and R_FX are small, cross-term R_FC × R_FX is small so R_DC ≈ R_FC + R_FX; then variance approximation σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ(R_FC, R_FX); risk more typically defined in terms of standard deviation because mean and standard deviation measured in same units.
new_option: when R_FC and R_FX are small, cross-term R_FC × R_FX is small so R_DC ≈ R_FC + R_FX.

original_option: because Equation 5 is based on addition of all three terms on right side of equal sign, exchange rate exposure will generally cause variance of domestic-currency returns σ²(R_DC) to increase to more than that of foreign-currency returns σ²(R_FC) considered on their own; if there was no exchange rate risk, then σ²(R_DC) = σ²(R_FC); using this as base case, adding exchange rate risk exposure to portfolio usually adds to domestic-currency return variance (effect is indeterminate if exchange rate movements are negatively correlated with foreign asset returns).
new_option: because Equation 5 is based on addition of all three terms on right side of equal sign, exchange rate exposure will generally cause variance of domestic-currency returns σ²(R_DC) to increase to more than that of foreign-currency returns σ²(R_FC) considered on their own.

original_option: σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2σ(R_FC)σ(R_FX)ρ = (3%)² + (2%)² + 2(3%)(2%)(0.50) = 9 + 4 + 6 = 19 in %² units = 0.0019; taking square root: σ(R_DC) ≈ 4.36%.

domestic-currency risk exposure of overall portfolio σ(R_DC) will depend not only on variances of each of the foreign-currency returns (R_FC) and exchange rate movements (R_FX) but also on how each of these interacts with the others; generally speaking, negative correlations among these variables will help reduce overall portfolio's risk through diversification effects; if short-selling is allowed some ω_i can be negative — for two foreign assets with strong positive return correlation, short selling one can create considerable diversification benefits (equivalent to trading movements in price spread).
new_option: domestic-currency risk exposure of overall portfolio σ(R_DC) will depend not only on variances of each of the foreign-currency returns (R_FC) and exchange rate movements (R_FX) but also on how each of these interacts with the others.

original_option: one camp holds that in the long run currency effects cancel out to zero as exchange rates revert to historical means or fundamental values; efficient currency market is zero-sum game — no long-run gains overall to speculating in currencies, especially after netting management/transaction costs — so both currency hedging and actively trading currencies represent a cost to portfolio with little prospect of consistently positive active returns; other camp notes currency movements can have dramatic impact on short-run returns and return volatility and there are pricing inefficiencies — much of currency flow is need-to-do basis, some participants not profit-oriented (central banks, gov agencies) or are 'uninformed traders' (retail); marketplace diversity presents potential for 'harvesting alpha' through active trading.
new_option: one camp holds that in the long run currency effects cancel out to zero as exchange rates revert to historical means or fundamental values.

original_option: IPS mandates degree of discretionary currency management allowed in portfolio, how it will be benchmarked, and limits on type of trading policies and tools (e.g., leverage) that can be used; usually specifies general objectives of investment portfolio, risk tolerance and capacity for bearing risk, time horizon, income/liquidity needs, and benchmark; currency risk management policy addresses target proportion of currency exposure to be passively hedged, latitude for active currency management around target, frequency of hedge rebalancing, currency hedge performance benchmark, and hedging tools permitted.
new_option: IPS mandates degree of discretionary currency management allowed in portfolio, how it will be benchmarked, and limits on type of trading policies and tools (e.g., leverage) that can be used.

original_option: many portfolio managers handle asset allocation with currency risk as two-step process: (1) portfolio optimization over fully hedged returns; (2) selection of active currency exposure, if any; derivative strategies allow various risk exposures in portfolio to be 'unbundled' from each other and managed separately; use of derivatives allows price risk R_FC,i and exchange rate risk R_FX,j of foreign-currency assets to be unbundled and managed separately; starting point is complete currency hedge — portfolio manager will choose exposures to foreign-currency assets first, then decide on appropriate currency exposures afterward.
new_option: many portfolio managers handle asset allocation with currency risk as two-step process: (1) portfolio optimization over fully hedged returns.

original_option: time horizon of IPS important; many practitioners believe that in long run adding unhedged foreign-currency exposure does not affect expected long-run portfolio returns — hence in long run would not matter if portfolio was hedged (indeed portfolio management costs would be reduced without hedging); belief based on view that in long run currencies 'mean revert' to fair value equilibrium level or historical average — expected %ΔS = 0 for sufficiently long time period; view typically draws on expectation that purchasing power parity (PPP) and other international parity conditions will eventually hold; investor with very long investment horizon and few immediate liquidity needs might choose to forgo currency hedging.
new_option: time horizon of IPS important.

original_option: often asserted that correlation between foreign-currency returns and foreign-currency asset returns tends to be GREATER for fixed-income portfolios than for equity portfolios; makes intuitive sense: both bonds and currencies react strongly to movements in interest rates whereas equities respond more to expected earnings; implication is that currency exposures provide LITTLE diversification benefit to fixed-income portfolios and that currency risk should be hedged; better argument can be made for carrying currency exposures in global equity portfolios; still more likely to see currency hedging for fixed-income portfolios rather than equity portfolios.
new_option: often asserted that correlation between foreign-currency returns and foreign-currency asset returns tends to be GREATER for fixed-income portfolios than for equity portfolios.

original_option: some currencies — USD, JPY, and CHF in particular — seem to act as safe haven and appreciate in times of market stress; keeping some of these currency exposures in portfolio (hedge ratios not set at 100%) can help hedge losses on riskier assets, especially for foreign-currency equity portfolios (which are more risk exposed than bond portfolios); Campbell/Serfaty-de Medeiros/Viceira (2007) found USD tended to appreciate against foreign currencies when global bond prices fell — for non-US investors this would mean under-hedging exposure to USD (hedge ratio less than 100%); for US investors would mean over-hedging their foreign-currency exposures back into USD.
new_option: some currencies — USD, JPY, and CHF in particular — seem to act as safe haven and appreciate in times of market stress.

original_option: hedging costs come mainly in two forms: trading costs and opportunity costs; most immediate costs of hedging involve trading expenses in several forms: trading involves dealing on bid-offer spread offered by banks — profit margin based on spreads, more the client trades and 'pays away the spread' more profit is generated by dealer; maintaining 100% hedge and rebalancing frequently with every minor move will be expensive; currency hedges not a 'free good'; optimal hedging decisions balance benefits of hedging against these costs.
new_option: hedging costs come mainly in two forms: trading costs and opportunity costs.

original_option: there are a variety of approaches to currency management ranging from trying to avoid all currency risk in portfolio to actively seeking foreign exchange risk in order to manage it and enhance portfolio returns; no firm consensus about most effective way to manage currency risk; some investment managers try to hedge all currency risk, some leave portfolios unhedged, others see currency risk as potential source of incremental return and actively trade FX; widely varying management practices reflect variety of factors including investment objectives, investment constraints, and beliefs about currency markets.
new_option: there are a variety of approaches to currency management ranging from trying to avoid all currency risk in portfolio to actively seeking foreign exchange risk in order to manage it and enhance portfolio returns.

original_option: passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures; discretionary hedging which allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures; active currency management which seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit; currency overlay programs that aggressively manage currency 'alpha'.
new_option: passive, rules-based, complete hedging of currency exposures.

original_option: passive hedging is rules-based approach to hedging currency exposures completely; portfolio matches its currency exposures to those of benchmark portfolio; hedging costs and opportunities for gain from active management are set aside in favor of avoiding currency risk; requires periodic rebalancing to maintain hedge as underlying values change.
new_option: passive hedging is rules-based approach to hedging currency exposures completely.

original_option: discretionary hedging allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures around a benchmark; permits manager to leave positions unhedged or partially hedged when doing so is expected to be advantageous based on market view; typical mandate might specify hedge ratio target with allowed range around it (e.g., 100% target with ±10% latitude); manager must operate within IPS constraints.
new_option: discretionary hedging allows portfolio manager some latitude on managing currency exposures around a benchmark.

original_option: seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit; requires manager to have market view and derives returns from taking positions consistent with that view; can be based on economic fundamentals, technical analysis, carry trade, or volatility trading; still operates within IPS-mandated risk parameters but has substantial latitude to construct positions distinct from benchmark; goal is to generate positive active return through currency management.
new_option: seeks out currency risk in order to manage it for profit.

original_option: aggressive management of currency alpha; entrusts currency exposures to specialized currency manager who is granted wide discretion; goal is to generate currency alpha separately from underlying asset portfolio; overlay manager may take positions outside portfolio's initial currency exposures; distinct from mainstream portfolio management and requires specific expertise.
new_option: aggressive management of currency alpha.

original_option: portfolio's currency management program depends on: general objectives of portfolio; risk tolerance and capacity for bearing risk; time horizon (longer horizons may support less hedging due to mean reversion); ongoing income/liquidity needs (higher liquidity needs may support more hedging to avoid forced liquidation at bad rates); benchmark against which portfolio measures returns.
new_option: portfolio's currency management program depends on: general objectives of portfolio.

original_option: market conditions affect cost/benefit calculations behind hedging decision — movements in forward points (expected roll yield) or exchange rate volatility (option premiums) affect expected cost of hedge; same hedge structure can be 'rich' or 'cheap' depending on current market conditions; when forward points move against hedger, amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates; opposite occurs when movements in forward points reduce hedging costs.
new_option: market conditions affect cost/benefit calculations behind hedging decision — movements in forward points (expected roll yield) or exchange rate volatility (option premiums) affect expected cost of hedge.

original_option: there is no single 'correct' approach to initiating and managing currency exposures; different managers likely make different decisions when confronted with same opportunity set; each manager will likely have good reason for their individual decision; most important point is that portfolio manager be aware of all benefits, costs, and risks of chosen strategy and be comfortable that any remaining residual currency risks in hedge are acceptable.
new_option: there is no single 'correct' approach to initiating and managing currency exposures.

original_option: at heart of approach is assumption that in flexible exchange rate system exchange rates are determined by logical economic relationships that can be modeled; simple economic framework based on assumption that in long run real exchange rate will converge to 'fair value' but short- to medium-term factors will shape convergence path; long-run equilibrium level for real exchange rate determined by purchasing power parity or some other model of exchange rate's fair value.
new_option: at heart of approach is assumption that in flexible exchange rate system exchange rates are determined by logical economic relationships that can be modeled.

original_option: all else equal, base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is upward movement in: (1) its long-run equilibrium real exchange rate; (2) either its real or nominal interest rates, which should attract foreign capital; (3) expected foreign inflation, which should cause foreign currency to depreciate; (4) the foreign risk premium, which should make foreign assets less attractive compared with base currency nation's domestic assets; real exchange rate should also increase if currently below its long-term equilibrium value.
new_option: all else equal, base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if there is upward movement in: (1) its long-run equilibrium real exchange rate.

original_option: (1) market technicians believe that in liquid freely traded market historical price data can be helpful in projecting future price movements — reason: many traders already used useful external data to generate positions so this information is already reflected in current prices; (2) historical patterns in price data have tendency to repeat and this repetition provides profitable trade opportunities — price patterns repeat because market prices reflect human behavior; (3) technical analysis does not attempt to determine where market prices should trade (fair value) but where they will trade — market may eventually converge to fair value in long run but shorter-term trading opportunities available.
new_option: (1) market technicians believe that in liquid freely traded market historical price data can be helpful in projecting future price movements — reason: many traders already used useful external data to generate positions so this information is already reflected in current prices.

original_option: price points on dealers' order boards where one would expect to see clustering of bids and offers respectively; at these exchange rate levels, price action expected to get 'sticky' because will take more order flow to pierce wall of either bids or offers; but once these price points are breached, price action can be expected to accelerate as stops are triggered (stops = stop-loss orders); often use technical indicators — 200-day moving average often seen as important support/resistance; when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average sometimes seen as price 'break out' point.
new_option: price points on dealers' order boards where one would expect to see clustering of bids and offers respectively.

original_option: trading strategy of borrowing in low-yield currencies and investing in high-yield currencies; term 'carry' related to cost of carry — that is, carrying or holding an investment; investment has either implicit or explicit cost (borrowing cost) but may also produce income; net cost of carry is difference between these two return rates; based on exploiting well-recognized violation of one of international parity conditions: uncovered interest rate parity.
new_option: trading strategy of borrowing in low-yield currencies and investing in high-yield currencies.

original_option: persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity described by carry trade is often referred to as forward rate bias; implication of uncovered interest rate parity is that forward rate should be unbiased predictor of future spot rates; historical data show that forward rate is NOT center of distribution for future spot rates — in fact is biased predictor (hence name 'forward rate bias'); with forward rate premium or discount defined as F_P/B - S_P/B, the 'bias' is that premium typically overstates amount of appreciation of base currency and discount overstates amount of depreciation; forward discount or premium often gets even direction of future spot rate movements wrong.
new_option: persistent violation of uncovered interest rate parity described by carry trade is often referred to as forward rate bias.

original_option: carry trade strategy (borrowing low-yield, investing high-yield) is equivalent to strategy based on trading forward rate bias; trading forward rate bias involves buying currencies trading at forward discount and selling currencies trading at forward premium; from covered interest rate parity, being low-yield currency and trading at forward premium is synonymous; being high-yield currency means trading at forward discount; borrowing in low-yield currency (=selling forward premium currency) and investing in high-yield currency (=buying forward discount currency); makes intuitive sense: 'buy low and sell high'.
new_option: carry trade strategy (borrowing low-yield, investing high-yield) is equivalent to strategy based on trading forward rate bias.

original_option: occasional large losses mean return distribution for carry trade has pronounced NEGATIVE skew; negative skew derives from fact that funding currencies (low-yield currencies where borrowing occurs) are typically safe haven currencies such as EUR, CHF, and JPY; investment currencies (high-yielding currencies) are typically currencies perceived to be higher risk, such as several emerging market currencies; any time global financial markets under stress there is flight to safety causing rapid movements in exchange rates and usually panicked unwinding of carry trades; carry trade sometimes characterized as 'picking up nickels in front of a steamroller'.
new_option: occasional large losses mean return distribution for carry trade has pronounced NEGATIVE skew.

original_option: type of active trading style unique to option markets; use of options allows trader through variety of trading strategies to unbundle and isolate all various risk factors (the Greeks) and trade them separately; once initial option position taken, trader has exposure to all Greeks; unwanted risk exposures can be hedged away, leaving only desired risk exposure to express specific directional view; volatility trading focuses on vega — sensitivity of option premium to small change in implied volatility (indication of volatility risk); trader expresses view on future movements in implied volatility, not the direction of underlying.
new_option: type of active trading style unique to option markets.

original_option: long straddle = long ATM put + long ATM call (both same strike); deltas -0.5 and +0.5 so net delta of position is zero (delta neutral); profitable in more volatile markets when either put or call go sufficiently in the money to cover upfront cost of two option premiums; long strangle = long OTM put + long OTM call (same expiry, same degree of being out of money); because OTM options being bought, cost is cheaper — but conversely does not pay off until spot rate passes OTM strike levels; risk-reward for strangle more moderate than straddle.
new_option: long straddle = long ATM put + long ATM call (both same strike).

original_option: changes in yield differential between currencies alter attractiveness of carry trade; if high-yield currency's rate increases while low-yield currency's rate remains stable, yield advantage of high-yield currency widens making carry trade more attractive; long periods of yield differential persistence between high-yield and low-yield currencies encourage build-up of carry trade positions; positive returns can remain in place long enough to present attractive investment opportunities; guide to riskiness is volatility of outright forward (not spot) rate movements — lower volatility is better for carry trade position.
new_option: changes in yield differential between currencies alter attractiveness of carry trade.

original_option: if authorities are expected to tighten monetary policy and change not fully priced into market, currency of tightening country would be expected to appreciate; increase in real or nominal interest rates should attract foreign capital, supporting base currency; surprise rate hike would cause currency to appreciate against most currencies; tactical trading decisions should reflect market view of forthcoming rate action — traders may position in options that profit from expected direction of movement.
new_option: if authorities are expected to tighten monetary policy and change not fully priced into market, currency of tightening country would be expected to appreciate.

original_option: if country's expected inflation rises relative to another country's, its currency should depreciate; because base currency's real exchange rate should appreciate if expected foreign inflation rises (which should cause foreign currency to depreciate); if analyst expects inflation to accelerate in New Zealand while Japan's remains stable, JPY/NZD should decrease (NZD depreciate against JPY); market view of relative inflation is key input for fundamentals-based currency views.
new_option: if country's expected inflation rises relative to another country's, its currency should depreciate.

original_option: if trader expects exchange rate volatility to increase without directional view, appropriate response is to buy volatility (e.g., long straddle or long strangle); if volatility expected to increase and CLP/USD expected to become much more volatile but no strong view on direction, buying options provides positive exposure to vega; option premiums increase with implied volatility, so if trader can position before increase in implied volatility, can benefit from repricing.
new_option: if trader expects exchange rate volatility to increase without directional view, appropriate response is to buy volatility (e.g., long straddle or long strangle).

original_option: when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average, sometimes seen as price 'break out' point; if current spot rate is close to 200-day moving average, that level acts as key support/resistance; technicians would time position entry/exit based on chart patterns and moving average signals; when JPY/NZD price at 77.1905 and 200-day moving average at 77.5035, chart shows spot rate below moving average — moving average acts as resistance to further upward move.
new_option: when 50-day moving average crosses 200-day moving average, sometimes seen as price 'break out' point.

original_option: look for widest yield differential to maximize carry; from Example 3 data (Switzerland -0.80%, US 0.30%, Poland 1.26%, Mexico 5.98%): funding in CHF (lowest yield -0.80%) and investing in MXN (highest yield 5.98%) gives largest yield differential of 6.78%; alternative funding in USD investing in MXN gives smaller 5.68%; funding CHF investing PLN gives only 2.06%; largest positive spread offers best carry potential (subject to volatility considerations).
new_option: look for widest yield differential to maximize carry.

original_option: when trader has directional view expecting significant move, deep-OTM options provide leveraged exposure — larger percentage change in premium for given change in spot; low-delta option (5-delta or 10-delta) is 'like highly leveraged lottery ticket on event occurring'; with surprise event odds swing in favor; if surprise rate hike expected to cause NZD appreciation, put option on NZD/AUD (which would depreciate as NZD is base currency and appreciates) would benefit — 5-delta put option has more extreme price reaction on premiums than higher-delta option.
new_option: when trader has directional view expecting significant move, deep-OTM options provide leveraged exposure — larger percentage change in premium for given change in spot.

original_option: exchange rate forecast is mix of long-term, medium-term, and short-term factors; long-run equilibrium real exchange rate is anchor for exchange rates and point of long-run convergence for exchange rate movements; movements in short- to medium-term factors (nominal interest rates, expected inflation) affect timing and path of convergence to long-run equilibrium; can be very demanding to model how each of these separate effects change over time and affect exchange rates.
new_option: exchange rate forecast is mix of long-term, medium-term, and short-term factors.

original_option: simultaneous spot and forward transaction — one leg is buying the base currency and the other is selling it; FX swaps are used to renew outstanding forward contracts once they mature, to 'roll them forward'; rolling forward hedging position involves selling base currency at then-current spot exchange rate to settle current forward contract, and then going long another far-dated forward contract.
new_option: simultaneous spot and forward transaction — one leg is buying the base currency and the other is selling it.

original_option: magnitude of roll yield is |(F_P/B - S_P/B)/S_P/B|; sign depends on whether investor needs to buy or sell base currency forward to maintain hedge; positive roll yield results from buying base currency at forward discount OR selling it at forward premium (intuition: profitable to 'buy low and sell high'); otherwise roll yield is negative (positive cost).
new_option: magnitude of roll yield is |(F_P/B - S_P/B)/S_P/B|.

original_option: if hedge requires buying base currency at forward premium: pay forward price of A; as time passes price of forward contract 'rolls down the curve' toward Price B as contract's settlement date approaches; at settlement roll forward hedge by selling base currency at then-current spot rate (Price B, lower than A) and going long another far-dated forward contract; buying high (Price A) and selling low (Price B) will be cost to portfolio — roll yield negative; the larger the gap between A and B at contract initiation, the less likely to be positive.
new_option: if hedge requires buying base currency at forward premium: pay forward price of A.

original_option: carry trade (borrow low-yield, invest high-yield) is equivalent to trading forward rate bias (buy forward discount currency, sell forward premium currency) and to earning positive roll yield; negative roll yield typically indicates hedger was trading against forward rate bias — buying currency at forward premium or selling at forward discount; hedger with negative roll yield is also essentially entering into negative carry trade, in effect borrowing at high rates and investing at low rates; on average, this will not be a winning strategy.
new_option: carry trade (borrow low-yield, invest high-yield) is equivalent to trading forward rate bias (buy forward discount currency, sell forward premium currency) and to earning positive roll yield.

original_option: when forward points move against hedger amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates; opposite occurs when movements in forward points reduce hedging costs; tendency to hedge will vary depending on whether implementing hedge trades in same direction as forward rate bias strategy or against it; if setting up hedge involves selling low-yield currency and buying high-yield currency (implicit carry position), more likely portfolio will be fully hedged or over-hedged; trading against forward rate bias likely to lead to lower hedge ratios.
new_option: when forward points move against hedger amount of hedging activity typically declines as cost/benefit ratio deteriorates.

original_option: long GBP against HKD; HKD/GBP selling at small forward discount of 0.099% vs current spot — this is expected roll yield, NOT in firm's favor because to implement hedge Kwun Tong would sell GBP (base currency) at price lower than current spot; but market strategist expects GBP to depreciate by 3.77% against HKD; negative carry of hedging is small in comparison to potential loss; both considerations argue for hedging this exposure.
new_option: long GBP against HKD; HKD/GBP selling at small forward discount of 0.099% vs current spot — this is expected roll yield, NOT in firm's favor because to implement hedge Kwun Tong would sell GBP (base currency) at price lower than current spot.

original_option: long ZAR against HKD; HKD/ZAR selling at forward discount of -2.5% vs current spot; would require selling ZAR (base currency) at lower price than spot; roll yield goes against firm — expected cost of hedge = 2.5%; strategist forecasts ZAR depreciation against HKD of 2.11%; risk-neutral investor would NOT hedge because expected cost of hedge (2.5%) more than expected depreciation (2.11%); but point forecast comes with uncertainty; decision depends on trade-offs between (1) level of risk aversion of firm and (2) conviction firm held in currency forecast.
new_option: long ZAR against HKD; HKD/ZAR selling at forward discount of -2.5% vs current spot.

original_option: when setting hedge ratio, portfolio manager must balance effect of expected future exchange rate movements on portfolio returns against expected effect of roll yield (expected cost of hedge); risk-neutral manager would not hedge if net expected value of hedge is negative; risk-averse manager might still implement hedge regardless of negative net expected value because it is possible market forecast is wrong and actual depreciation may be higher than cost of hedge; risk-averse manager must weigh certainty of hedge cost against risk that actual unhedged currency losses might be higher.
new_option: when setting hedge ratio, portfolio manager must balance effect of expected future exchange rate movements on portfolio returns against expected effect of roll yield (expected cost of hedge).

original_option: long position in underlying with put option — matching long position in underlying with put option is known as protective put strategy; if manager is long base currency and needs to sell to implement hedge, buy ATM put option on P/B currency pair; if P/B rate goes down (P appreciates, B depreciates) by expiry, manager can exercise option, implement hedge, and guarantee selling price at strike; if P/B rate increases (P depreciates, B appreciates), manager can simply let option expire and collect currency gains; option premium is upfront cost.
new_option: long position in underlying with put option — matching long position in underlying with put option is known as protective put strategy.

original_option: way to reduce cost of using options is to accept some downside risk by using OTM option, such as 25- or 10-delta option; these options will be less costly but also do NOT fully protect portfolio from adverse currency movements; makes sense to insure against larger risks but accept some smaller day-to-day price movements in currencies; analogous to home/car insurance with deductible: cheaper insurance policy accepting responsibility for minor events but insuring against extreme damage.
new_option: way to reduce cost of using options is to accept some downside risk by using OTM option, such as 25- or 10-delta option.

original_option: one strategy to obtain downside protection at lower cost than straight protective put: buy OTM put option and write OTM call option — selling some upside potential (writing call) using option premiums to help pay cost of long put; similar to creating collar in fixed-income markets; portfolio protected against downside movements but upside limited to strike price on OTM call option — exchange rate risk confined to corridor or 'collar'; in professional FX markets, long position in call option + short position in put option is called risk reversal; buying 25-delta call and writing 25-delta put = long position in 25-delta risk reversal; the collar structure (buying put, writing call) is SHORT position in risk reversal.
new_option: one strategy to obtain downside protection at lower cost than straight protective put: buy OTM put option and write OTM call option — selling some upside potential (writing call) using option premiums to help pay cost of long put.

original_option: variation of short risk reversal position; used to reduce upfront cost of buying protective put; buy put option and write another put option to help cover cost of long put's premiums; typically structured by buying OTM put, and writing deeper-OTM put; both options same maturity; example: current spot 1.3550, buy put with strike 1.3500 and write put with strike 1.3450 — no hedge protection between 1.3550 and 1.3500; portfolio hedged from 1.3500 down to 1.3450; at spot rates below 1.3450 portfolio becomes unhedged again; reduces cost of hedge but at cost of more limited downside protection.
new_option: variation of short risk reversal position.

original_option: to make put spread cheaper or zero-cost, portfolio manager could write larger notional amount for deeper-OTM option — e.g., ratio for notionals for options written vs bought might be 1:2; in standard FX market notation this would be 1 × 2 put spread (option with exercise price closest to being ATM given first); may now be approximately zero-cost but not without risks: for spot rates below deep-OTM strike portfolio has now seen exposure to base currency double-up (because of 1:2 proportion of notionals) and at worse spot rate; creating zero-cost structure with 1 × 2 put spread is equivalent to adding LEVERAGE — selling more options than buying; requires careful management.
new_option: to make put spread cheaper or zero-cost, portfolio manager could write larger notional amount for deeper-OTM option — e.g., ratio for notionals for options written vs bought might be 1:2.

original_option: extension of risk reversal and put spread; core of hedge is long position in put option; to reduce cost, short risk reversal writes call option while put spread writes deep-OTM put option; manager can do both: long protective put and write both call and deep-OTM put; option structure sometimes referred to as seagull spread; 'seagull' indicates option structure with at least three individual options, options at most distant strikes (wings) on opposite side of market from middle strike(s) (body); if current spot 1.3550: long ATM put at 1.3550 (body), short OTM put at 1.3500, short OTM call at 1.3600 (wings) = short seagull; provides full downside protection from 1.3550 to 1.3500 and upside participation to 1.3600.
new_option: extension of risk reversal and put spread.

original_option: knock-in option = vanilla option that only comes into existence when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified level (trigger level, called 'barrier', not same as strike price); knock-out option = vanilla option that ceases to exist when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified barrier level; because these options only exist under certain circumstances, they are more restrictive than vanilla options and hence are CHEAPER; but knock-in/out features provide less upside potential and/or downside protection.
new_option: knock-in option = vanilla option that only comes into existence when spot exchange rate touches pre-specified level (trigger level, called 'barrier', not same as strike price).

original_option: also called binary options or all-or-nothing options; expiry value of in-the-money vanilla option varies based on amount of difference between expiry level and strike price; in contrast, digital options pay out a FIXED amount if they are determined to be in-the-money; American digital options pay fixed amount if they 'touch' their exercise level at any time before expiry (even if by single pip); characteristic of 'extreme payoff' options makes them almost akin to lottery ticket; because of these large payoffs, digital options usually cost MORE than vanilla options with same strike price; digitals provide highly leveraged exposure to movements in spot rate — more appropriate as trading tools for active currency management rather than as hedging tools.
new_option: also called binary options or all-or-nothing options.

original_option: for manager with long exposure to currency, cost of 'core' hedge (short position in forward or upfront premium on long put) can be reduced by: (a) writing options to gain upfront premiums; (b) varying strike prices of options written or bought; (c) varying notional amounts of derivative contracts; keep in mind lower hedging costs equate to less downside protection and/or upside potential; higher allowed discretion for active management, lower risk aversion, and firmer market view lead to hedge structured to allow risk exposures — positioning in derivatives that 'lean the same way' as market view.
new_option: for manager with long exposure to currency, cost of 'core' hedge (short position in forward or upfront premium on long put) can be reduced by: (a) writing options to gain upfront premiums.

original_option: second determinant of option's premium is time value, heavily influenced by volatility in exchange rates; regardless of exchange rate volatility, options are always moving toward expiry; in general, time value of option is always declining — time decay of option's value (theta, one of Greeks) similar in concept to negative roll yield on forward contracts; time decay always works against owner of an option; means long option positions have known ongoing cost even in stable markets.
new_option: second determinant of option's premium is time value, heavily influenced by volatility in exchange rates.

original_option: hedging one currency exposure using derivative contract in different but correlated currency; useful when direct hedge is unavailable or too expensive; e.g., can use long-short positions in positively correlated currencies to achieve much lower portfolio risk through benefits of cross hedging; requires appropriate correlation between currency being hedged and hedging instrument; some types of cross hedges often referred to as macro hedges.
new_option: hedging one currency exposure using derivative contract in different but correlated currency.

original_option: cross hedge focused on entire portfolio rather than individual assets or currency pairs, particularly when individual asset price movements are highly correlated; another way of viewing macro hedge: seeing portfolio not just as collection of financial assets but as collection of risk exposures — various risk exposures typically defined in categories such as term risk, credit risk, liquidity risk; can also be defined in terms of financial scenarios portfolio is exposed to (recession, financial sector stress, inflation); often macro hedges defined in terms of financial scenario they protect portfolio from; e.g., putting gold in portfolio, using volatility overlay program, using derivative based on index rather than specific assets or currencies.
new_option: cross hedge focused on entire portfolio rather than individual assets or currency pairs, particularly when individual asset price movements are highly correlated.

original_option: mathematical approach to determining optimal cross hedging ratio using OLS regression; regression y_t = α + β x_t + ε_t used to minimize variance of ε; y = percentage change in value of asset to be hedged, x = percentage change in value of hedging instrument (both in domestic currency terms); minimum-variance hedge ratio (β̂) = correlation(y,x) × [std.dev(y) / std.dev(x)] = covariance(y,x)/variance(x); calculated coefficient gives optimal hedging ratio minimizing tracking error.
new_option: mathematical approach to determining optimal cross hedging ratio using OLS regression.

original_option: 10 years monthly data on CHF/GBP spot and SMI; McYelland inverts CHF/GBP to GBP/CHF so domestic currency is price; regresses R_DC = α + β(%ΔS_GBP/CHF) + ε; result β̂ = 1.35 = minimum-variance hedge ratio; makes sense because β̂ represents sensitivity of domestic-currency return on portfolio to percentage changes in spot rate; SMI seems very sensitive to appreciation of CHF; correlation +0.6 between %ΔSMI and %ΔS_GBP/CHF; recommends minimum-variance hedge ratio set at approximately 135% — long CHF1M SMI exposure hedged with short CHF/GBP position of CHF1.35M.
new_option: 10 years monthly data on CHF/GBP spot and SMI.

original_option: calculating minimum-variance hedge ratio typically applies only for 'indirect' hedges based on cross hedging or macro hedges; NOT typically applied to 'direct' hedge in which exposure to spot rate is hedged with forward contract in that same currency pair; correlation between movements in spot rate and its forward contract likely very close to +1; variance in spot price movements and movements in forward contract price also approximately equal; therefore β̂ regression estimate very close to 1 and minimum-variance hedge ratio close to 100% — undertaking regression is superfluous.
new_option: calculating minimum-variance hedge ratio typically applies only for 'indirect' hedges based on cross hedging or macro hedges.

original_option: any time direct currency hedge (spot rate hedged against own forward contract) is replaced with indirect hedge (cross hedge, macro hedge), basis risk is brought into portfolio; reflects fact that price movements in exposure being hedged and price movements in cross hedge instrument are not perfectly correlated; correlation will change with time and sometimes both dramatically and unexpectedly; for minimum-variance hedge ratio, risk expressed as instability in β̂ coefficient estimate as more data become available.
new_option: any time direct currency hedge (spot rate hedged against own forward contract) is replaced with indirect hedge (cross hedge, macro hedge), basis risk is brought into portfolio.

original_option: one macro hedge specific to foreign exchange markets uses derivatives based on fixed-weight baskets of currencies (available in both exchange-traded and OTC form); in multi-currency portfolio may not always be cost efficient to hedge each single currency separately — macro hedge using currency basket derivatives is alternative approach; can protect portfolio against broad currency movements without needing to individually hedge each currency exposure.
new_option: one macro hedge specific to foreign exchange markets uses derivatives based on fixed-weight baskets of currencies (available in both exchange-traded and OTC form).

original_option: h = ρ × [σ(R_DC)/σ(R_FX)] = 0.2 × (4.4%/2.7%) = 0.33 or 33%; for USD10M investment in S&P 500 (long position), Kilmarnock Capital would want to be short approximately USD3.3M in forward contract; because standard market quote for this currency pair is USD/GBP, to be short USD means one has to buy GBP → long position in USD/GBP forward contract of notional size USD3.3 million.

(1) higher trading costs than the major currencies under 'normal' market conditions; (2) increased likelihood of extreme market events and severe illiquidity under stressed market conditions; many emerging market currencies are thinly traded causing higher transaction costs (bid-offer spreads); may also be fewer derivatives products to choose from especially exchange-traded products.
new_option: (1) higher trading costs than the major currencies under 'normal' market conditions.

original_option: for EM cross rates like CLP/THB, typically no direct trade because insufficient trade or capital flows between countries; any trade between these two currencies would go through major intermediary currency (usually USD); trade broken into two legs: trade in CLP/USD pair and another in THB/USD pair; may go through different traders/desks at same bank or one leg at one bank and other at different bank; time zone issues affecting liquidity — one leg may be relatively liquid while other more thinly traded; liquidity in most EM currencies typically deepest in their domestic time zones; two bid-offer spreads to cover.
new_option: for EM cross rates like CLP/THB, typically no direct trade because insufficient trade or capital flows between countries.

original_option: investment return probability distributions for currency (and other) trades subject to such relatively frequent extreme events have FATTER TAILS than normal distribution as well as pronounced NEGATIVE SKEW; risk measurement and control tools (such as value at risk, or VaR) that depend on normal distributions can be misleading and greatly understate risks; many investment performance measures also based on normal distribution — Sharpe ratio can look very attractive during times of relative tranquility but this seeming outperformance can disappear into deep losses faster than most investors can react.
new_option: investment return probability distributions for currency (and other) trades subject to such relatively frequent extreme events have FATTER TAILS than normal distribution as well as pronounced NEGATIVE SKEW.

original_option: hedging long exposure to foreign currency typically involves selling foreign currency forward; when currencies under severe downward pressure, central banks often react by hiking policy rate to support domestic currency; but higher interest rates in country → all else equal, deeper forward discount for its currency (enforced by covered interest rate parity arbitrage); having to sell currency forward at increasingly deep discounts will cause losses through negative roll yield and undermine cost effectiveness of hedging program.
new_option: hedging long exposure to foreign currency typically involves selling foreign currency forward.

original_option: currencies of many emerging market countries trade with some form of capital controls; where capital controls exist and delivery in controlled currency is limited by local government, often possible to use NDFs; similar to regular forward contracts but cash settled (in non-controlled currency of currency pair) rather than physically settled; non-controlled currency for NDFs usually USD or some other major currency; important currencies with NDFs include Chinese yuan (CNY), Korean won (KRW), Russian ruble (RUB), Indian rupee (INR), Brazilian real (BRL); NDF is essentially cash-settled 'bet' on movement in spot rate of these currencies.
new_option: currencies of many emerging market countries trade with some form of capital controls.

original_option: credit risk of NDF typically LOWER than for outright forward because principal sums in NDF do not move, unlike with outright 'vanilla' forward contract; in the BRL/USD NDF illustration cash pay-off was small (USD2,169.98) — there was no delivery of USD1M against receipt of BRL5,518,000; but NDFs exist because of some form of government involvement in FX markets — sudden changes in government policy can lead to sharp movements in spot and NDF rates, often reversing investment gains earned during long periods of seeming market calm; implicit market risk of NDF embodies element of 'tail risk'.
new_option: credit risk of NDF typically LOWER than for outright forward because principal sums in NDF do not move, unlike with outright 'vanilla' forward contract.

original_option: when capital controls exist, free cross-border flow of capital that enforces arbitrage condition underlying covered interest rate parity no longer functions consistently; therefore pricing on NDFs need not be exactly in accord with covered interest rate parity theorem; NDF pricing will reflect individual supply and demand conditions (and risk premia) in offshore market, which need not be same as onshore market of specific emerging market country; some most active participants in NDF market are offshore hedge funds and proprietary traders making directional bets rather than corporate/institutional portfolio managers hedging; volatility in net speculative demand for EM exposure can affect level of forward points; type and strictness of capital controls can vary among EMs.
new_option: when capital controls exist, free cross-border flow of capital that enforces arbitrage condition underlying covered interest rate parity no longer functions consistently.

original_option: a single-factor model — the capital asset pricing model (CAPM); investors seek market return (beta exposure) and do not seek outperformance (alpha).
new_option: a single-factor model — the capital asset pricing model (CAPM).

original_option: factor investing — provides expected return based on exposure to various factors like Value, Size, Momentum, Volatility, Quality; maintains low-cost advantage of index funds; concepts long known but use grew dramatically.
new_option: factor investing — provides expected return based on exposure to various factors like Value, Size, Momentum, Volatility, Quality.

original_option: mature companies with stable net incomes or experiencing cyclical downturn; frequently have low price-to-book and price-to-earnings ratios as well as high dividend yields.
new_option: mature companies with stable net incomes or experiencing cyclical downturn.

original_option: further returns from stocks that have experienced an above-average increase in price during the prior period; example: MSCI's Momentum Index uses stock's most recent 12-month and 6-month price performance to weight securities.
new_option: further returns from stocks that have experienced an above-average increase in price during the prior period.

original_option: risk-oriented — investors seek to lower downside risk; volatility often measured as standard deviation of stock returns; volatility weighting can involve holding all index constituents weighted by inverse of relative price volatility.
new_option: risk-oriented — investors seek to lower downside risk.

original_option: intentionally overweight and underweight certain risk factors based on judgment of market conditions; index-based factor strategies can be used in place of or to complement more traditional market-cap-weighted indexed portfolio.
new_option: intentionally overweight and underweight certain risk factors based on judgment of market conditions.

original_option: tend to concentrate risk exposures, leaving investors exposed during periods when a chosen risk factor is out of favor; observation that even strong risk factors experience periods of underperformance has led many investors toward multi-factor approaches.
new_option: tend to concentrate risk exposures, leaving investors exposed during periods when a chosen risk factor is out of favor.

original_option: a cluster of company fundamentals — book value, cash flow, revenue, sales, dividends, and employee count — as basis for weighting each company; weights averaged across fundamental measures; over 43 years fundamental index outperformed cap-weighted by ~200 bps/year (though may reflect Value and Size factor exposure).
new_option: a cluster of company fundamentals — book value, cash flow, revenue, sales, dividends, and employee count — as basis for weighting each company.

original_option: active decision making — decisions on timing and degree of factor exposure are made up front rather than continuously (Jacobs and Levy 2014); factor selection, weighting, and rebalancing are transparent but decisions still occur.
new_option: active decision making — decisions on timing and degree of factor exposure are made up front rather than continuously (Jacobs and Levy 2014).

original_option: rule-based, transparent strategies that do not involve identifying mispriced individual securities but instead seek to replicate the performance of an index; main advantages: low costs, diversification, and tax efficiency.
new_option: rule-based, transparent strategies that do not involve identifying mispriced individual securities but instead seek to replicate the performance of an index.

original_option: conventional open-end mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) — Qualidex Fund started 1970 (first open-end index mutual fund tracking DJIA); Vanguard S&P 500 Index Fund 1975 (first to attract investors on large scale); first ETF launched Canada 1990 tracking 35 large Toronto Stock Exchange stocks; US ETFs 1993.
new_option: conventional open-end mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) — Qualidex Fund started 1970 (first open-end index mutual fund tracking DJIA).

original_option: ease of trading throughout the day like stocks, low management fees, tax efficiency, ability to buy on margin, ability to short-sell, flexibility to track wide array of indexes; ETFs use creation/redemption in-kind mechanism vs mutual funds' portfolio sales.
new_option: ease of trading throughout the day like stocks, low management fees, tax efficiency, ability to buy on margin, ability to short-sell, flexibility to track wide array of indexes.

original_option: need to buy at offer and sell at bid, commission costs, and risk of illiquid market when investor needs to buy or sell actual ETF shares; brokerage fees for frequent trades can negate expense ratio advantage over open-end mutual funds.
new_option: need to buy at offer and sell at bid, commission costs, and risk of illiquid market when investor needs to buy or sell actual ETF shares.

original_option: acts as market maker for the ETF and intermediary between investors and ETF fund manager when shares are created or redeemed; delivers basket of underlying stocks to fund manager to create ETF shares (or reverses process for redemption); enables in-kind redemption mechanism.
new_option: acts as market maker for the ETF and intermediary between investors and ETF fund manager when shares are created or redeemed.

original_option: accounted for 17% of approximately $7 trillion in global equity ETF AUM as of Q4 2022; index-tracking ETFs 77%, actively managed 3%, other 3%; provide exposure to single factors (Size, Value, Momentum, Quality, Volatility, Yield) or multifactor mixes.
new_option: accounted for 17% of approximately $7 trillion in global equity ETF AUM as of Q4 2022.

original_option: US 12.5%, Europe 7.1%, Asia 3.8% — Fixed Income ETF share much lower; ETFs still represent only 9% of equity assets across US, Europe, Asia Pacific combined at end of 2022.
new_option: US 12.5%, Europe 7.1%, Asia 3.8% — Fixed Income ETF share much lower.

original_option: US governed by Investment Company Act of 1940; Europe governed by UCITS (Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities) — agreement among EU countries governing management and sale of collective investment funds across European borders.
new_option: US governed by Investment Company Act of 1940.

original_option: same steps as active fund selection — decide investor's return and risk objectives + investment constraints, then find corresponding strategy; risk-averse investors may seek low volatility, broad-market investors may prefer all-cap market-cap-weighted strategy.
new_option: same steps as active fund selection — decide investor's return and risk objectives + investment constraints, then find corresponding strategy.

original_option: fund marketplace = brokerage offering funds from different providers, streamlined record keeping in single account; financial adviser = personalized guidance for identifying strategy, single account for shares, potential access to lower-cost share classes not available to all investors.
new_option: fund marketplace = brokerage offering funds from different providers, streamlined record keeping in single account.

original_option: trading securities, managing cash, deciding how to proceed with corporate actions, voting proxies, and reporting performance; rebalancing, reconstitution, and other changes to keep portfolio in line with index; registration, custodial, and audit costs similar to actively managed funds.
new_option: trading securities, managing cash, deciding how to proceed with corporate actions, voting proxies, and reporting performance.

original_option: low costs and convenience of the fund structure — investment manager handles all needed rebalancing, reconstitution, and other changes to keep investment portfolio in line with index; primary advantage is ease of investing and record keeping.
new_option: low costs and convenience of the fund structure — investment manager handles all needed rebalancing, reconstitution, and other changes to keep investment portfolio in line with index.

original_option: low cost, easy to implement, and leverage — but introduce counterparty default risk for OTC derivatives not traded on exchanges or cleared through clearing house; can be relatively difficult for individual investors to access.
new_option: low cost, easy to implement, and leverage — but introduce counterparty default risk for OTC derivatives not traded on exchanges or cleared through clearing house.

original_option: options and futures traded on exchanges and processed through clearing house (eliminates virtually all default risk since exchange is effective counterparty); equity swaps generally executed with single counterparty, adding counterparty default risk.
new_option: options and futures traded on exchanges and processed through clearing house (eliminates virtually all default risk since exchange is effective counterparty).

original_option: notional value of contracts can be many times greater than initial cash outlay; but derivatives expire (stocks can be held indefinitely); risk of expiring options: complete loss of premium paid; futures and swaps can be extended by 'rolling' (selling expiring contract and buying longer-dated one).
new_option: notional value of contracts can be many times greater than initial cash outlay.

original_option: derivative positions typically used to adjust a pre-existing portfolio to move closer to meeting its objectives; three types: completion overlay (correct diverged exposure), rebalancing overlay (need to sell some securities and buy others), currency overlay (hedge foreign currency exposure back to home currency).
new_option: derivative positions typically used to adjust a pre-existing portfolio to move closer to meeting its objectives.

original_option: increase/decrease exposure to entire index portfolio in single transaction; managers make tactical adjustments more efficiently than cash-market transactions; many derivatives contracts highly liquid (sometimes more than underlying cash assets); adjustment quick and low cost.
new_option: increase/decrease exposure to entire index portfolio in single transaction.

original_option: strategic long-term changes usually best made using cash instruments — indefinite expirations and no need to roll expiring positions; futures markets can impose position limits that constrain scale; derivatives usage sometimes restricted by regulatory bodies or IPS stipulations; may not have suitable exchange-traded futures for tracked index.
new_option: strategic long-term changes usually best made using cash instruments — indefinite expirations and no need to roll expiring positions.

original_option: cash-settled (unlike many commodity futures) — counterparties exchange cash rather than underlying shares; provide exposure to specific index; buyer obtains right to buy underlying on expiration date at futures price prevailing at purchase; buyer required to post margin (collateral) to decrease credit risk to exchange.
new_option: cash-settled (unlike many commodity futures) — counterparties exchange cash rather than underlying shares.

original_option: $250 — every USD change in futures price produces $250 change in contract value; for example if September S&P 500 futures settles at 2,159.30 after previous day 2,157, contract value change = 250 × ($2,159.30 − $2,157) = $575.
