clearing house member assigned to trade on behalf of investor for futures positions; posts initial margin required to open position; settles daily to comply with maintenance margin required by clearing house; helps close position at expiration; futures accounts require management of daily cash flows (adding or drawing down).
new_option: clearing house member assigned to trade on behalf of investor for futures positions.

original_option: many institutional investors face short-sale constraints on stocks; but many do NOT face constraints on opening a futures position with a sale of the contracts — futures market provides easy shorting mechanism relative to cash equities.
new_option: many institutional investors face short-sale constraints on stocks.

original_option: over-the-counter contracts, generally executed with a single counterparty adding counterparty default risk; unlike futures which are exchange-traded; sometimes used strategically to synthetically mimic index return; can be extended by rolling.
new_option: over-the-counter contracts, generally executed with a single counterparty adding counterparty default risk.

original_option: investor size (large investors find building their own portfolios via replication/sampling/optimization more cost-effective; smaller investors prefer pooled or ETF); cost/flexibility trade-offs; regulatory and IPS constraints; index availability; long-term vs tactical goals; individual investor access constraints.
new_option: investor size (large investors find building their own portfolios via replication/sampling/optimization more cost-effective.

original_option: risk that futures and spot prices do not move in concert — index fund managers use futures because instruments are expected to move in line with underlying index, but any divergence creates tracking error.
new_option: risk that futures and spot prices do not move in concert.

original_option: holds ALL constituent securities of the index in the same proportion as the index — provides the tightest tracking; requires large-scale portfolio and high-quality information about constituent characteristics; portfolio manager must maintain portfolio according to index changes; trade at market-on-close price where available to match index calculation.
new_option: holds ALL constituent securities of the index in the same proportion as the index — provides the tightest tracking.

original_option: arranges population into distinct strata (mutually exclusive and exhaustive subgroupings) that closely match characteristics and performance of index; common stratification approaches: industry membership (e.g., 11 GICS sectors for S&P 500) and equity style characteristics; multinational indexes stratified first by country then by industry within each country.
new_option: arranges population into distinct strata (mutually exclusive and exhaustive subgroupings) that closely match characteristics and performance of index.

original_option: the portfolio manager wants to track indexes with many constituents (e.g., S&P Global Broad Market Index with >11,000 constituents) or when dealing with a relatively low level of AUM — most investors reluctant to trade and maintain 11,000 securities when significantly smaller number would achieve most portfolios' tracking objectives.
new_option: the portfolio manager wants to track indexes with many constituents (e.g., S&P Global Broad Market Index with >11,000 constituents) or when dealing with a relatively low level of AUM.

original_option: typically involves maximizing a desirable characteristic or minimizing an undesirable one, subject to constraints — e.g., minimize index tracking error subject to holding 50 or fewer constituents; other common constraints: market-cap floor, style characteristics matching benchmark, round lots, low rebalancing costs.
new_option: typically involves maximizing a desirable characteristic or minimizing an undesirable one, subject to constraints — e.g., minimize index tracking error subject to holding 50 or fewer constituents.

original_option: running an optimization to minimize tracking error can lead to portfolios that are mean-variance inefficient versus the benchmark — optimized portfolio may exhibit higher risk than benchmark; useful way to address this is to add constraint on total portfolio volatility so optimized fund total volatility equals benchmark.
new_option: running an optimization to minimize tracking error can lead to portfolios that are mean-variance inefficient versus the benchmark — optimized portfolio may exhibit higher risk than benchmark.

original_option: lower tracking error than stratified sampling; optimization process accounts explicitly for covariances among portfolio constituents — although two securities from different industry sectors may be included under stratified sampling, if their returns move strongly together one will likely be excluded from an optimized portfolio.
new_option: lower tracking error than stratified sampling.

original_option: constituents and weights typically determined based on past market data but returns, variances, and correlations vary over time — output may apply only to period from which data drawn; even if current results apply to future they might not apply for long; would need frequent re-optimization with costs; requires technically sophisticated analyst familiar with computerized optimization software.
new_option: constituents and weights typically determined based on past market data but returns, variances, and correlations vary over time — output may apply only to period from which data drawn.

original_option: full replication for more-liquid issues + stratified sampling or optimization for less-liquid issues — useful for indexes spanning capitalization spectrum like Russell 3000, S&P 1500, Wilshire 5000; the ~1,000 largest constituents are quite liquid (low brokerage fees, tight spreads, low costs); full replication is sensible for large-cap portion.
new_option: full replication for more-liquid issues + stratified sampling or optimization for less-liquid issues — useful for indexes spanning capitalization spectrum like Russell 3000, S&P 1500, Wilshire 5000.

original_option: in practice, index-based portfolio managers often conduct mean-variance optimization using all index constituents (output shows highly diverse weightings); given that investing in lowest-weight stocks may involve marginal transaction costs exceeding marginal diversification benefits, managers may then delete lowest-weighted stocks in a post-optimization stage.
new_option: in practice, index-based portfolio managers often conduct mean-variance optimization using all index constituents (output shows highly diverse weightings).

original_option: set maximum number of constituents (e.g., 200) to limit average lot size and reduce commission costs; select securities based on largest market capitalizations; then match sector weightings of sampled portfolio to those of index by comparing and reweighting.
new_option: set maximum number of constituents (e.g., 200) to limit average lot size and reduce commission costs.

original_option: indexes have few constituents — full replication typically advisable; when index has many constituents or hard-to-find securities, sampling/optimization become preferred; large-cap portions of broad indexes support full replication due to high liquidity and low costs.
new_option: indexes have few constituents — full replication typically advisable.

original_option: using spreadsheet/database programs (Excel, Access) to compute current vs model weights variance and shares to trade; OMS provides pre-trade compliance checks for client-specific restrictions, front-running issues, and other compliance rules; OMS delivers buy and sell orders using FIX or SWIFT protocol; changes announced publicly by index provider must be tracked and applied timely.
new_option: using spreadsheet/database programs (Excel, Access) to compute current vs model weights variance and shares to trade.

original_option: number of index constituents, liquidity of constituents, AUM size, technical sophistication of manager, cost/tracking-error trade-off, and whether portfolio must accommodate specific covariance structures; blended approaches combining methods are common for broad indexes.
new_option: number of index constituents, liquidity of constituents, AUM size, technical sophistication of manager, cost/tracking-error trade-off, and whether portfolio must accommodate specific covariance structures.

original_option: the standard deviation of the difference between the portfolio return and its benchmark index return; measures how closely the portfolio behaves like its benchmark; always non-negative (as it's a standard deviation).
new_option: the standard deviation of the difference between the portfolio return and its benchmark index return.

original_option: the difference between portfolio returns and benchmark returns; can be positive or negative and tells investor how manager performed relative to benchmark; index fund managers endeavor to have non-negative excess returns.
new_option: the difference between portfolio returns and benchmark returns.

original_option: an index with a large number of constituents tends to create higher tracking error than one with fewer constituents — because large numbers may prevent full replication; sampled portfolios typically report greater tracking error than fully replicated.
new_option: an index with a large number of constituents tends to create higher tracking error than one with fewer constituents — because large numbers may prevent full replication.

original_option: (1) fees charged (always negative, drive down excess return); (2) number of securities held vs benchmark (sampled portfolios have higher TE); (3) intra-day trading price differences from close prices; (4) commission costs; (5) cash holdings (equity indexes don't have cash allocation).
new_option: (1) fees charged (always negative, drive down excess return).

original_option: tracking error caused by temporarily uninvested cash — cash can be accumulated from dividends received, sale proceeds, investor contributions; effect on portfolio value is NEGATIVE when market is rising and POSITIVE when it is falling.
new_option: tracking error caused by temporarily uninvested cash — cash can be accumulated from dividends received, sale proceeds, investor contributions.

original_option: the price levels used to report index returns are struck at close of trading day — securities bought or sold at different prices from those of the index create tracking error; index fund managers minimize this by transacting at market-on-close price or as near to closing as feasible.
new_option: the price levels used to report index returns are struck at close of trading day — securities bought or sold at different prices from those of the index create tracking error.

original_option: cash drag — Hill and Cheong (1996) discuss how to equitize a portfolio that would otherwise suffer from cash drag; one method uses futures contracts (widely used); ETFs also used; portfolio managers establish FCM relationship to offset cash positions with futures contract representing replicated index and calculate accrued dividends to hedge dividend drag.
new_option: cash drag — Hill and Cheong (1996) discuss how to equitize a portfolio that would otherwise suffer from cash drag.

original_option: minimize cash held (creates undesirable TE); invest cash flows at same valuations used by benchmark index provider (not always feasible); aim to maintain beta of 1.0 relative to benchmark index while keeping other risk factor exposures similar to index; net investor cash inflows/redemptions; use equitization tools.
new_option: minimize cash held (creates undesirable TE).

original_option: value will differ depending on whether data frequency is daily or less frequent; for an index fund, degree of tracking error fluctuates over time.
new_option: value will differ depending on whether data frequency is daily or less frequent.

original_option: Australian funds have far more constituents than Korean funds and lower explicit fund fees; Korean funds hold ~80% of index constituents (using sampling) while Australian funds hold more like ~100% (replication); Australian negative excess returns close to management fees of 18-20 bps.
new_option: Australian funds have far more constituents than Korean funds and lower explicit fund fees.

original_option: even a manager whose return equals the index could have lagged then led producing net difference of zero — but being a standard deviation, tracking error cannot be zero in such cases; only truly perfect replication with zero timing differences produces zero TE.
new_option: even a manager whose return equals the index could have lagged then led producing net difference of zero — but being a standard deviation, tracking error cannot be zero in such cases.

original_option: company-specific returns, sector returns, country returns, and currency returns — same as for any actively managed fund; can also group by portfolio objective (e.g., high dividend yield indexed portfolio grouped by dividend yield vs broad market benchmark; low volatility portfolio grouped by volatility buckets).
new_option: company-specific returns, sector returns, country returns, and currency returns — same as for any actively managed fund.

original_option: helps understand sources of return; index fund managers who track a broad market index need to understand what factors are driving returns of portfolio and underlying index; factor-based strategy managers should understand sources of return AND how those returns relate to broad market from which constituents were chosen — similar to actively managed funds since factor-based strategies are actively chosen.
new_option: helps understand sources of return.

original_option: additional revenue offset against portfolio management costs — often resulting in lower net fees to investors; example: iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) $63.9B AUM lent $5.97B in securities as of 31 March 2021 (100% collateralized with cash); net securities lending income ~$63M nearly offset ~$90.7M in investment advisory fees.
new_option: additional revenue offset against portfolio management costs — often resulting in lower net fees to investors.

original_option: EUR 28 trillion in securities made available for lending by institutional investors; of this EUR 2.6 trillion in value was actually loaned; collective investment vehicles and pension funds accounted for 59% of total value of securities loaned; collateral held with European triparty agents: equities 45%, government bonds 44%.
new_option: EUR 28 trillion in securities made available for lending by institutional investors.

original_option: credit risk (borrower credit quality), market risk (value of posted collateral), liquidity risk, operational risk; collateral investment risk (if lender invests cash collateral in long-term/risky securities, collateral value at risk of erosion); Sigma Finance Company 2008-2009 example: SIV downgraded, lost short-term borrowing ability, defaulted; investors who lent securities suffered substantial losses.
new_option: credit risk (borrower credit quality), market risk (value of posted collateral), liquidity risk, operational risk.

original_option: typically 2-10% annualized cost of borrowing depending on borrower's credit quality and how difficult the security is to borrow; some securities are widely recognized as 'easy to borrow' (ETB); borrowers pay cash to lenders in lieu of any cash dividends received.
new_option: typically 2-10% annualized cost of borrowing depending on borrower's credit quality and how difficult the security is to borrow.

original_option: governance policies, executive compensation, and social, environmental, and strategy issues — via voting shares (large influence in corporate elections) and access to private meetings with corporate management; typical points of contact: investor relations specialists, general counsel/corporate secretary, board chair, CEO/CFO.
new_option: governance policies, executive compensation, and social, environmental, and strategy issues — via voting shares (large influence in corporate elections) and access to private meetings with corporate management.

original_option: governance improvements aimed at improving effectiveness of operations, management, and board oversight — if resulting efficiency improvements evidenced in higher returns to index-constituent stocks, index performance rises and so does the performance of an index-tracking portfolio; goal is to increase returns; may have higher duty than transient active managers since as long as stock is in benchmark index, index-based managers are permanent shareholders.
new_option: governance improvements aimed at improving effectiveness of operations, management, and board oversight — if resulting efficiency improvements evidenced in higher returns to index-constituent stocks, index performance rises and so does the performance of an index-tracking portfolio.

original_option: staff resources to become familiar with key issues and engage management, regulators, and other investors; researching and voting thousands of proxy ballots (many managers hire proxy voting service like Institutional Shareholder Services or Broadridge Financial Services); potential conflicts of interest (e.g., financial firm managing retirement plan of company whose stock is index constituent may hesitate to challenge that company's management).
new_option: staff resources to become familiar with key issues and engage management, regulators, and other investors.

original_option: some companies give greater voting rights to long-term shareholders (Dallas and Barry 2016) — 12 US companies with voting rights that increase to four, five, or even ten votes per share if holding period exceeds three (and sometimes four) years; reinforces permanence of index-based holders.
new_option: some companies give greater voting rights to long-term shareholders (Dallas and Barry 2016) — 12 US companies with voting rights that increase to four, five, or even ten votes per share if holding period exceeds three (and sometimes four) years.

original_option: a decline in required return, and as prices adjust the market should deliver a higher return than was previously expected. This is a rationale for reallocating toward that market, often meaning a shift from developed markets to emerging markets.
new_option: a decline in required return, and as prices adjust the market should deliver a higher return than was previously expected.

original_option: fundamental (based on research into companies/sectors/markets involving analyst discretion and judgment) and quantitative (based on quantitative models of security returns applied systematically with limited involvement of human judgment); also called 'discretionary' vs 'systematic'.
new_option: fundamental (based on research into companies/sectors/markets involving analyst discretion and judgment) and quantitative (based on quantitative models of security returns applied systematically with limited involvement of human judgment).

original_option: analysis of a company's financial statements — obtains detailed understanding of current and past profitability, financial position, and cash flows; combined with insights into business model, management, product lines, and economic outlook to form view on future business prospects; typically produces intrinsic value and/or relative value estimates.
new_option: analysis of a company's financial statements — obtains detailed understanding of current and past profitability, financial position, and cash flows.

original_option: involve analyst judgment at the design stage but largely replace ongoing reliance on human judgment and discretion with systematic processes that are often dependent on computer programming for execution; search for factors that have predictive power to identify securities or trades that will earn superior investment returns.
new_option: involve analyst judgment at the design stage but largely replace ongoing reliance on human judgment and discretion with systematic processes that are often dependent on computer programming for execution.

original_option: factors empirically shown to be positively associated with a long-term return premium — commonly size, valuation, momentum, and profitability; other factors called 'unrewarded factors' are used in portfolio construction but have not been empirically proven to offer persistent return premium.
new_option: factors empirically shown to be positively associated with a long-term return premium — commonly size, valuation, momentum, and profitability.

original_option: judgment — absolute or index-relative sizes of positions based on manager's conviction of forecasts; portfolio must comply with risk parameters set out in investment agreements or fund prospectus; portfolio managers see risk at company level (fair value inaccuracy, business performance different from expectations, market failing to recognize under/overvaluation).
new_option: judgment — absolute or index-relative sizes of positions based on manager's conviction of forecasts.

original_option: generally uses a portfolio optimizer which controls for risk at the portfolio level in arriving at individual stock weights — risk lies at portfolio level rather than at specific stocks; risk is that factor returns will not perform as expected; portfolios usually rebalanced at regular intervals (monthly/quarterly) using pre-determined rules.
new_option: generally uses a portfolio optimizer which controls for risk at the portfolio level in arriving at individual stock weights — risk lies at portfolio level rather than at specific stocks.

original_option: fundamental portfolio 50 individually selected stocks reviewed continuously for potential sale/trimming, pre-screened by earnings yield, dividend yield, earnings growth, and financial leverage; quantitative portfolio makes active bets on 400 stocks with monthly rebalancing based on five-factor model.
new_option: fundamental portfolio 50 individually selected stocks reviewed continuously for potential sale/trimming, pre-screened by earnings yield, dividend yield, earnings growth, and financial leverage.

original_option: fundamental managers continuously monitor portfolio holdings and may increase/decrease/eliminate positions at any time; quantitative portfolios usually rebalanced at regular intervals (monthly/quarterly) with pre-determined rules automatically selecting positions.
new_option: fundamental managers continuously monitor portfolio holdings and may increase/decrease/eliminate positions at any time.

original_option: begin the asset selection process with data at the individual asset and company level (price momentum, profitability); fundamental investors begin analysis at company level before forming opinion on wider sector or market; use economic and financial analysis to assess intrinsic value and compare with market price.
new_option: begin the asset selection process with data at the individual asset and company level (price momentum, profitability).

original_option: aim to buy stocks trading at significant discount to estimated intrinsic value; typically focus on companies with attractive valuation metrics reflected in low earnings (or asset) multiples; Benjamin Graham (father of value investing) posited that buying earnings and assets relatively inexpensively affords a 'margin of safety'; opportunities may arise due to behavioral biases and often reflect investors' overreaction to negative news.
new_option: aim to buy stocks trading at significant discount to estimated intrinsic value.

original_option: evaluates companies by comparing their value indicators (P/E, P/B multiples) to average valuation of companies in same industry sector — aims to identify stocks that offer value relative to sector peers; different sectors face different market structures, competitive/regulatory conditions, so average sector multiples vary.
new_option: evaluates companies by comparing their value indicators (P/E, P/B multiples) to average valuation of companies in same industry sector — aims to identify stocks that offer value relative to sector peers.

original_option: combines growth expectations with value discipline — measured by PEG ratio (P/E divided by growth); Example 2 Company A: forward P/E 50/5 = 10, PEG = 10/20 = 0.5 (lowest among four companies), making it good candidate for GARP given favorable valuation relative to growth.

business model and branding, competitive advantages, and company management and corporate governance; valuation based on discounted cash flow model or preferred market multiple (often earnings-related P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA).
new_option: business model and branding, competitive advantages, and company management and corporate governance.

original_option: stock that appears attractively valued (low P/E, P/B, or P/CF multiple) because of significant price fall but that may still be overpriced given its worsening future prospects — company/entire sector may face deteriorating future prospects; stock prices may stay low or decline further; stock prices need catalysts to advance; without catalysts stock may not adjust to fair value.
new_option: stock that appears attractively valued (low P/E, P/B, or P/CF multiple) because of significant price fall but that may still be overpriced given its worsening future prospects — company/entire sector may face deteriorating future prospects.

original_option: at end of 2013 HKD 80, dropped below HKD 41 at end of 2020 — 48.7% decline in seven years, while Hang Seng Financial Index gained 16.0%; average P/E 10.9x and P/B 0.9x looked cheap compared to peers and own history; despite appearing undervalued, stock performed poorly due to need for extensive cost cutting.
new_option: at end of 2013 HKD 80, dropped below HKD 41 at end of 2020 — 48.7% decline in seven years, while Hang Seng Financial Index gained 16.0%.

original_option: when company's results fall short of expectations, stock performance affected negatively; stock may have been overpriced at purchase; company may deliver above-average earnings/CF growth in line with expectations but share price may not move higher due to already high starting level; investors often willing to justify paying high multiples but earnings sustainability may prove incorrect — superior market position may be unsustainable as competitors respond and compete away supernormal profits.
new_option: when company's results fall short of expectations, stock performance affected negatively.

original_option: insight into company's value proposition, operational flow, structure of value chain, branding strategy, market segment, and revenue/profit margin generation; helps evaluate sustainability of competitive advantages; brand equity plays important role in product price determination (Apple, BMW premium pricing due to brand loyalty); companies with superior business models compete successfully, have scalability, and generate significant earnings.
new_option: insight into company's value proposition, operational flow, structure of value chain, branding strategy, market segment, and revenue/profit margin generation.

original_option: use investment process that begins at a top or macro level; instead of focusing on individual company- and asset-level variables, top-down portfolio managers study variables affecting many companies (macroeconomic environment, demographic trends, government policies); often use instruments like futures, ETFs, swaps, and custom baskets to capture macro dynamics.
new_option: use investment process that begins at a top or macro level.

original_option: form portfolios by investing in different geographic regions depending on assessment of regions' prospects; manager may prefer a particular region and establish position while limiting exposure to others; global equity fund managers may make US-Europe tradeoff decisions, or allocate among all investable country equity markets using futures or ETFs; may seek to track supply/demand for equities via aggregate share buybacks, fund flows, IPO volumes, secondary share issuance.
new_option: form portfolios by investing in different geographic regions depending on assessment of regions' prospects.

original_option: have a view on expected returns of various sectors and industries across borders; globally integrated industries (technology, energy) more suitable to global sector allocation; sectors more local (real estate, consumer staples) more suitable to sector allocation within a country; sector/industry ETFs greatly facilitate implementation.
new_option: have a view on expected returns of various sectors and industries across borders.

original_option: based on investors' view on volatility, usually implemented using derivative instruments; managers who believe they can predict future market volatility better than option-implied volatility (VIX Index) can trade VIX futures on CBOE Futures Exchange (CFE), trade index options, or enter into volatility/variance swaps; index straddle strategy = purchase call and put options with same strike and expiry when expecting high volatility without directional view; max loss limited to total call+put premiums.
new_option: based on investors' view on volatility, usually implemented using derivative instruments.

original_option: use broad macroeconomic, demographic, or political drivers, or bottom-up ideas on industries and sectors, to identify investment opportunities; disruptive technologies, processes, regulations, innovations, and economic cycles present opportunities and pose challenges to existing companies; important to determine whether trend is structural (long-term, like smartphones/tablets/cloud computing) or short-term (e.g., companies with foreign sales exposure for currency views); further examples include mobile computing, clean energy, fintech, medical advances.
new_option: use broad macroeconomic, demographic, or political drivers, or bottom-up ideas on industries and sectors, to identify investment opportunities.

original_option: smart beta investment portfolios offer benefits of passive strategies combined with some advantages of active ones — top-down managers can choose among different equity style ETFs and structured products to obtain risk exposures consistent with their views on different economic cycle stages or market sentiment; e.g., high-quality stocks tend to perform well in recessions, cyclical deep-value companies more likely to deliver superior returns in 'risk-on' environments.
new_option: smart beta investment portfolios offer benefits of passive strategies combined with some advantages of active ones — top-down managers can choose among different equity style ETFs and structured products to obtain risk exposures consistent with their views on different economic cycle stages or market sentiment.

original_option: an array of derivative positions managed separately from securities portfolio to achieve overall portfolio characteristics desired by manager — bottom-up strategies can lead to unintended macro (sector or country) risk exposures; investor may adjust sector weights to align with benchmark's weights via long and short positions in derivatives; overlays can also add active returns not correlated with underlying portfolio strategy.
new_option: an array of derivative positions managed separately from securities portfolio to achieve overall portfolio characteristics desired by manager — bottom-up strategies can lead to unintended macro (sector or country) risk exposures.

original_option: a typical method is to have the portfolio strategist set target country and sector weights (top-down); portfolio managers then construct stock portfolios (bottom-up) that are consistent with these preset weights; both country/geographic allocation and sector/industry rotation can use both top-down macroeconomic AND bottom-up fundamental analysis.
new_option: a typical method is to have the portfolio strategist set target country and sector weights (top-down).

original_option: a variable or characteristic with which individual asset returns are correlated; broadly defined as any variable believed to be valuable in ranking stocks for investment and in predicting future returns or risks; hundreds identified but many not empirically proven ('unrewarded factors').
new_option: a variable or characteristic with which individual asset returns are correlated.

original_option: identify significant factors that can predict future stock returns and construct a portfolio that tilts towards such factors; some rely on single factor (transparent, stable exposure with regular rebalancing); others rely on selection of factors; some attempt to time factor exposure recognizing that factor performance varies over time.
new_option: identify significant factors that can predict future stock returns and construct a portfolio that tilts towards such factors.

original_option: after choosing factor and ranking investable stock universe by that factor, divide universe into quantiles (typically quintiles or deciles) to form quantile portfolios; long/short hedged portfolio formed by going long best quantile and shorting worst quantile; performance tracked over time.
new_option: after choosing factor and ranking investable stock universe by that factor, divide universe into quantiles (typically quintiles or deciles) to form quantile portfolios.

original_option: (1) middle-quantile information not utilized; (2) implicitly assumes linear (or monotonic) relationship between factor and future returns which may not be case; (3) portfolios tend to be concentrated (if many managers use similar factors, portfolios concentrated in specific stocks); (4) requires shorting stocks (may not be possible in some markets or overly expensive); (5) most importantly, hedged portfolio is not a 'pure' factor portfolio because it has significant exposures to other risk factors.
new_option: (1) middle-quantile information not utilized.

original_option: uses valuation metrics — earnings yield, book-to-market, price-to-cash-flow, dividend yield; typical implementation via hedged portfolio: buy top decile of value-attractive stocks and short bottom decile; historically shown to earn long-term return premium (rewarded factor).
new_option: uses valuation metrics — earnings yield, book-to-market, price-to-cash-flow, dividend yield.

original_option: buys past winners and shorts past losers; sector-neutral price momentum focuses on stock selection without industry-level bets which tends to reduce downside risk; Exhibit 12 shows sector-neutralized price momentum long/short decile portfolio performance; Exhibit 13 extends analysis to European and Japanese markets where similar effect on downside risk shown over the period.
new_option: buys past winners and shorts past losers.

original_option: aims to measure company's growth potential; can be calculated using historical growth rates or projected forward growth rates; classifiable as short-term growth (last quarter, last year, next quarter, next year) or long-term (last 5 years, next 5 years); while higher growth generally considered indicator for strong future performance, growth of some metrics (like assets) results in weaker future performance.
new_option: aims to measure company's growth potential.

original_option: quality factor based on earnings quality — Sloan proposed that stock prices fail to reflect fully the information contained in accrual and cash flow components of current earnings; performance of accruals anomaly factor appears to be quite cyclical; long-term cash flows from operations and net income should converge and be consistent (but can diverge short-term as management has more discretion in accruals-based accounting).
new_option: quality factor based on earnings quality — Sloan proposed that stock prices fail to reflect fully the information contained in accrual and cash flow components of current earnings.

original_option: based on belief that different factors (size, value, momentum, quality) work well during some time periods but less well during others; use process that allocates to stock baskets representing each style when style expected to offer positive excess return vs benchmark; generally more in domain of quantitative investing; classification of securities into style categories is less standardized than sector or country allocation.
new_option: based on belief that different factors (size, value, momentum, quality) work well during some time periods but less well during others.

original_option: specialize in taking stakes in listed companies and advocating changes for the purpose of producing a gain on the investment; may seek board representation or use other measures to initiate strategic, operational, or financial structure changes (asset sales, cost-cutting, management changes, capital structure changes, dividend increases, share buybacks); include hedge funds, public pension funds, private investors and vary greatly in approaches/expertise/horizons.
new_option: specialize in taking stakes in listed companies and advocating changes for the purpose of producing a gain on the investment.

original_option: typically less than 10% — activist investors aim to achieve their goals with smaller stakes rather than pursuing a full takeover bid; equity stakes generally made public; stakes above certain threshold must be made public in most jurisdictions; time horizon often shorter than buy-and-hold investors but whole process can last for a number of years.
new_option: typically less than 10% — activist investors aim to achieve their goals with smaller stakes rather than pursuing a full takeover bid.

original_option: reached $50 billion in 2007 before falling sharply during global financial crisis; strongly recovered with AUM close to $46 billion in 2018; benefit from lighter regulation than other fund types and fee structure justifying concerted campaigns for change; US has seen greatest amount of activist activity; some in Europe; more limited elsewhere due to cultural reasons and concentrated shareholder ownership.
new_option: reached $50 billion in 2007 before falling sharply during global financial crisis.

original_option: average annual return 7.7% with annual volatility 13.7% and Sharpe ratio 0.56 — slightly higher than S&P 500 Sharpe ratio of 0.54; but difficult to conclude how much value activist investors add because HFRX index does not include large enough number of managers and managers vary in approaches and risks.
new_option: average annual return 7.7% with annual volatility 13.7% and Sharpe ratio 0.56 — slightly higher than S&P 500 Sharpe ratio of 0.54.

original_option: target company stocks go up by 2% on average on announcement day (based on all activist events in Thomson Reuters Corporate Governance Intelligence database during discrete 30-year period); positive reaction comes on top of stock appreciation prior to announcements; per Maug (1998), activists trade in stock prior to announcement to build up stake, assert control, and profit from value creation; modest post-announcement drift: month after activist announcement date, target share prices move up by 0.6% on average.
new_option: target company stocks go up by 2% on average on announcement day (based on all activist events in Thomson Reuters Corporate Governance Intelligence database during discrete 30-year period).

original_option: companies with underperforming operations (below-industry return on assets), underutilized assets, potential for improvements via reducing fixed-asset investments, concentrating production, disposing of assets in line with peer practices; example (Trian Fund Management proposing splitting PepsiCo; Third Point calling for leadership change at Yahoo!); financial leverage of target companies increases significantly after activist involvement.
new_option: companies with underperforming operations (below-industry return on assets), underutilized assets, potential for improvements via reducing fixed-asset investments, concentrating production, disposing of assets in line with peer practices.

original_option: goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues); activist campaigns are typically diverse.
new_option: goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues).

original_option: identify target companies → buy target stock → study corporate structure/governance/shareholder base → submit proposal to company management → if agreement, exit or buy more; if not, threaten proxy contest → negotiate settlement; if not agreement, launch proxy contest → shareholder vote.
new_option: identify target companies → buy target stock → study corporate structure/governance/shareholder base → submit proposal to company management → if agreement, exit or buy more.

original_option: use statistical and technical analysis to exploit pricing anomalies; make extensive use of data such as stock price, dividend, trading volume, and limit order book; analytical tools include (1) traditional technical analysis, (2) sophisticated time-series analysis and econometric models, and (3) machine-learning techniques; portfolio managers typically take advantage of mean reversion in share prices or opportunities created by market microstructure issues.
new_option: use statistical and technical analysis to exploit pricing anomalies.

original_option: identifies two securities historically highly correlated with each other — when price relationship deviates from long-term average, managers expecting temporary deviation go long the underperforming stock and simultaneously short the outperforming stock; if prices converge to long-term average, investors close the trade and realize profit; bets on mean-reversion pattern; biggest risk is that observed divergence is structural (not temporary); risk management critical, so investors often employ stop-loss rules.
new_option: identifies two securities historically highly correlated with each other — when price relationship deviates from long-term average, managers expecting temporary deviation go long the underperforming stock and simultaneously short the outperforming stock.

original_option: log price differential (spread = ln(CNR/CP)) with moving average computed on rolling 130-day window and bands at 2 standard deviations above and below moving average; simple strategy: enter trade when spread more than 2 SDs from moving average, close when spread reaches moving average again; correlation coefficient between the two stocks was 69% based on daily returns from 2 January 2014 to 26 May 2016.
new_option: log price differential (spread = ln(CNR/CP)) with moving average computed on rolling 130-day window and bands at 2 standard deviations above and below moving average.

original_option: first trade opened 2 October 2014 when spread crossed -2 SD mark, closed 18 November 2014 (profitable, position held slightly more than a month); second trade also profitable but lasted much longer; third trade entered 21 July 2015 experienced structural break — CP's decline further intensified while CNR stayed relatively flat, spread continued to narrow; loss on third trade could have been significantly greater than profits from first two if positions closed prior to mean reversion in spring 2016; example highlights risk inherent in mean-reversion strategies.
new_option: first trade opened 2 October 2014 when spread crossed -2 SD mark, closed 18 November 2014 (profitable, position held slightly more than a month).

original_option: take advantage of NYSE Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and often involve extensive analysis of the limit order book to identify very short-term mispricing opportunities; e.g., temporary imbalance between buy and sell orders may trigger price spike lasting only a few milliseconds; only investors with analytical tools and trading capabilities for high-frequency trading are in position to capture such opportunities, usually within portfolio of many stocks designed to take advantage of very short-term discrepancies.
new_option: take advantage of NYSE Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and often involve extensive analysis of the limit order book to identify very short-term mispricing opportunities.

original_option: category of active strategies that do not fit neatly into fundamental/quantitative/factor categorizations; rely on extensive use of quantitative data and usually implemented in systematic, rules-based way but can incorporate manager's judgment; exploit corporate events like M&A, spin-offs, restructurings.
new_option: category of active strategies that do not fit neatly into fundamental/quantitative/factor categorizations.

original_option: the most difficult aspect; can be done either by using a quantitative approach (creating models of stock prices) or by using a fundamental approach (judging the two stocks whose prices should move together for qualitative reasons); Example 7 Pair 1 (two food-producers with mean-reverting price ratio) preferred over Pair 2 (food retailer vs car manufacturer — correlation likely coincidental) and Pair 3 (fundamental change in one bank).
new_option: the most difficult aspect.

original_option: that the observed price divergence is not temporary; rather, it might be due to structural reasons (e.g., one company developed new technology or product not easily replicated by competitors); investors often employ stop-loss rules to exit trades when loss limit reached because risk management is critical for success.
new_option: that the observed price divergence is not temporary.

original_option: defining the investment universe, prescreening the universe, and building an appropriate research infrastructure — Example 8 growth-oriented prescreen: (1) YoY EPS growth from continuing operations increased over each of last 4 fiscal years, (2) growth in EPS from continuing operations over last 12 months positive, (3) percentage difference between actual announced earnings and consensus earnings estimate for most recent quarter ≥10%, (4) percentage change in stock price over last 4 weeks positive, (5) 26-week relative price strength ≥ industry's 26-week relative price strength, (6) average daily volume for last 10 days in top 50% of market.
new_option: defining the investment universe, prescreening the universe, and building an appropriate research infrastructure.

original_option: growth orientation with focus on companies delivering EPS growth in recent years and maintaining earnings and price growth momentum — criterion 1 specifies accelerating EPS growth rates; criterion 2 discards companies with negative recent earnings growth; criterion 3 screens for positive earnings surprise (past growth continuing); criteria 4 and 5 screen for positive recent stock price momentum; criterion 6 retains only stocks with at least average market liquidity; absence of valuation multiples among screening criteria (value investor's screening would typically include screen for expensive valuations).
new_option: growth orientation with focus on companies delivering EPS growth in recent years and maintaining earnings and price growth momentum — criterion 1 specifies accelerating EPS growth rates.

original_option: cognitive error — tendency of analysts and investors to look for information that confirms existing beliefs about favorite companies and to ignore or undervalue any information that contradicts existing beliefs; creates selective exposure/perception/retention; consequences include poorly diversified portfolio, excessive risk exposure, holdings in poorly performing securities; can be reduced by actively seeking opinions of other investors/team members and looking for information from range of sources.
new_option: cognitive error — tendency of analysts and investors to look for information that confirms existing beliefs about favorite companies and to ignore or undervalue any information that contradicts existing beliefs.

original_option: a cognitive error — 'an expectancy of a personal success probability inappropriately higher than the objective probability would warrant'; human tendency to overestimate abilities to control or influence outcomes; could lead to excessive trading and/or heavy weighting on a few stocks; investors should seek contrary viewpoints and set/enforce proper trading and portfolio diversification rules.
new_option: a cognitive error — 'an expectancy of a personal success probability inappropriately higher than the objective probability would warrant'.

original_option: an emotional bias whereby investors tend to prefer avoiding losses over achieving gains; psychologically losses are significantly more powerful than gains; in absolute value terms, utility derived from a gain is much lower than utility given up in equivalent loss; can cause investors to hold unbalanced portfolios (poorly performing positions maintained in hope of recovery; successful investments sold prematurely to avoid further risk); disciplined trading strategy with firmly established stop-loss rules essential.
new_option: an emotional bias whereby investors tend to prefer avoiding losses over achieving gains.

original_option: emotional bias whereby investors demonstrate unwarranted faith in their own intuitive reasoning, judgment, and/or cognitive abilities — result of overestimating knowledge levels, abilities, and access to information; unlike illusion of control bias (which is cognitive error), overconfidence bias is illusion of exaggerated knowledge and abilities; investors may attribute success to own ability rather than luck; portfolio manager underestimates risks and overestimates expected returns; regularly reviewing actual investment records and seeking constructive feedback helps.
new_option: emotional bias whereby investors demonstrate unwarranted faith in their own intuitive reasoning, judgment, and/or cognitive abilities — result of overestimating knowledge levels, abilities, and access to information.

original_option: an information-processing bias (cognitive error category) whereby individuals take mental shortcut in estimating probability of outcome based on availability of information and how easily outcome comes to mind; easily recalled outcomes often perceived as being more likely than those harder to recall or understand; in fundamental equity investing, may reduce investment opportunity set and result in insufficient diversification as manager relies on familiar stocks reflecting narrow range of experience; setting appropriate investment strategy in line with investment horizon and conducting disciplined portfolio analysis with long-term focus helps.
new_option: an information-processing bias (cognitive error category) whereby individuals take mental shortcut in estimating probability of outcome based on availability of information and how easily outcome comes to mind.

original_option: an emotional bias — causes investors to avoid making decisions they fear will turn out poorly; investors try to avoid pain of regret associated with bad decisions; may actually prevent investors from making decisions and cause them to hold onto positions for too long and lose out on profitable investment opportunities; carefully defined portfolio review process (require investors to periodically review and justify existing positions or substantiate decision not to have exposure to other stocks) helps mitigate.
new_option: an emotional bias — causes investors to avoid making decisions they fear will turn out poorly.

original_option: began in 1970s and became mainstream investment approach in subsequent decades; many quant equity funds suffered significant losses in August 2007 (the 'quant meltdown'); subsequent global financial crisis contributed to growing suspicions about sustainability; both performance and perception recovered significantly since 2012 as investors improved research/processes/construction.
new_option: began in 1970s and became mainstream investment approach in subsequent decades.

original_option: (1) company mapping (track many companies over time and across data vendors — each company may have multiple share classes; new go public, others disappear; identifiers change over time; different vendors have own identifiers); (2) company fundamentals (demographics, financial statements, market data); (3) survey data; (4) unconventional/unstructured data (satellite images, news sentiment, customer-supplier chain metrics, corporate events).
new_option: (1) company mapping (track many companies over time and across data vendors — each company may have multiple share classes.

original_option: Pearson IC = simple correlation between factor scores and next period's returns (always between -1 and +1); Spearman rank IC = Pearson correlation between ranked factor scores and ranked forward returns (more robust to outliers); as simple rule of thumb, factor with average monthly IC of 5%-6% considered very strong for US equities; the higher the IC, higher the predictive power.
new_option: Pearson IC = simple correlation between factor scores and next period's returns (always between -1 and +1).

original_option: Pearson IC is negative at -0.8% (single outlier Stock I distorts result), suggesting signal did not perform well and was negatively correlated with subsequent month's returns; but the sample factor generally in line with subsequent returns except Stock I (highest factor predicted but worst performer); Spearman rank IC at 40% suggests strong predictive power for subsequent returns; long/short tercile portfolio return 0.56% — long basket (Stocks G, H, I) outperformed short basket (Stocks A, B, C) by 56 bps.
new_option: Pearson IC is negative at -0.8% (single outlier Stock I distorts result), suggesting signal did not perform well and was negatively correlated with subsequent month's returns.

original_option: treating each factor as an asset — factor weighting becomes an asset allocation decision; standard mean-variance optimization can also be used to weight factors; investors should bear in mind that factors may be effective individually but not add material value to a factor model because they are correlated with other factors.
new_option: treating each factor as an asset — factor weighting becomes an asset allocation decision.

original_option: (1) risk models — estimate variance-covariance matrix of stock returns (directly estimating from sample data typically infeasible with significant estimation errors; managers rely on commercial risk model vendors like MSCI Barra and Axioma); (2) trading costs — two kinds: explicit (commissions, fees, taxes) and implicit (bid-ask spread, market impact); when two stocks have similar expected returns and risks, normally the one with lower execution costs is preferred.
new_option: (1) risk models — estimate variance-covariance matrix of stock returns (directly estimating from sample data typically infeasible with significant estimation errors.

original_option: common pitfalls in quantitative investment processes — survivorship bias (back-tests using only companies currently in business today ignore stocks that left universe due to bankruptcy/delisting/acquisition, leading to overly optimistic results); look-ahead bias (using information unknown or unavailable at time investment decision was made, e.g., accounting data used before released); data mining (excessive search of past financial data to uncover patterns and conform to pre-determined model can introduce bias resulting in model overfitting).
new_option: common pitfalls in quantitative investment processes — survivorship bias (back-tests using only companies currently in business today ignore stocks that left universe due to bankruptcy/delisting/acquisition, leading to overly optimistic results).

original_option: in theoretical world with no transaction costs, a simple long/short strategy (buying top 20% dividend-paying stocks in Japan with worst performance in previous month and shorting bottom 20% with highest previous-month returns) generated annual return of 12%, beating classic value factor of price to book; however if transaction cost assumption changed from 0 bps to 30 bps per trade, return of reversal strategy drops sharply while return of price-to-book strategy drops only modestly.
new_option: in theoretical world with no transaction costs, a simple long/short strategy (buying top 20% dividend-paying stocks in Japan with worst performance in previous month and shorting bottom 20% with highest previous-month returns) generated annual return of 12%, beating classic value factor of price to book.

original_option: pairs of common attributes — value and growth, large cap and small cap, high volatility and low volatility, high dividend and low dividend, developed markets and emerging markets; style pairs need not be mutually exclusive; each pair interprets stock performance from different perspective; combination of several style pairs may give more complete picture of sources of stock returns.
new_option: pairs of common attributes — value and growth, large cap and small cap, high volatility and low volatility, high dividend and low dividend, developed markets and emerging markets.

original_option: holdings-based approach (aggregation of attributes from individual stocks in portfolio, more accurate, requires availability of all constituents and style attributes) and returns-based approach (compares returns of strategy to set of style indexes, can be applied when portfolio holdings not disclosed); Morningstar and Thomson Reuters Lipper use 0/1 style attribute; MSCI and FTSE Russell allow stocks to have characteristics of two styles at once via multifactor approach with style inclusion factors.
new_option: holdings-based approach (aggregation of attributes from individual stocks in portfolio, more accurate, requires availability of all constituents and style attributes) and returns-based approach (compares returns of strategy to set of style indexes, can be applied when portfolio holdings not disclosed).

original_option: first appeared in 1992 to help investors and advisers determine investment style of a fund; nine-square grid with three stock investment styles (value/core/growth for stocks, blend for funds) × three size categories (large/mid/small); uses holdings-based style analysis; classifies about same number of stocks in each of value/core/growth styles; determines value and growth scores using five stock attributes.
new_option: first appeared in 1992 to help investors and advisers determine investment style of a fund.

original_option: no consensus on boundaries — different data and research providers use different criteria for size classification; one practice defines large-cap stocks as those accounting for top ~70% of capitalization of all stocks in universe, mid-cap next ~20%, and small-cap accounting for balance; large-cap = well-established with strong market presence, good information disclosure, extensive analyst/media scrutiny, lower risk, more limited future growth potential; small-cap = less mature, potentially greater growth, higher failure risk, lower analyst/public scrutiny.
new_option: no consensus on boundaries — different data and research providers use different criteria for size classification.

original_option: value score = 50% forward-looking (price to projected earnings, 50%) + 50% historical (price to book 12.5%, price to sales 12.5%, price to cash flow 12.5%, dividend yield 12.5%); growth score = 50% forward-looking (long-term projected earnings growth, 50%) + 50% historical (historical earnings growth 12.5%, sales growth 12.5%, cash flow growth 12.5%, book value growth 12.5%); scores scaled 0-100, difference = net style score; strongly negative → value, strongly positive → growth, close to zero → core.
new_option: value score = 50% forward-looking (price to projected earnings, 50%) + 50% historical (price to book 12.5%, price to sales 12.5%, price to cash flow 12.5%, dividend yield 12.5%).

original_option: value defined using three variables: book-value-to-price ratio, 12-month forward-earnings-to-price ratio, dividend yield; growth defined using five variables: long-term forward EPS growth rate, short-term forward EPS growth rate, current internal growth rate, long-term historical EPS growth trend, long-term historical sales-per-share growth trend; z-scores calculated and aggregated for each security to determine overall style characteristics; a 'value inclusion factor' of 0.6 means stock contributes 60% to value index and 40% to growth index.
new_option: value defined using three variables: book-value-to-price ratio, 12-month forward-earnings-to-price ratio, dividend yield.

original_option: holdings-based generally more accurate than returns-based because uses actual portfolio holdings; requires availability of all portfolio constituents and style attributes of each stock; point-in-time databases required to track historical style changes; Kaplan (2011) argued returns-based models impose unnecessary constraints that limit results (harder to detect aggressive positions like deep value or micro cap); limited data on derivatives makes holdings-based less effective for funds with substantial derivatives positions; ideal to use both approaches (returns-based more widely applicable; holdings-based deeper).
new_option: holdings-based generally more accurate than returns-based because uses actual portfolio holdings.

original_option: fund's investment strategy usually described in fund prospectus and can be used to identify fund's investment objective; this objective can be regarded as manager's self-identification of investment style; returns-based or holdings-based analysis commonly used to determine whether style corresponds to self-identified style; styles of equity hedge funds, equity income funds, and special sector funds more efficiently identified using combination of manager self-identification and holdings-/returns-based analysis; some equity hedge fund styles (long/short equity, market neutral, dedicated short bias) are non-standard.
new_option: fund's investment strategy usually described in fund prospectus and can be used to identify fund's investment objective.

original_option: (1) strategically adjusting active weights to create long-term exposures to rewarded risks different from benchmark; (2) tactically adjusting active weights using skills to identify mispricing (alpha not explained by long-term rewarded risk exposure); (3) assuming excessive idiosyncratic risk that may result in lucky/unlucky returns.
new_option: (1) strategically adjusting active weights to create long-term exposures to rewarded risks different from benchmark.

original_option: investment risks for which investors expect to be compensated through a long-run return premium (such as exposure to market risk and liquidity risk); existence well documented and supported by strong empirical evidence; recognition fundamentally altering investment management industry with large asset owners negotiating fee structures compensating managers for returns above/beyond simple rewarded factor exposure.
new_option: investment risks for which investors expect to be compensated through a long-run return premium (such as exposure to market risk and liquidity risk).

original_option: rewarded factor weightings, alpha skills, position sizing, and breadth of expertise; the first three encompass all of Equation 2; breadth of expertise is necessary to assemble the three main blocks into a successful portfolio construction process.
new_option: rewarded factor weightings, alpha skills, position sizing, and breadth of expertise.

original_option: E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC, where IC = expected information coefficient (extent to which forecasted active returns correspond to realized), BR = breadth (number of truly independent decisions made each year), TC = transfer coefficient (ability to translate insights into decisions without constraint; unconstrained portfolio TC = 1), σ_RA = manager's active risk.
new_option: E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC, where IC = expected information coefficient (extent to which forecasted active returns correspond to realized), BR = breadth (number of truly independent decisions made each year), TC = transfer coefficient (ability to translate insights into decisions without constraint.

original_option: manager could expect to generate active return of 15 bps yearly on average with single independent decision (0.10 × √1 × 6% × 0.25 = 0.15%); to achieve 1% excess return would need approximately 40 fully independent decisions (0.10 × √40 × 6% × 0.25 ≈ 0.95% ≈ 1%); constraint TC 0.25 representative of a constrained long-only investor.
new_option: manager could expect to generate active return of 15 bps yearly on average with single independent decision (0.10 × √1 × 6% × 0.25 = 0.15%).

original_option: uncorrelated decisions (much like two uncorrelated stocks are diversifying); not the same as individual securities; overweighting both GM and Toyota (two auto companies) relative to benchmark are not fully independent because much of their respective returns driven by common influences (consumer spending, gasoline price, steel/aluminum prices); must distinguish between nominal number of decisions and effective number of independent decisions.
new_option: uncorrelated decisions (much like two uncorrelated stocks are diversifying).

original_option: diversification is powerful tool but has limitations — even most diversified portfolio of high-volatility stocks (500-stock portfolio with 30% avg single-stock vol) cannot achieve same level of vol as portfolios of lower-vol stocks; even most concentrated portfolio of lower-vol stocks displays portfolio vol lower than highly diversified portfolio of higher-vol stocks; concentrated portfolio bears higher idiosyncratic risk.
new_option: diversification is powerful tool but has limitations — even most diversified portfolio of high-volatility stocks (500-stock portfolio with 30% avg single-stock vol) cannot achieve same level of vol as portfolios of lower-vol stocks.

original_option: Russell 1000 has Market β ≈ 0.99, Size β -0.16 (large-cap tilt), Value β 0.02, Momentum β -0.01, monthly alpha 0.05%; Russell 1000 Value has Market β 0.92, Size -0.23, Value 0.41, Momentum 0.13, alpha -0.05%; Value Fund has Market β 0.90, Size 0.13, Value 0.59 (large exposure), Momentum 0.09, monthly alpha -0.35%; factor monthly performance: Market 0.64%, Size 0.16%, Value 0.18%, Momentum 0.61%.
new_option: Russell 1000 has Market β ≈ 0.99, Size β -0.16 (large-cap tilt), Value β 0.02, Momentum β -0.01, monthly alpha 0.05%.

original_option: designed around construction of portfolios seeking to extract return premiums from balanced exposure to known rewarded factors; typically incorporate research-based rules across broad universe of securities; seek to reduce exposure to idiosyncratic risk and often use broadly diversified portfolios; typically more adaptable to a formal portfolio optimization process; unlikely to run concentrated portfolios.
new_option: designed around construction of portfolios seeking to extract return premiums from balanced exposure to known rewarded factors.

original_option: search for active returns by building greater depth of understanding of firm's governance, business model, and competitive landscape, through better factor proxies (e.g., better definition of Quality), or through successful timing strategies; integrate judgment of manager on smaller subset of securities; generally more concentrated portfolios reflecting depth of manager's insights; less formal approach to portfolio construction (building portfolio of securities deemed attractive subject to agreed-upon risk constraints).
new_option: search for active returns by building greater depth of understanding of firm's governance, business model, and competitive landscape, through better factor proxies (e.g., better definition of Quality), or through successful timing strategies.

original_option: top-down seeks to understand overall geo-political/economic/financial/social/public policy environment then project effect on countries/asset classes/sectors/securities; bottom-up develops understanding of environment by first evaluating risk-return characteristics of individual securities; both rely on returns from factors but top-down emphasizes macro factors while bottom-up emphasizes security-specific factors.
new_option: top-down seeks to understand overall geo-political/economic/financial/social/public policy environment then project effect on countries/asset classes/sectors/securities.

original_option: Discretionary top-down = emphasizes macro factors, factor timing, diversified; Discretionary bottom-up = emphasizes firm-specific characteristics/factors, potential factor timing, diversified or concentrated depending on strategy/style; Systematic top-down = emphasizes macro factors, factor timing, diversified; Systematic bottom-up = emphasizes security-specific factors, no factor timing, diversified; also: systematic managers unlikely to run concentrated portfolios; factor timing more likely among discretionary especially top-down; few managers exist in systematic top-down category.
new_option: Discretionary top-down = emphasizes macro factors, factor timing, diversified.

original_option: maximize risk-adjusted return — if risk measured by predicted active risk, objective seeks to maximize information ratio (ratio of active return to active risk); if risk measured by predicted portfolio volatility, objective seeks to maximize Sharpe ratio (ratio of return in excess of risk-free rate to portfolio volatility); ideally would specify net returns adjusted for implementation costs.
new_option: maximize risk-adjusted return — if risk measured by predicted active risk, objective seeks to maximize information ratio (ratio of active return to active risk).

original_option: limits on geographic, sector, industry, and single-security exposures; limits on transaction costs (to limit turnover and/or help manage liquidity issues); limits on exposure to specific factors (e.g., required minimum market capitalization for any single security, or maximum price-to-book ratio); constraints can be defined relative to benchmark or without regard to it.
new_option: limits on geographic, sector, industry, and single-security exposures.

original_option: do not integrate explicit expected return component but implicitly create exposure to risk factors; products built using such objective functions often exhibit Market beta below 1.0 and have statistically significant exposure to Value factor and to low-minus-high-β (BAB) factor; occurs because objective function that seeks to manage/minimize risk will tend to favor value and low-beta securities.
new_option: do not integrate explicit expected return component but implicitly create exposure to risk factors.

original_option: objectives and constraints of systematic managers are explicitly specified whereas those of discretionary managers are less explicitly specified; however, most managers at least conceptually optimize their portfolios using expected returns for each security, own view of risk, and constraints imposed by stated portfolio construction process or by client.
new_option: objectives and constraints of systematic managers are explicitly specified whereas those of discretionary managers are less explicitly specified.

original_option: philosophical divide seems to be shrinking; systematic and discretionary strategies were commonly differentiated in terms of breadth and depth (discretionary conducting more in-depth research on subset; systematic having more breadth); advancements in and accessibility of technology together with greater range of quality data allow discretionary to extend in-depth analyses across broader universe; technology allows systematic to design strategies capturing risk premiums in rewarded factors (previously considered part of alpha of discretionary managers).
new_option: philosophical divide seems to be shrinking.

original_option: each investment approach is implemented within a framework that specifies acceptable levels of active risk and Active Share relative to a clearly articulated benchmark (Active Share is a measure of how similar a portfolio is to its benchmark); manager may emphasize these dimensions to varying degrees to differentiate portfolio from benchmark; approaches also vary in extent to which they are benchmark aware versus benchmark agnostic.
new_option: each investment approach is implemented within a framework that specifies acceptable levels of active risk and Active Share relative to a clearly articulated benchmark (Active Share is a measure of how similar a portfolio is to its benchmark).

original_option: Active Share = ½ × Σ |Weight_portfolio,i − Weight_benchmark,i| where n represents total number of securities in either portfolio or benchmark; involves no statistical analysis or estimation; simple arithmetic; measures proportion of portfolio holdings different from benchmark; is 0 for portfolio matching benchmark and 100% for portfolio sharing no investments with benchmark.
new_option: Active Share = ½ × Σ |Weight_portfolio,i − Weight_benchmark,i| where n represents total number of securities in either portfolio or benchmark.

original_option: (1) including securities in the portfolio that are not in the benchmark; (2) holding securities in portfolio that are in benchmark but at weights different than benchmark weights; portfolio manager has complete control over Active Share because he determines weights of securities in his portfolio.
new_option: (1) including securities in the portfolio that are not in the benchmark.

original_option: portfolio manager can completely control Active Share but cannot completely control active risk because active risk depends on correlations and variances of securities beyond her control; realized active risk (historical SD of portfolio return minus benchmark return) is easy to calculate, but forward-looking predicted active risk requires estimates of correlations and variances.
new_option: portfolio manager can completely control Active Share but cannot completely control active risk because active risk depends on correlations and variances of securities beyond her control.

original_option: underweighting one bank stock to overweight another bank stock will likely have less effect on active risk than underweighting one bank stock and overweighting an information technology stock; active risk is affected by degree of cross correlation, but Active Share is not; if extent of underweighting and overweighting is the same in both cases, effect on Active Share would be identical but effect on active risk would differ because correlation of bank/tech pair is most likely lower than correlation of bank/bank pair.
new_option: underweighting one bank stock to overweight another bank stock will likely have less effect on active risk than underweighting one bank stock and overweighting an information technology stock.

original_option: (1) high net exposure to a risk factor leads to high level of active risk irrespective of level of idiosyncratic risk; (2) if factor exposure is fully neutralized, active risk will be entirely attributed to Active Share; (3) active risk attributed to Active Share will be smaller if number of securities is large and/or average idiosyncratic risk is small; (4) level of active risk will rise with increase in factor and idiosyncratic volatility (as occurred in 2008).
new_option: (1) high net exposure to a risk factor leads to high level of active risk irrespective of level of idiosyncratic risk.

original_option: pure indexing (low AS, low AR), closet indexing (low AS, low AR), factor neutral and diversified stock picks (high AS, low AR), diversified factor bets (low-medium AS, medium AR), concentrated factor bets (high AS, high AR), concentrated stock picks (high AS, high AR); a closet indexer is defined as a fund that advertises itself as being actively managed but is substantially similar to an index fund in its exposures.
new_option: pure indexing (low AS, low AR), closet indexing (low AS, low AR), factor neutral and diversified stock picks (high AS, low AR), diversified factor bets (low-medium AS, medium AR), concentrated factor bets (high AS, high AR), concentrated stock picks (high AS, high AR).

original_option: have low concentration among securities, often holding more than 250 positions (purpose is balanced exposure to risk factors and minimize idiosyncratic risks); diversified across factors and securities; typically high Active Share such as 0.70 but reasonably low active risk (tracking error) often in range of ±3%.
new_option: have low concentration among securities, often holding more than 250 positions (purpose is balanced exposure to risk factors and minimize idiosyncratic risks).

original_option: has both a high Active Share (typically above 0.90) and a high active risk (such as 8%-12% or higher); average active manager owns about 100 stocks and fewer than 20% of managers own more than 200 stocks; level of idiosyncratic risk in average active discretionary portfolio greater than that of average multi-factor fund.
new_option: has both a high Active Share (typically above 0.90) and a high active risk (such as 8%-12% or higher).

original_option: Vanguard Index Fund (indexed): 0.0% active risk, 0.00 Active Share; RiverSource Disciplined Equity (large-cap growth, small active weight, limited factor timing): 4.4% active risk, 0.54 Active Share; T. Rowe Price Mid-Cap Value (limited active weights on sectors but significant stock picking): 5.4% active risk, 0.93 Active Share; AIM Constellation (large-cap growth, significant sector bets): 9.7% active risk, 0.66 Active Share.
new_option: Vanguard Index Fund (indexed): 0.0% active risk, 0.00 Active Share.

original_option: process by which total risk appetite of portfolio is allocated among various components of portfolio choice; portfolio manager seeks to optimize portfolio's exposures relative to benchmark to ensure choices among stocks/sectors/countries make efficient use of active risk budget; ex ante active risk is just one possible measure of risk.
new_option: process by which total risk appetite of portfolio is allocated among various components of portfolio choice.

original_option: (1) determine which type of risk measure is most appropriate to strategy; (2) understand how each aspect of strategy contributes to overall risk (total portfolio variance may be dominated by exposure to rewarded risk factors or by allocations to countries/sectors/securities; if these exposures dynamic, timing also introduces risk); (3) determine what level of risk budget is appropriate; (4) properly allocate risk among individual positions/factors.
new_option: (1) determine which type of risk measure is most appropriate to strategy.

original_option: driven by mandate of manager and goals of investors; if mandate is to outperform market index over horizon, manager focuses on active risk; if investment objective expressed in terms of total returns, manager focuses on volatility of portfolio returns; long/short equity manager benchmarked against cash plus target usually prefers absolute risk measure; long-only equity manager benchmarked against cap-weighted index may prefer relative risk measure.
new_option: driven by mandate of manager and goals of investors.
