new_option: may take following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with person committing violation are unsuccessful: FIRST step is to attempt to stop behavior by bringing it to attention of employer through supervisor or firm's compliance department.

original_option: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates; although failure to report is less likely to be construed as violation than failure to dissociate from unethical conduct, impact of inactivity on integrity of capital markets can be significant; Code and Standards do NOT compel members and candidates to report violations to their governmental or regulatory organizations UNLESS such disclosure is mandatory under applicable law (voluntary reporting is often referred to as 'whistle-blowing'); such disclosure may be prudent under certain circumstances; should consult legal and compliance advisers for guidance.
new_option: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates.

original_option: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination; those responsible for providing services or creating/providing investment products must understand and comply with applicable laws and regulations in ALL relevant jurisdictions; must undertake necessary due diligence when transacting cross-border business to understand multiple applicable laws and regulations; should seek appropriate guidance including firm's compliance/legal departments and legal counsel outside organization to gain reasonable understanding of their responsibilities.
new_option: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination.

original_option: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation; this creates record supporting their position that they took appropriate steps to separate themselves from unethical activity.
new_option: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation.

original_option: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company; restricted lists help manage conflicts inherent in investment banking relationships.
new_option: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company.

original_option: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict; corporations should not reimburse members and candidates for air transportation.
new_option: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict.

original_option: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items; Standard I(B) does not preclude customary, ordinary business-related entertainment as long as its purpose is not to influence or reward members or candidates.
new_option: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items.

original_option: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients; if notification not possible prior to acceptance, must disclose to employer benefits previously accepted from clients; disclosure allows employer to make independent determination about extent to which gift may affect member's or candidate's independence and objectivity.
new_option: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients.

original_option: work of credit rating agencies raises concerns similar to those inherent in investment banking relationships; analysts may face pressure to issue ratings at specific level because of other services agency offers companies (advising on development of structured products); rating agencies need to develop necessary FIREWALLS and protections to allow independent operations of their different business lines; when using information provided by credit rating agencies, members and candidates should be mindful of potential conflicts of interest and may need to INDEPENDENTLY VALIDATE the rating granted.
new_option: work of credit rating agencies raises concerns similar to those inherent in investment banking relationships.

original_option: having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate ONLY when conflicts are adequately and effectively managed; any such conflict that is not avoided must be disclosed (see Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts); requires firms to carefully balance conflicts of interest inherent in collaboration.
new_option: having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate ONLY when conflicts are adequately and effectively managed.

original_option: members and candidates may find themselves on either side of manager selection process; can be involved in selecting external managers to receive portions of firm's or client's assets to manage OR represent their firms in trying to be selected to manage external client's assets; both sides of process must maintain their independence and objectivity to ensure decisions are made in best interest of firm's or client's assets; must not attempt to compromise objectivity of person making selection through offering gifts, benefits, or other considerations.
new_option: members and candidates may find themselves on either side of manager selection process.

original_option: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information; members and candidates who publish content through websites should also ensure that all reasonable precautions have been taken to protect website's integrity, confidentiality, and security and that website does not misrepresent any information and provides full disclosure.
new_option: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information.

original_option: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report; (2) attribute quotations — attribute to sources any direct quotations including projections, tables, statistics, model/product ideas, and new methodologies prepared by persons other than recognized financial and statistical reporting services or similar sources; (3) attribute summaries — attribute to sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by others.
new_option: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report.

original_option: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations; whether or not employer provides guidance, members and candidates must make certain they understand services firm performs and its qualifications and disclose that information without misrepresentation; each member/candidate should prepare summary of his or her own qualifications and experience and list of services member/candidate is capable of performing.
new_option: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations.

original_option: members and candidates must exercise care and diligence when incorporating third-party information into their own work; misrepresentations that result from using materials produced by outside parties BECOME THE RESPONSIBILITY of members and candidates when they incorporate that material into or make it part of their work; when providing information to clients from third parties, share responsibility for accuracy of marketing and distribution materials pertaining to third party's capabilities, services, and products; must disclose their intended use of external third parties and must not represent the work of others as their own.
new_option: members and candidates must exercise care and diligence when incorporating third-party information into their own work.

original_option: performance benchmark selection process is another area where misrepresentations may occur; members and candidates may misrepresent success of their performance record through presenting benchmarks that are not comparable to their strategies; clients can be misled if benchmark's results are not reported on basis comparable to that of the fund's or client's results; best practice is selecting the most appropriate available benchmark from universe of available options; transparent presentation of appropriate performance benchmarks is important in providing clients with information useful for investment decisions; Standard I(C) does not require that a benchmark always be provided in order to comply; some investment strategies may not lend themselves to displaying an appropriate benchmark because of complexity or diversity of investments; when reference indexes such as 'cash plus' basis are used for hedge fund, must disclose reasons behind use of this reference index.
new_option: performance benchmark selection process is another area where misrepresentations may occur.

original_option: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source; when different options are available, members and candidates may be TEMPTED to switch providers to obtain higher security valuations; process of shopping for values may misrepresent security's worth, lead to misinformed decisions to sell or hold investment, and result in overcharging clients for advisory fees; must take reasonable steps to provide accurate and reliable security pricing information to clients on consistent basis; changing pricing providers must NOT be based solely on justification that new provider reports higher current value of security; consistency in valuation process will improve value of security pricing information.
new_option: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source.

original_option: omission of fact or outcome can be misleading, especially in use of models and technical analysis processes; when inputs are knowingly omitted, resulting outcomes may provide misleading information; additionally, outcomes from models must NOT be presented as fact because they represent expected results based on inputs and analysis process; omissions in performance measurement and attribution process can also misrepresent manager's performance and skill; members and candidates must NOT misrepresent performance history by engaging in practices that distort past investment performance (e.g., cherry-picking highest-performing accounts and presenting them as representative of a strategy).
new_option: omission of fact or outcome can be misleading, especially in use of models and technical analysis processes.

original_option: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession; activities in which member/candidate represents to others he/she is a CFA charterholder, candidate, or CFA Institute member; conduct while working as unpaid officer, representative, or volunteer with CFA Institute or local society; conduct while attending or participating in CFA Institute, local society, or industry-related meeting, course, conference, or event; writing about investments or markets in articles, books, reports, message boards, or blogs outside workplace or academia; communicating with government agency, regulator, or other professional organization regarding information within scope of member's/candidate's professional responsibilities related to investment profession.
new_option: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession.

original_option: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard; determination depends on facts and circumstances.
new_option: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard.

original_option: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics; CFA Institute is aware of this issue, and appropriate disciplinary policies, procedures, and enforcement mechanisms are in place to address misuse of the Code and Standards and the Professional Conduct Program in this way.
new_option: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics.

original_option: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program; studying for or earning professional certifications or designations; attending conferences, seminars, or webinars; regularly participating in training offered by their employer; diligently engaging in informal continuing education or self-study (outside reading of subject matter articles, treatises, publications); participating in expert groups or organizations; becoming proficient with any new skill or knowledge, as necessary, when their professional responsibilities change.
new_option: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program.

original_option: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan; members and candidates can attain and maintain the competence needed to fulfill their professional responsibilities in a variety of ways.
new_option: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan.

original_option: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied; skills = capabilities to perform role-specific act or function to complete specific tasks and achieve professional goals; abilities = capabilities and attitudes that support behaviors that result in observable outcomes.
new_option: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied.

original_option: competence is NOT limited to examination of the education level of a member or candidate; highly educated investment consultant may not have experience to undertake consulting activities in unfamiliar area of practice; accounting professional supremely competent in providing financial statement audits may not be competent in assessing compliance with performance presentation standards (such as GIPS standards); investment consultant hired to conduct search for investment manager focused on sustainable investing may not have skills needed to competently evaluate managers with that focus.
new_option: competence is NOT limited to examination of the education level of a member or candidate.

original_option: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities; members and candidates will develop and refine their skills and abilities as their careers progress; Standard I(E) imposes duty to not simply achieve but also MAINTAIN competence, emphasizing need for members to continuously maintain or improve competence required of their professional position.
new_option: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities.

original_option: information regarding pending sale is both material and nonpublic; Staple violated Standard II(A) by communicating inside information to his broker; Halsey also violated the standard by buying the shares on the basis of material nonpublic information.
new_option: information regarding pending sale is both material and nonpublic.

original_option: Peter violated Standard II(A) because he failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of material nonpublic information to others in his firm; Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent communication of nonpublic information between departments; firm personnel who are members or candidates and who traded on the information also violated Standard II(A) by trading on inside information.
new_option: Peter violated Standard II(A) because he failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of material nonpublic information to others in his firm.

original_option: Levenson must first determine whether material information is public; according to Standard II(A), if company has not made this information public (a small group forum does NOT qualify as a method of public dissemination), she must NOT use the information.
new_option: Levenson must first determine whether material information is public.

original_option: although information on an expected takeover bid may be generally material and nonpublic, in this case the SOURCE of information is UNRELIABLE, so the information cannot be considered material; therefore, Fechtman is not prohibited from trading the stock based on this information.
new_option: although information on an expected takeover bid may be generally material and nonpublic, in this case the SOURCE of information is UNRELIABLE, so the information cannot be considered material.

original_option: while company information on quarterly earnings data is material and nonpublic, Teja based his conclusion about the earnings drop on public information and pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic information (such as opinions of designers and retailers); therefore, trading based on Teja's correct conclusion is not prohibited by Standard II(A).
new_option: while company information on quarterly earnings data is material and nonpublic, Teja based his conclusion about the earnings drop on public information and pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic information (such as opinions of designers and retailers).

original_option: to reach conclusion about the value of the company, Clement pieced together a number of nonmaterial or public bits of information that affect Turgot Chariots; therefore, under the mosaic theory, Clement did NOT violate Standard II(A) in drafting the report.
new_option: to reach conclusion about the value of the company, Clement pieced together a number of nonmaterial or public bits of information that affect Turgot Chariots.

original_option: when Zito receives advance notice of Clement's change of opinion, she knows it will have a material impact on the stock price, even if she is not aware of Clement's underlying reasoning; she is not a client of Clement but obtains early access to material nonpublic information prior to publication; her trades are based on material nonpublic information, thus she violated Standard II(A); Zito further violated the standard by relaying the information to her father — it would NOT matter if he or any other family member traded; the act of PROVIDING the information violated Standard II(A).
new_option: when Zito receives advance notice of Clement's change of opinion, she knows it will have a material impact on the stock price, even if she is not aware of Clement's underlying reasoning.

original_option: even though Kellogg believes that Mayfield would not break the law by disclosing inside information, Kellogg violated Standard II(A) by purchasing additional shares of National; the fact that he lost money on the purchase does NOT absolve Kellogg for trading on material nonpublic information; it is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information.
new_option: even though Kellogg believes that Mayfield would not break the law by disclosing inside information, Kellogg violated Standard II(A) by purchasing additional shares of National.

original_option: there are often rumors and 'whisper numbers' before release of any kind; text message from the other trader would most likely be considered market noise; unless Nadler knew that the trader had an ongoing business relationship with the public firm, he had no reason to suspect he was receiving material nonpublic information that would prevent him from completing the trading request of the portfolio manager.
new_option: there are often rumors and 'whisper numbers' before release of any kind.

original_option: McCoy is appropriately using expert networks to enhance her evaluation process; she neither asked for nor received information that may be considered material and nonpublic (such as preliminary trial results); McCoy is allowed to seek advice from professionals within the industry that she follows.
new_option: McCoy is appropriately using expert networks to enhance her evaluation process.

original_option: Watson violated Standard II(A) by passing along material nonpublic information concerning the ongoing product tests, which the fund used to trade in the securities and options of the related company; Watson cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity; he must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.
new_option: Watson violated Standard II(A) by passing along material nonpublic information concerning the ongoing product tests, which the fund used to trade in the securities and options of the related company.

original_option: placing the securities mentioned as part of the merger on the firm's restricted trading list; guidance for Standard II(A) recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information; by adding these securities to this list, Andrews would uphold this standard; waiting until next day will not ensure news of the merger is made public — negotiations may take longer or merger may never happen — Andrews must wait until information is disseminated to market; should NOT disclose information to other managers, no trading allowed on MNPI.
new_option: placing the securities mentioned as part of the merger on the firm's restricted trading list.

original_option: Hart violated Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information; Hart's decision to invest in retail fund appears directly correlated with Rodriguez's statement about the successful quarter of Mega Retail; Rodriguez's information would be considered material because it would influence share price of Mega Retail and probably influence price of entire exchange-traded retail fund; Rodriguez also shared MNPI — sharing of such information in conversation between friends also violates Standard II(A).
new_option: Hart violated Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.

original_option: in addition to substance and specificity of information, source or relative reliability of information also determines materiality; less reliable a source, less likely information provided would be considered material; factual information from corporate insider regarding significant new contract is likely to be material, whereas assumption based on speculation by competitor about same contract is likely to be less reliable and, therefore, not material; information about trials of new drug/product/service under development from qualified personnel involved in trials is likely material; educated conjecture by subject experts NOT connected to the trials is unlikely to be material.
new_option: in addition to substance and specificity of information, source or relative reliability of information also determines materiality.

original_option: the more ambiguous the effect of the information on price, the LESS material that information is considered; if it is unclear whether and to what extent the information will affect price of a security, the information may not be considered material; passage of time may also render information that was once important immaterial.
new_option: the more ambiguous the effect of the information on price, the LESS material that information is considered.

original_option: information is 'nonpublic' until it has been disseminated or is available to the marketplace in general (as opposed to a select group of investors); 'disseminated' can be defined as 'made known' — for example, company report of profits posted on internet and distributed widely through press release or accompanied by a filing has been effectively disseminated to the marketplace; must have reasonable expectation that people have received the information before it can be considered public; is NOT necessary to wait for slowest method of delivery.
new_option: information is 'nonpublic' until it has been disseminated or is available to the marketplace in general (as opposed to a select group of investors).

original_option: must be particularly aware of information that is selectively disclosed by corporations to small group of investors, analysts, or other market participants; information that is made available to analysts remains nonpublic until it is made available to investors in general; corporations that disclose information on a limited basis create the potential for insider-trading violations; issues of selective disclosure may arise when a corporate insider provides material information to analysts in a briefing or conference call BEFORE that information is released to the public; analysts must be aware that a disclosure made to a room full of analysts does NOT necessarily make the disclosed information 'public'.
new_option: must be particularly aware of information that is selectively disclosed by corporations to small group of investors, analysts, or other market participants.

original_option: a member or candidate MAY use insider information provided legitimately by source company for specific purpose of conducting due diligence according to business agreement between parties for such activities as mergers, loan underwriting, credit ratings, and offering engagements; in such instances, investment professional would NOT be considered in violation of Standard II(A) by using material information; HOWEVER, use of insider information provided by source company for OTHER purposes, especially to trade or entice others to trade the securities of the firm, is a violation of this standard.
new_option: a member or candidate MAY use insider information provided legitimately by source company for specific purpose of conducting due diligence according to business agreement between parties for such activities as mergers, loan underwriting, credit ratings, and offering engagements.

original_option: important for investment professionals to understand implications of using information from internet and social media platforms because all such information may not actually be considered public; some social media platforms require membership in specific groups to access published content; members and candidates participating in groups with membership limitations should VERIFY that material information obtained from these sources can also be accessed from a source that would be considered available to the public BEFORE using that information.
new_option: important for investment professionals to understand implications of using information from internet and social media platforms because all such information may not actually be considered public.

original_option: when a particularly well-known or respected analyst issues a report or makes changes to his or her recommendation, that information alone may influence the market and thus may be considered material; the analyst is NOT a company insider, however, and does not have access to inside information; presumably, analyst created report from information available to public (mosaic theory) and by using expertise to interpret information; analyst's hard work paid for by the client generated the conclusions; simply because public in general would find conclusions material does not require analyst make his or her work public; investors who are not clients of the analyst can either do the work themselves or become clients of the analyst.
new_option: when a particularly well-known or respected analyst issues a report or makes changes to his or her recommendation, that information alone may influence the market and thus may be considered material.

original_option: can use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in the analyst's analysis; mosaic theory states that an analyst may use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in creating a larger picture than shown by any individual piece of information — conclusions the analyst reaches become material only after the pieces are assembled.
new_option: can use material public information and nonmaterial nonpublic information in the analyst's analysis.

original_option: financial analyst gathers and interprets large quantities of information from many sources; analyst may use significant conclusions derived from analysis of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information as basis for investment recommendations and decisions EVEN IF those conclusions would have been material inside information had they been communicated directly to the analyst by a company; under 'mosaic theory,' financial analysts are free to act on this collection, or mosaic, of information without risking violation.
new_option: financial analyst gathers and interprets large quantities of information from many sources.

original_option: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they SHOULD save and document all their research; evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.
new_option: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they SHOULD save and document all their research.

original_option: members and candidates may provide compensation to individuals for their insights without violating this standard; however, members and candidates are ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE for ensuring that they are not requesting or acting on confidential information received from external experts, which may violate security regulations and would violate this standard; firms connecting experts with members or candidates often require both parties to sign agreements concerning disclosure of material nonpublic information; even with protections from such compliance practices, if an expert provides MNPI, members and candidates are PROHIBITED from taking investment actions using this information until the information becomes publicly known to the market.
new_option: members and candidates may provide compensation to individuals for their insights without violating this standard.

original_option: prohibition on using this information goes BEYOND the direct buying and selling of individual securities or bonds; members and candidates must NOT use material nonpublic information to influence their investment actions related to DERIVATIVES, MUTUAL FUNDS, or other alternative investments; any trading based on material nonpublic information constitutes a violation of Standard II(A).
new_option: prohibition on using this information goes BEYOND the direct buying and selling of individual securities or bonds.

original_option: material information may include, but is not limited to, information on: earnings; mergers, acquisitions, tender offers, or joint ventures; changes in assets or asset quality; innovative products, processes, or discoveries; new licenses, patents, registered trademarks, or regulatory approval/rejection of product; developments regarding customers or suppliers (e.g., acquisition or loss of a contract); changes in management; change in auditor notification or fact that issuer may no longer rely on auditor's report or qualified opinion; events regarding issuer's securities (defaults on senior securities, calls for redemption, repurchase plans, stock splits, dividend changes, changes to rights of security holders, public/private sales of additional securities); bankruptcies; significant legal disputes; government reports of economic trends (employment, housing starts, currency information); orders for large trades before executed; new or changing equity or debt ratings issued by third party.
new_option: material information may include, but is not limited to, information on: earnings.

original_option: information is 'material' if its disclosure is likely to have an impact on the price of a security OR if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision; in other words, information is material if it would significantly alter the total mix of information currently available about a security in such a way that the price of the security would be affected.
new_option: information is 'material' if its disclosure is likely to have an impact on the price of a security OR if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision.

original_option: trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information erodes confidence in capital markets, institutions, and investment professionals by supporting the idea that those with inside information and special access can take unfair advantage of the general investing public; although trading on inside information may lead to short-term profits, in the long run, individuals and the profession as a whole suffer from such trading; these actions have caused and will continue to cause investors to avoid capital markets because markets are perceived to be 'rigged' in favor of the knowledgeable insider; when investing public avoids capital markets, markets and capital allocation become less efficient and less supportive of strong and vibrant economies.
new_option: trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information erodes confidence in capital markets, institutions, and investment professionals by supporting the idea that those with inside information and special access can take unfair advantage of the general investing public.

original_option: analysts should also be alert to the possibility that they are selectively receiving material nonpublic information when a company provides them with GUIDANCE or INTERPRETATION of such publicly available information as financial statements or regulatory filings; even when underlying information is public, additional interpretation from company insider may itself constitute MNPI.
new_option: analysts should also be alert to the possibility that they are selectively receiving material nonpublic information when a company provides them with GUIDANCE or INTERPRETATION of such publicly available information as financial statements or regulatory filings.

original_option: Gray violated Standard II(B) by fraudulently creating appearance that there was greater investor interest in the stock through the online rumors; additionally, through his trading strategy, he created appearance that there was greater liquidity in the stock than actually existed; he manipulated the price through both misinformation and trading practices.
new_option: Gray violated Standard II(B) by fraudulently creating appearance that there was greater investor interest in the stock through the online rumors.

original_option: by exaggerating her earnings projections in order to try to fuel a quick gain in Hygene's stock price, Gordon is in violation of Standard II(B); however, it would have been acceptable for Gordon to write a report that (1) framed her earnings projection in a RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) outlined CLEARLY the assumptions used in her Hygene models that took into consideration findings from her trip through Latin America.
new_option: by exaggerating her earnings projections in order to try to fuel a quick gain in Hygene's stock price, Gordon is in violation of Standard II(B).

original_option: place buy order for 2,000 shares (four times average weekly volume) through Vogt for his personal account; through placing a personal purchase order that is significantly greater than average volume, Pietro is violating Standard II(B) Market Manipulation — he is attempting to manipulate an increase in the share price and thus bring a buyer to the negotiating table; the news of possible merger and confirmation of firm's earnings guidance may also have positive effects but Pietro's actions in instructing release of information does not represent violation through market manipulation; announcements are common and practical to keep investors informed.
new_option: place buy order for 2,000 shares (four times average weekly volume) through Vogt for his personal account.

original_option: information-based manipulation includes, but is not limited to, spreading false rumors to induce trading by others; members and candidates must refrain from 'pumping up' the price of an investment by issuing misleading positive information or overly optimistic projections of a security's worth only to later 'dump' the investment (i.e., sell it) once the price, fueled by the misleading information's effect on other market participants, reaches an artificially high level.
new_option: information-based manipulation includes, but is not limited to, spreading false rumors to induce trading by others.

original_option: transaction-based manipulation involves instances where member/candidate knows or should know that his or her actions could distort the price of a security; this type of manipulation includes, but is not limited to: (1) transactions that artificially affect prices or volume to give impression of activity or price movement in a financial instrument, which represent diversion from expectations of fair and efficient market; (2) securing a controlling, dominant position in a financial instrument to exploit and manipulate the price of a related derivative and/or the underlying asset.
new_option: transaction-based manipulation involves instances where member/candidate knows or should know that his or her actions could distort the price of a security.

original_option: Standard II(B) is NOT intended to preclude transactions undertaken on legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies; legitimate trading activity may APPEAR abusive, especially where the market is illiquid or volatile; the INTENT of the action is critical to determining whether it is a violation of this standard.
new_option: Standard II(B) is NOT intended to preclude transactions undertaken on legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies.

original_option: orders entered for an unusually short time period; orders above market price; orders resulting in no change in beneficial ownership; orders that intend to change the bid or offer prices and are canceled before they are executed; orders on both sides of the trade using different platforms; coordinated action by two or more traders in a market with a small number of participants.
new_option: orders entered for an unusually short time period.

original_option: market manipulation damages the interests of all investors by disrupting the smooth functioning of financial markets and lowering investor confidence; market manipulation may lead to a lack of trust in fairness of the capital markets, resulting in higher risk premiums and reduced investor participation; a reduction in efficiency of a local capital market may negatively affect growth and economic health of the country and may also influence operations of the globally interconnected capital markets.
new_option: market manipulation damages the interests of all investors by disrupting the smooth functioning of financial markets and lowering investor confidence.

original_option: the fact that a member/candidate lost money on a trade based on material nonpublic information does NOT absolve the member/candidate for trading on MNPI; it is the member's or candidate's responsibility to make sure, before executing investment actions, that comments about earnings are not material nonpublic information; per Example 8 (Kellogg): even though he lost money, he violated Standard II(A).
new_option: the fact that a member/candidate lost money on a trade based on material nonpublic information does NOT absolve the member/candidate for trading on MNPI.

original_option: if member/candidate obtains material nonpublic information, should make reasonable efforts to achieve public dissemination of the information; these efforts usually entail encouraging the issuing company to make the information public; if public dissemination is not possible, member/candidate must communicate the information only to the designated supervisory and compliance personnel within his/her firm and must not take investment action or alter current investment recommendations based on the information; moreover, must not knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.
new_option: if member/candidate obtains material nonpublic information, should make reasonable efforts to achieve public dissemination of the information.

original_option: members and candidates must comply with all compliance procedures adopted by their employer to prevent misuse of material nonpublic information; such procedures might address such areas as: review of employee and proprietary trading; review of investment recommendations; supervision of interdepartmental communications in multiservice firms; informing compliance personnel of suspected inappropriate use of material nonpublic information.
new_option: members and candidates must comply with all compliance procedures adopted by their employer to prevent misuse of material nonpublic information.

original_option: members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed; if material nonpublic information is disclosed for the first time in an analyst meeting or call, members and candidates should encourage the company to promptly issue a press release or otherwise make the information publicly available.
new_option: members and candidates should encourage companies they follow to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to script those meetings and calls to decrease the chance that further information will be disclosed.

original_option: establish physical and informational barriers within firm to prevent exchange of information between the investment banking and brokerage operations; the best policy to prevent violation of Standard II(A) is the establishment of firewalls in a firm to prevent exchange of insider information; physical and informational barrier of a firewall between investment banking department and brokerage operation prevents the investment banking department from providing information to analysts on the brokerage side who may be writing recommendations on a company stock; prohibiting recommendations is an alternative — but permanent prohibition is not the best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI becomes public, resuming publishing is not a violation; monitoring exchanges is not effective because NO exchange of information should be occurring between the departments.
new_option: establish physical and informational barriers within firm to prevent exchange of information between the investment banking and brokerage operations.

original_option: elements of firewall system include, but not limited to: substantial control of relevant interdepartmental communications, preferably through clearance area within firm in either compliance or legal department; review of employee trading through maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists; documentation of procedures designed to limit flow of information between departments and of actions taken to enforce those procedures; heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while firm is in possession of material nonpublic information.
new_option: elements of firewall system include, but not limited to: substantial control of relevant interdepartmental communications, preferably through clearance area within firm in either compliance or legal department.

original_option: there should be NO overlap of personnel between investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments; for a firewall to be effective in a multiservice firm, an employee should be on ONLY ONE side of the firewall at any time.
new_option: there should be NO overlap of personnel between investment banking and corporate finance areas of a brokerage firm and the sales and research departments or between a bank's commercial lending department and its trust and research departments.

original_option: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company; that analyst must then be treated as though he/she were an investment banker; must remain on investment banking side of the wall until any information he/she learns is publicly disclosed; in short, analyst cannot use any information learned in the course of the project for research purposes and cannot share that information with colleagues in research department.
new_option: analyst who follows a particular company may provide limited assistance to investment bankers under carefully controlled circumstances when firm's investment banking department is involved in a deal with the company.

original_option: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments; if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared; if sharing is necessary, compliance officer should coordinate the process of 'LOOKING OVER THE WALL' so that necessary information will be shared and integrity of procedure will be maintained.
new_option: primary objective of an effective firewall procedure is to establish a reporting system in which authorized people review and approve communications between departments.

original_option: members and candidates must comply with their employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading by employees; such practices may include firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.
new_option: members and candidates must comply with their employers' restrictions or prohibitions on personal trading by employees.

original_option: guidance recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information; by adding these securities to this list, member/candidate would uphold Standard II(A); restricted list is one of the tools that prevents both trading and issuance of recommendations while firm holds MNPI.
new_option: guidance recommends adding securities to firm's restricted list when firm has or may have material nonpublic information.

original_option: an information barrier commonly referred to as 'firewall' is the most widely used approach for preventing the communication of material nonpublic information within firms; it restricts the flow of confidential information to those who need to know the information to perform their jobs effectively.
new_option: an information barrier commonly referred to as 'firewall' is the most widely used approach for preventing the communication of material nonpublic information within firms.

original_option: members and candidates must NOT knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information; this is part of the achieve public dissemination compliance practice.
new_option: members and candidates must NOT knowingly engage in any conduct that may induce company insiders to privately disclose material nonpublic information.

original_option: Watson (Example 11) illustrates: analyst cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity; must make his own determination whether information he received through these arrangements reaches a materiality threshold that would influence his trading.
new_option: Watson (Example 11) illustrates: analyst cannot simply rely on the agreements signed by individuals who participate in expert networks that state that he has not received information that would prohibit his trading activity.

original_option: controls that address the use of trading algorithms for manipulative trading practices; algorithms represent a specific area of concern because their speed and complexity can be leveraged for manipulation without human oversight of individual trades.
new_option: controls that address the use of trading algorithms for manipulative trading practices.

original_option: trade exception reporting — a compliance practice designed to identify trades that deviate from expected patterns or trigger established thresholds, allowing compliance and supervisory personnel to review the trades for possible manipulative activity.
new_option: trade exception reporting.

original_option: prohibiting recommendations of stock of companies that are clients of investment banking department is an ALTERNATIVE to firewalls, but PERMANENT prohibition would not be best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI obtained by IB department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of Code and Standards — the information no longer gives brokerage operation an advantage.
new_option: prohibiting recommendations of stock of companies that are clients of investment banking department is an ALTERNATIVE to firewalls, but PERMANENT prohibition would not be best answer because once an offering is complete and MNPI obtained by IB department becomes public, resuming publishing recommendations on the stock is not a violation of Code and Standards.

original_option: review of employee trading through the maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists is one of the elements of a firewall system; this creates layered categorization of securities under different levels of monitoring/restriction.
new_option: review of employee trading through the maintenance of 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists is one of the elements of a firewall system.

original_option: heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information is an element of a firewall system — recognizes that proprietary trading is high-risk area for MNPI misuse.
new_option: heightened review or restriction of proprietary trading while a firm is in possession of material nonpublic information is an element of a firewall system.

original_option: encourage companies to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to SCRIPT those meetings/calls to decrease chance further information will be disclosed; if material nonpublic information is disclosed for first time in analyst meeting or call, encourage company to promptly issue press release or otherwise make information publicly available.
new_option: encourage companies to issue press releases prior to analyst meetings and conference calls and to SCRIPT those meetings/calls to decrease chance further information will be disclosed.

original_option: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research; evidence of the analyst's knowledge of public and nonmaterial nonpublic information about a corporation strengthens the assertion that the analyst reached his or her conclusions solely through appropriate methods rather than through using material nonpublic information.
new_option: although analysts are free to use mosaic information in their research reports, they should save and document all their research.

original_option: acceptable practice is to write reports that (1) FRAME earnings projections in RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) OUTLINE CLEARLY the assumptions used in models that took into consideration relevant findings from research; this contrasts with Example 5 Gordon's violation of exaggerating single-point projections to force company guidance updates.
new_option: acceptable practice is to write reports that (1) FRAME earnings projections in RANGE of possible outcomes and (2) OUTLINE CLEARLY the assumptions used in models that took into consideration relevant findings from research.

original_option: per Example 2 (Peter/Bright Ideas): Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm; firm-level information barriers protect against personnel overhearing/observing MNPI during interdepartmental communications.
new_option: per Example 2 (Peter/Bright Ideas): Peter's firm should have adopted information barriers to prevent the communication of nonpublic information between departments of the firm.

original_option: if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared; if sharing is necessary, compliance officer should coordinate the process of 'looking over the wall' so that necessary information will be shared and integrity of procedure will be maintained.
new_option: if employee behind firewall believes he/she needs to share confidential information with someone on the other side, employee should consult designated compliance officer to determine whether sharing is necessary and how much information should be shared.

original_option: such practices may include: firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies; combination of monitoring, reporting, and list-maintenance forms comprehensive personal trading compliance program.
new_option: such practices may include: firm monitoring of personal trading, mandating periodic reports of personal transactions, and implementing a restricted list and watch list of companies.

original_option: participants and beneficiaries of Goodner's pension plan; when the manager is responsible for portfolios of pension plans or trusts, the client is not the person or entity that hires the manager but, rather, the beneficiaries of the plan or trust; duty of loyalty is owed to the ultimate beneficiaries.
new_option: participants and beneficiaries of Goodner's pension plan.

original_option: Wiley, acting on behalf of his employer which was trustee for pension plan, clearly violated Standard III(A); he used pension plan to perpetuate existing management, perhaps to detriment of plan participants and company's shareholders, and to benefit himself; Wiley's responsibilities to plan participants and beneficiaries must take precedence over any ties of his bank to corporate managers and over his self-interest; Wiley had duty to examine takeover offer on its own merits and to make an independent decision; guiding principle is appropriateness of investment decision to the pension plan, not whether decision benefited Wiley or company that hired him.
new_option: Wiley, acting on behalf of his employer which was trustee for pension plan, clearly violated Standard III(A).

original_option: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients; because Jackson is not upholding his duty of loyalty, he is violating Standard III(A).
new_option: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients.

original_option: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence; may trade other accounts through Scott as reward for directing clients to Everett only if accounts receive best price and execution AND practice is disclosed to the accounts; because Everett does not disclose the directed trading, Everett violated Standard III(A).
new_option: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence.

original_option: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms; Rome is placing her own interests before those of her clients.
new_option: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms.

original_option: Parker is violating Standard III(A) by not exercising her duty of loyalty to her clients; must determine whether commissions charged by SouthAM are reasonable in relation to benefit of research provided; must determine whether best execution and prices could be received from SouthAM; the 5 extra days are NOT part of research effort — they do not assist in investment decision-making, so the hotel expenses for the 5 days must NOT be paid for with client commission dollars.
new_option: Parker is violating Standard III(A) by not exercising her duty of loyalty to her clients.

original_option: Knauss violated Standard III(A) because she is using the assets of her clients to benefit her firm and herself; even though individual securities are appropriate, excessive turnover to hit firm's commission target places firm/self interests ahead of clients.
new_option: Knauss violated Standard III(A) because she is using the assets of her clients to benefit her firm and herself.

original_option: Dill violated Standard III(A) because he did not act for the benefit of his brother's account or his other accounts; the brother's account is a regular fee-paying account comparable to accounts of his other clients; by not allocating shares proportionately across all accounts for which he thought IPO was suitable, Dill disadvantaged specific clients; would have been correct in not allocating shares to his brother's account IF that account was being managed OUTSIDE the normal fee structure of the firm.
new_option: Dill violated Standard III(A) because he did not act for the benefit of his brother's account or his other accounts.

original_option: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound; Hensley must NOT let law firm influence testimony she provides in the legal proceedings; in this situation, the law firm represents Hensley's employer and 'who is the client' is not well defined.
new_option: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound.

original_option: Spears' statement is not entirely accurate; because SHE provides the advisory services to her new client, she ALONE is bound by the duty of loyalty to this client; Miller's responsibility is to manage the fund according to the investment policy statement of the fund; his actions must NOT be influenced by needs of any particular fund investor.
new_option: Spears' statement is not entirely accurate.

original_option: Nguyen's actions align with requirements of Standard III(A); even though correction may lead to firm's termination by client, withholding information on errors is NOT in best interest of client; loyalty requires accuracy even when unfavorable to member's firm.
new_option: Nguyen's actions align with requirements of Standard III(A).

original_option: in this instance, Sulejman serves in an execution-only capacity; his duty of loyalty, prudence, and care is centered on the skill and diligence used when executing trades — namely by seeking best execution and making trades within the parameters set by the clients (instructions on quantity, price, timing, etc.); acting in the best interests of the client dictates that trades are executed on the most favorable terms that can be achieved for the client; given this job function, the requirements of the Code and Standards for loyalty, prudence, and care clearly do NOT impose a fiduciary duty.
new_option: in this instance, Sulejman serves in an execution-only capacity.

original_option: advise customer of the change in recommendation before accepting the order; per Standard III(B) Guidance: 'Clients who do not know that the member or candidate has changed a recommendation and who, therefore, place orders contrary to a current recommendation should be advised of the changed recommendation before the order is accepted.'
new_option: advise customer of the change in recommendation before accepting the order.

original_option: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts; Jackson must execute orders on a systematic basis to be fair to all clients.
new_option: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts.

original_option: Morris violated Standard III(B) by not treating all customers fairly; to meet obligations, Morris needed to REFRAIN from taking any shares himself and needed to PRORATE the distribution of shares to clients or use some other distribution method for treating clients fairly; should have avoided conflict of interest caused by the options by not seeking those additional benefits; because Morris did not avoid the conflict, he must disclose to his firm and to his clients that he received options as part of the deal (Standard VI(A) Disclosure of Conflicts).
new_option: Morris violated Standard III(B) by not treating all customers fairly.

original_option: Preston violated Standard III(B) by failing to deal fairly with her clients in taking these investment actions; Preston should have allocated trades PRIOR to executing the orders, OR she should have had a systematic approach to allocating trades, such as pro rata, as soon as it was practical after they were executed.
new_option: Preston violated Standard III(B) by failing to deal fairly with her clients in taking these investment actions.

original_option: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B); differential informal advice to only some clients constitutes selective disclosure that disadvantages other clients.
new_option: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B).

original_option: Weng did NOT violate Standard III(B); he widely disseminated the recommendation and information to all his clients PRIOR to discussing it with a select few; Weng's largest clients received additional personal service because they PAY HIGHER FEES; IF Weng had discussed the report with a select group of clients PRIOR to distributing it to all his clients, he would have violated Standard III(B).
new_option: Weng did NOT violate Standard III(B).

original_option: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size; with total allocation being significantly below amount requested, Hampton ensured that each client received at least minimum lot size of the issue and that filled allocations were close in [proportion].
new_option: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size.

original_option: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai; Smith violated Standard III(C) by applying Robertson's investment strategy to Lanai because the two clients' financial circumstances and objectives differ.
new_option: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai.

original_option: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis; basic characteristics of entire portfolio will largely determine whether investment recommendations are taking client factors into account; here, McDowell properly considers investment in context of entire portfolio and thoroughly explains investment to client.
new_option: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis.

original_option: Gubler violated Standard III(C); new investment locks up part of subsidiary's assets for at least 3 years and up to as many as 5 years or more; IPS requires investments in highly liquid investments and describes accepted asset classes; private equity investments with lockup period would not fall within the mandate; even without lockup, asset class with only occasional and thus implicitly illiquid market may not be suitable for the portfolio.
new_option: Gubler violated Standard III(C).

original_option: Snead violated Standard III(C) by investing clients' assets in high-beta stocks; these high-risk investments are contrary to long-term risk profile established in clients' IPSs; Snead has changed investment strategy of clients in attempt to reap short-term rewards offered by her firm's new compensation arrangement, NOT in response to changes in clients' IPSs.
new_option: Snead violated Standard III(C) by investing clients' assets in high-beta stocks.

original_option: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund; in this case, a preference for incorporating ESG issues into investment process is clearly mandated; trustees have a duty to ensure fund's assets are invested in accordance with IPS.
new_option: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund.

original_option: Kim has a responsibility to select investments that are suitable for the IPS objectives; he is PERMITTED to incorporate criteria beyond financial metrics, including but not limited to ESG issues, into the investment decision-making process; Kim's actions are NOT in conflict with his obligation to make effective suitability determinations.
new_option: Kim has a responsibility to select investments that are suitable for the IPS objectives.

original_option: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client; discussions and resulting actions may encompass a variety of scenarios depending on how the requested unsuitable investment relates to client's full portfolio; in discussing the trade, member/candidate should focus on educating the investor on how the request deviates from current policy statement; member/candidate should require client to acknowledge the discussion, including that the trade is against the advice of the member/candidate because it is unsuitable for the portfolio.
new_option: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client.

original_option: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate; the responsibility of these members and candidates is to invest in a manner consistent with the stated mandate; member/candidate who serves as fund manager for large-cap income fund would not be following fund mandate by investing heavily in small-cap companies or start-ups whose stock is speculative in nature; members and candidates who manage pooled assets to a specific mandate are NOT responsible for determining suitability of the fund as an investment for investors who may be purchasing shares in the fund; responsibility for determining suitability of an investment for clients can be conferred only on members and candidates who have an advisory relationship with clients.
new_option: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate.

original_option: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client; effort to determine needs and circumstances of each client is NOT a one-time occurrence; investment recommendations or decisions are usually part of ongoing process that takes into account diversity and changing nature of portfolio and client characteristics; passage of time is bound to produce changes important with respect to investment objectives.
new_option: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client.

original_option: Taylor's brochure is in violation of Standard III(D); Taylor must disclose that 25% growth occurred only in one year and only for firm's common trust fund; general claim of firm performance must take into account performance of ALL categories of accounts; by stating that clients can expect a steady 25% annual compound growth rate, Taylor is misrepresenting one portfolio's single-year actual performance as expected performance.
new_option: Taylor's brochure is in violation of Standard III(D).

original_option: Judd violated Standard III(D); when claiming compliance with GIPS standards, firms must meet ALL the requirements, make mandatory disclosures, and meet any other requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation; GIPS standards REQUIRE firms to asset-weight portfolio returns to calculate composite returns; Judd's violation is NOT from any misuse of the data but from publishing a performance report with her firm's FALSE claim of GIPS compliance.
new_option: Judd violated Standard III(D).

original_option: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind; Standard III(D) does NOT prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at prior firm as part of performance track record AS LONG AS showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and person's specific role in achieving that performance; if McCoy chooses to use his past performance from G&P in Mastermind's advertising, he must make full disclosure of the source of the historical performance.
new_option: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind.

original_option: Davis violated Standard III(D) by failing to clearly identify simulated performance results; Standard III(D) prohibits members and candidates from making any statements that misrepresent the performance achieved by them or their firms and requires them to make every reasonable effort to ensure performance information presented to clients is fair, accurate, and complete; Davis's use of simulated results must be accompanied by full disclosure as to source of performance data, including fact that results from 2016 through 2022 are the result of applying the model retroactively to that time period.
new_option: Davis violated Standard III(D) by failing to clearly identify simulated performance results.

original_option: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm; changing methodology solely to make performance look better without proper disclosure is misleading; complete information is provided when clients have sufficient information to judge performance.
new_option: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm.

original_option: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies; deviating from firm's stated policy to opportunistically capture favorable performance is a violation.
new_option: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies.

original_option: trustees gave Connor internal reports so she could advise them on how to manage their endowment funds; because information in reports is clearly both confidential AND within scope of the confidential relationship, Standard III(E) prohibits Connor from divulging the information to Kasey.
new_option: trustees gave Connor internal reports so she could advise them on how to manage their endowment funds.

original_option: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client; well-intentioned motives do not excuse confidentiality breach.
new_option: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client.

original_option: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators; Samuel may well have a duty to provide pension fund records her firm possesses to the government.
new_option: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators.

original_option: Moody has taken reasonable steps to protect confidentiality of client information while using social media platform; provided instructions clarifying that all information posted on the site would be publicly viewable to all group members and warned against using this method for communicating confidential information; accidental disclosure of confidential information by a client is not under Moody's control; her actions to remove the information promptly once she became aware further align with Standard III(E).
